title: Enhancing Spin Coherence of Optically-Addressed Molecular Qubit by Nuclear Spin Hyperpolarization
abstract: Optically addressable molecular triplet spins provide a chemically tunable platform for quantum application, but their coherence is often limited by interactions with surrounding spin baths. Here we demonstrate controlled suppression of nuclear-bath-induced decoherence in photoexcited triplet spins of pentacene co-crystallized in high-purity naphthalene single crystals. By hyperpolarizing the proton spin bath through triplet dynamic nuclear polarization (triplet-DNP), magnetic noise generated by the nuclear spins is suppressed, leading to an extension of the electron spin transverse coherence time. Experimentally, we observe a 25\% enhancement of the spin-echo decay time with $60\%$ polarization of the proton spin bath. The measured scaling of the spin-echo decay time ($T_2$) with nuclear polarization quantitatively follows the predicted dependence derived from the polarization-controlled nuclear second moment. Both the enhancement and the absolute value of the coherence time are quantitatively reproduced by cluster correlation expansion (CCE) simulations. These results establish nuclear spin hyperpolarization as a general and actively tunable approach to engineering coherence in molecular qubits. This work provides a broadly applicable design framework for high-coherence molecular and solid-state spin systems.
one_liner: Triplet-DNP hyperpolarizes the proton bath around a molecular qubit, extending optically addressed spin coherence in pentacene:naphthalene crystals.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates an active materials-level knob for reducing nuclear-spin noise in molecular qubits, a key limit for scalable spin-based quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The work studies photoexcited triplet spins of pentacene co-crystallized in high-purity naphthalene single crystals, where electron-spin coherence is limited by surrounding proton-spin noise. By hyperpolarizing the proton bath via triplet dynamic nuclear polarization, the authors suppress the nuclear second moment and observe a 25% increase in spin-echo decay time at about 60% proton polarization. The measured T2 scaling with bath polarization matches analytical predictions and is quantitatively reproduced by cluster correlation expansion simulations. This gives a concrete coherence-engineering route for chemically tunable molecular spin qubits.
platform_or_system: Pentacene triplet spins in naphthalene single crystals
main_result: Hyperpolarizing the proton spin bath to roughly 60% yields a measured 25% enhancement of the electron spin-echo coherence time, consistent with theory and CCE simulation.
application_angle: Actively reducing spin-bath noise could improve molecular-qubit memory times and make optically addressable molecular spins more viable for quantum hardware.

title: High PDMR contrast in single NV centres and related photocurrent properties
abstract: This paper aims to extend the understanding of the mechanism of photo-electrical detection of magnetic resonance (PDMR) in nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centres. This technique is particularly important for development of solid-state quantum computing platforms. In particular, we report on the new insight in the photocurrent (PC) generation and charge cycling in the single NV centre, which is related to PDMR contrast reaching 50\% and above. We develop a technique to locate PC related features. We find that electrons generated at the NV centre are stored in interface trap levels and establish that the interface states serve as an amplifier that can be driven by introducing a second laser into our confocal setup. We show that controlling these interface states allows one to significantly enhance the PDMR contrast. We develop a model that consistently explains observed amplification effects even without the application of a bias voltage.
one_liner: Single NV centres show PDMR contrast above 50% by exploiting interface-trap-mediated photocurrent amplification and charge-state control.
why_it_matters: High-contrast electrical spin readout in single NVs can reduce reliance on photon collection and strengthen prospects for compact diamond quantum sensors and chip-scale devices.
detailed_summary: This paper analyzes photo-electrical detection of magnetic resonance in single nitrogen-vacancy centres, focusing on the microscopic origin of the photocurrent and charge cycling. The authors identify interface trap levels that store electrons generated at the NV and act as an amplifier, and they develop a method to locate the photocurrent-related features spatially. Adding a second laser allows controlled driving of these interface states, strongly boosting the PDMR contrast to 50% and above even without external bias. A consistent model is presented for the amplification mechanism and observed charge dynamics.
main_result: Control of interface-trap-assisted charge dynamics enables single-NV PDMR contrast exceeding 50% and explains the associated photocurrent amplification behavior.
application_angle: Improved electrical readout of NV spin resonance is directly relevant for integrated quantum sensing and potentially for scalable diamond-based quantum device architectures.

title: Contextuality of quantum non-demolition measurement via state discrimination
abstract: Quantum non-demolition measurements facilitate various quantum technologies, including quantum communication. Notably, their operational structure can be replicated by a classical model--referred to as a noncontextual model--making it crucial to identify which features prevents such models from reproducing the corresponding quantum measurements. In this work, we theoretically demonstrate contextual features inherent in the structure of quantum non-demolition measurements. These features not only reveal the nonclassicality of unambiguous state discrimination, but also extend to sequential unambiguous discrimination and probabilistic quantum cloning, both of which involve post-measurement states. Moreover, our analysis extends to noisy scenarios, highlighting its potential relevance for practical implementations. We believe that our results broaden the scope of observing nonclassicality in quantum systems and ultimately contribute to the advancement of various quantum technologies.
one_liner: A theoretical contextuality test links quantum non-demolition measurements to nonclassical advantages in state discrimination, cloning, and noisy sequential protocols.
why_it_matters: It identifies a rigorous nonclassical resource behind QND-based communication primitives, helping separate genuinely quantum performance from classical mimicry.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a theoretical framework showing that quantum non-demolition measurement structures exhibit contextuality when analyzed through state-discrimination tasks. This connects the nonclassicality of unambiguous state discrimination to sequential discrimination and probabilistic cloning, where post-measurement states matter operationally. The analysis is extended to noisy settings, indicating that the signatures are not restricted to idealized models. The result clarifies which aspects of QND-based protocols cannot be reproduced by noncontextual classical models.
main_result: The authors prove that QND measurement scenarios can display contextuality in state-discrimination-based tasks, including sequential and noisy variants relevant to communication primitives.
application_angle: The framework can guide certification of genuinely quantum behavior in QND-enabled communication and information-processing schemes under realistic imperfections.

title: Magnetic doping-induced second-order and first-order topological phase transition inthe photonic alloy
abstract: The bulk-edge correspondence principle, a cornerstone of topological physics, ensures that first-order topological systems host robust chiral edge states in two dimension. This was later extended to higher-order phases, where second-order topological insulators exhibit localized, topologically protected corner states. While the transition between these distinct phases has been demonstrated in periodic systems, its existence in disordered platforms remains an open question. Here, we demonstrate a controllable topological phase transition between a second-order topological phase and a first-order topological phase in a two-dimensional photonic alloy. By tuning the magnetic doping concentration - implemented by attaching permanent magnets randomly to nonmagnetized yttrium iron garnet rods in an alternately magnetized honeycomb lattice with C3 rotational symmetry - we flexibly control the system's topology. At zero doping, we observe higher-order corner states, confirmed by a trivial Chern number and non-zero bulk polarizations of 1/3. As doping concentration increases, these corner states progressively merge with the bulk states, culminating in the closure of the bulk transmission gap. After the bulk transmission gap reopens with further increased doping, the system transitions to a first-order topological phase, characterized by a nontrivial Chern number of -1 and the emergence of a chiral edge state. This transition is reversible, providing a highly tunable and experimentally simple platform for flexibly switching between localized corner states and delocalized chiral edge states within a single photonic system.
one_liner: A disordered photonic alloy switches reversibly between higher-order corner states and first-order chiral edge states via magnetic doping.
why_it_matters: It shows topological phase control in a realistically disordered photonic medium, expanding reconfigurable protected transport beyond ideal periodic lattices.
detailed_summary: The authors realize a two-dimensional photonic alloy based on an alternately magnetized honeycomb lattice of yttrium iron garnet rods with random magnetic doping implemented by attached permanent magnets. At zero doping, the system supports second-order topology with corner states, trivial Chern number, and nonzero bulk polarizations of 1/3. As doping increases, the transmission gap closes and reopens, and the platform enters a first-order topological phase with Chern number -1 and a chiral edge state. The transition is reversible, providing a simple experimental knob for moving between localized and propagating topological modes in disorder.
main_result: Magnetic doping concentration drives a reversible disorder-tolerant transition from a second-order corner-state phase to a first-order Chern phase with chiral edge transport.
application_angle: A tunable switch between corner confinement and edge routing could support reconfigurable photonic devices for robust signal localization and transport.

title: Resonance fluorescence of an artificial atom with a time-delayed coherent feedback
abstract: The model of light-matter interaction in quantum electrodynamics typically relies on the Markovian approximation, which assumes that the system's future evolution depends solely on its current state, effectively treating it as a ``memoryless" process. However, this approximation is not valid in scenarios when retardation effects are significant. These memory and retardation effects have the potential to improve existing quantum technologies (e.g., large-scale quantum networks, quantum information processing) and unlock new phenomena for future applications. In this work, we show theory and experiments of a time-delayed coherent feedback system using a transmon artificial atom (treated as a qubit) embedded in a superconducting circuit waveguide, in both linear and nonlinear excitation regimes. By using a feedback loop with a delay time comparable to the qubit relaxation time, pronounced non-Markovian effects appear in the dynamics of the qubit evolution. We also show how the resonance fluorescence spectrum, including elastic and inelastic scattering (such as the well-known Mollow triplet), can be significantly modified through the interaction between the qubit and feedback loop to show genuine non-Markovian and quantum nonlinear phenomena that cannot be explained with instantaneous coupling parameters. This work presents the first experimental report of Mollow triplets in the non-Markovian regime.
one_liner: A transmon with delayed coherent feedback shows non-Markovian resonance fluorescence and the first experimental Mollow triplet in this regime.
why_it_matters: Engineered time delay adds a practical non-Markovian control resource for superconducting quantum hardware and network nodes beyond standard memoryless models.
detailed_summary: This work combines theory and experiment for a superconducting-circuit waveguide containing a transmon artificial atom coupled to a coherent feedback loop with delay comparable to the qubit relaxation time. In this regime, the qubit dynamics and resonance fluorescence depart strongly from Markovian behavior in both linear and nonlinear driving conditions. The feedback reshapes elastic and inelastic scattering, including the Mollow-triplet structure, in ways that cannot be captured by instantaneous coupling models. The study reports the first experimental observation of Mollow triplets in a genuinely non-Markovian setting.
platform_or_system: Transmon qubit in a superconducting waveguide with delayed feedback
main_result: A time-delayed coherent feedback loop produces experimentally observed non-Markovian modifications of resonance fluorescence, including the first Mollow triplet measured in this regime.
application_angle: Delay-engineered light-matter interfaces could be used to control microwave photons, memory effects, and node dynamics in superconducting processors and quantum networks.

title: Entanglement generation of arbitrary squeezed Fock states
abstract: We propose an efficient and robust protocol for the generation of entanglement between a superconducting qubit and a squeezed cavity. By applying a parametric drive to the cavity coupled to the qubit, the dynamical evolution of the system is precisely described by an anisotropic Rabi model within a squeezed reference frame. Utilizing high-order time-averaging methods, we analytically derive the resonance conditions and the effective Rabi frequency for the high-order three-photon process. By implementing an adiabatic passage, slowly tuning the cavity frequency across the resonance, the system is steered into a maximally entangled state, e.g., between the three-photon state $\ket{g,3}$ and the qubit excited state $\ket{e,0}$ in the squeezed picture. Numerical simulation results confirm the high fidelity and robustness of the proposed protocol. Our method provides a practical pathway for generating complex non-Gaussian entangled states, which are of significant value for fault-tolerant quantum computation and quantum metrology beyond the standard quantum limit.
one_liner: An adiabatic three-photon protocol generates high-fidelity entanglement between a superconducting qubit and squeezed cavity Fock states.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical route to non-Gaussian entangled resources useful for quantum metrology and bosonic-encoded quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The proposal uses a parametrically driven cavity coupled to a superconducting qubit, where the dynamics map to an anisotropic Rabi model in a squeezed frame. Using high-order time-averaging, the authors derive resonance conditions and effective Rabi frequencies for a high-order three-photon transition, then sweep the cavity frequency adiabatically across resonance. This prepares maximally entangled states such as a superposition of the squeezed-picture states |g,3> and |e,0>. Numerical simulations indicate high fidelity and robustness against imperfections, supporting feasibility in circuit-QED hardware.
main_result: The scheme deterministically prepares entanglement between a qubit and arbitrary squeezed Fock states through an adiabatic high-order multiphoton process with high simulated fidelity.
application_angle: Such controllable non-Gaussian entanglement is relevant for bosonic quantum processors and for sensing protocols that aim to beat standard quantum limits.

title: Device independent quantum key distribution with robust self-tests
abstract: Device-independent quantum key distribution (DIQKD) provides a model of quantum key distribution with minimal assumptions and highly abstract theoretical building blocks. Although DIQKD frees us from detailed discussions of specific device models and associated error parameters, it replaces them with fundamental assumptions about the validity of quantum experiments. In this work, we propose a way to lift a protocol based on DIQKD-style assumptions to a device-dependent QKD protocol by performing local self-tests in the laboratories of the two key-generating parties. In particular, we consider routed Bell-test setups as a means of self-testing the local parties in earnest and develop a rigorous mathematical framework showing that the underlying optimization problems can indeed be transferred to the device-dependent QKD setting. As an application, we illustrate many of the relevant techniques through the case study of a routed BB84 protocol.
one_liner: A DIQKD framework uses local self-tests in routed Bell setups to translate device-independent security tools into device-dependent QKD protocols.
why_it_matters: It narrows the gap between ideal DIQKD theory and deployable QKD by converting strong self-testing guarantees into practical security analysis.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a rigorous framework in which local self-tests performed by Alice and Bob in routed Bell-test configurations certify the components needed for QKD security analysis. Rather than relying purely on device-independent abstractions, it maps the relevant optimization problems into a device-dependent setting while preserving robust certification logic. A routed BB84 case study is used to show how the method can be instantiated and analyzed concretely.
main_result: It shows that robust local self-tests can lift DIQKD-style assumptions into a device-dependent QKD protocol framework, with routed BB84 as an explicit example.
application_angle: This could make high-assurance QKD more implementable by using experimentally accessible self-tests instead of fully idealized device-independent assumptions.

title: Topological Valley-Reshaped Device: Bifunctional Waveguiding and Single-Beam Leaky-Wave Radiation for Terahertz Communication
abstract: Topological photonics has emerged as a powerful platform for terahertz on-chip systems due to its robust waveguiding capabilities. However, directly extracting topological valley-locked edge states into directional free-space radiation without auxiliary couplers while preserving guided-wave functionality remains a fundamental challenge. In this work, we propose and experimentally demonstrate a bifunctional topological valley-reshaped device. By introducing an angular truncation and a spatial displacement to a complete topological waveguide (TW), the resulting structure inherently retains its waveguiding capabilities. Furthermore, when operated as an isolated section, it functions as a topological leaky-wave antenna (TLWA) that exhibits directional single-lobe radiation. The TW shows low-loss guided-wave performance with an 18 GHz operating bandwidth, supporting error-free transmission up to 60 Gbps. For the TLWA, by gradually reducing the number of protective lattices that are orthogonal to the propagation direction, the valley-locked edge state becomes momentum-matched to the free-space light line, generating leaky-wave radiation. Simultaneously, reshaping of the opposite valley-locked edge state suppresses far-field side lobes and reduces reflection, yielding a clean single-beam radiation pattern with a side-lobe suppression ratio (SLSR) exceeding 15 dB. The TLWA realizes a measured peak gain of 12.5 dBi and a 19 GHz operating bandwidth. Notably, the low-dispersion property of the K-valley radiation allows the main-lobe direction to vary by only 2 degrees across the entire operating band, enabling error-free free-space reception at 24 Gbps. This bifunctional design represents a key step toward highly integrated and modular terahertz on-chip systems.
one_liner: A topological terahertz device combines low-loss valley waveguiding with single-beam leaky-wave radiation for guided and free-space links.
why_it_matters: It integrates robust on-chip transport and directional wireless emission in one topological structure, which is valuable for modular terahertz communication hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally realize a bifunctional valley-topological structure that acts either as a complete waveguide or, when isolated, as a topological leaky-wave antenna. The waveguide delivers low-loss transmission over an 18 GHz bandwidth and supports error-free data transport up to 60 Gbps. By reducing protective lattices and reshaping the opposite valley mode, the device momentum-matches one edge state to free space, producing a single-lobe beam with more than 15 dB side-lobe suppression, 12.5 dBi peak gain, and 24 Gbps error-free free-space reception over a 19 GHz band.
main_result: It demonstrates a single structure that provides robust topological terahertz waveguiding and directional low-dispersion leaky-wave radiation with high data-rate links.
application_angle: The design supports compact on-chip to free-space interconnects for terahertz communication modules and integrated photonic wireless systems.

title: Quantum engineering with ultracold polar molecules using trap-induced resonances
abstract: Polar molecules represent a promising platform for quantum simulation and computation protocols. Highly controllable arrays of optical tweezers are now accessible in experiments, allowing for unprecedented control of individual molecules. Motional dephasing is typically seen as an obstacle in quantum computing scenarios. Here, we instead consider using the trap structure as a resource for implementing efficient quantum gates. By numerically solving the two-body problem of dipoles trapped in separate tweezers, we identify trap-induced resonances that can serve as the mechanism for achieving state-dependent dynamics and can be further utilized for quantum sensing.
one_liner: Trap-induced resonances between tweezer-trapped polar molecules are proposed as a controllable resource for gates and quantum sensing.
why_it_matters: It turns motional structure from a decoherence source into a tunable interaction mechanism for molecule-based quantum devices.
detailed_summary: By numerically solving the two-body problem for dipolar molecules confined in separate optical tweezers, the work identifies trap-induced resonances that strongly modify state-dependent dynamics. These resonances arise from the interplay of dipolar interactions and confinement and can be tuned through the trap geometry. The analysis suggests that the same mechanism can be used both to engineer efficient entangling operations and to enhance sensitivity to external perturbations.
platform_or_system: ultracold polar molecules in optical tweezers
main_result: It identifies tunable trap-induced resonances that enable state-dependent control of tweezer-trapped polar molecules for gate and sensing protocols.
application_angle: The approach offers a practical path to molecule-based quantum processors and sensors that exploit confinement engineering rather than fighting it.

title: Genuine and Non-Genuine Quantum Non-Markovianity: A Unified Information-Theoretic Review
abstract: Understanding whether the features of open quantum dynamics are genuinely quantum remains a central challenge in quantum dynamics. Even though the non-Markovian behavior of quantum dynamics has been widely investigated across different settings, there is still no consensus on which properties of a dynamics reflect genuine quantum features and which arise from classical or non-genuine quantum sources. In this review, we provide detailed information on recent developments in characterizing quantum non-Markovianity based on information backflow and the nature of its origin. We also present a survey on how various approaches separate classical and quantum contributions, as well as how they define operational tasks that reveal genuine quantum non-Markovianity. We analyze several frameworks, including state-distinguishability -based, channel-based (``CP-divisibility''), and process-tensor methods. For each framework, we outline the underlying physical motivation, the criteria proposed to distinguish genuine quantum non-Markovianity from practical or apparent memory effects. We further compare different approaches and their strengths and limitations. The review aims to clarify the conceptual and operational aspects of quantum non-Markovian processes based on their nature and to provide a foundation for future research on quantum non-Markovianity and its role in advancing quantum information science and technology.
one_liner: This review unifies information-theoretic approaches for separating genuine quantum non-Markovian memory effects from classical or apparent ones.
why_it_matters: Clarifying which memory effects are genuinely quantum is important for designing reliable control, metrology, and noise-aware quantum technologies.
detailed_summary: The paper surveys state-distinguishability, channel-divisibility, and process-tensor frameworks for characterizing non-Markovian dynamics and distinguishing genuinely quantum memory from classical or effective memory effects. It compares how each framework defines information backflow, operational witnesses, and the role of system-environment correlations. The review also highlights strengths and limitations of existing criteria in realistic noisy settings relevant to quantum information tasks.
main_result: It provides a unified information-theoretic synthesis of criteria and operational tasks for identifying genuine versus non-genuine quantum non-Markovianity.
application_angle: The framework can guide noise diagnostics and protocol design in sensing and other quantum technologies where memory effects affect performance.

title: Local robust shadows on a trapped ion computer -- a case study
abstract: We experimentally demonstrate local robust shadows on a trapped-ion device, a protocol developed to counteract measurement errors. We alternate between a calibration stage and the shadow estimation stage and also introduce Pauli-X-twirling before measurements in both stages to symmetrize error rates. We then demonstrate the protocol on a trapped-ion quantum computer with artificially shortened measurement pulse duration. This yields faster experiments at the cost of increased error rates which are subsequently mitigated by the robust shadow protocol. We benchmark this approach on three exemplary quantum states: a local Haar random state, as well as standard and Pauli-correlation encoded QAOA states. In all three cases, the local robust shadow protocol succeeds at mitigating the increased error rates hailing from shorter measurement pulse durations.
one_liner: A trapped-ion experiment shows local robust shadows can offset higher readout noise and preserve state-estimation quality under faster measurements.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates a concrete mitigation tool that trades slower, cleaner readout for faster experiments without losing useful classical-shadow estimates.
detailed_summary: The experiment implements local robust shadows on a trapped-ion processor by alternating calibration and estimation stages and adding Pauli-X twirling to symmetrize measurement errors. The device is intentionally operated with shortened measurement pulses, increasing readout noise while reducing experiment time. Across local Haar-random states and two QAOA state encodings, the protocol mitigates the induced errors and recovers more reliable shadow estimates.
main_result: It experimentally validates that local robust shadows with calibration and Pauli-X twirling can compensate elevated measurement errors caused by shortened readout pulses.
application_angle: This supports faster trapped-ion characterization workflows and could improve throughput for near-term algorithms and benchmarking on hardware with imperfect readout.

title: Compact Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution System Employing Monolithically Integrated Silicon Photonic Transceiver
abstract: We demonstrate the first CV-QKD system featuring a custom-designed monolithic silicon photonic dual-polarisation transceiver. Leveraging PS-64-QAM, we achieved 1.9 Mbit/s secret key rate across 25 km of standard single-mode fibre, highlighting the potential of electronic-photonic integration for practical QKD.
one_liner: A monolithically integrated silicon photonic transceiver enables compact CV-QKD with megabit-per-second secret keys over metro-scale fiber.
why_it_matters: It is a strong step toward deployable QKD by shrinking the optics into a silicon photonic transceiver while maintaining useful key rates.
detailed_summary: The work reports the first continuous-variable QKD system built around a custom monolithic silicon photonic dual-polarization transceiver. Using probabilistically shaped 64-QAM modulation, the system achieves a 1.9 Mbit/s secret key rate over 25 km of standard single-mode fiber. The result highlights the compatibility of advanced coherent modulation formats with highly integrated electronic-photonic QKD hardware.
main_result: It demonstrates 1.9 Mbit/s CV-QKD secret key generation over 25 km using a monolithically integrated silicon photonic dual-polarization transceiver.
application_angle: Integrated transceivers reduce size, cost, and packaging complexity, making metro-access quantum-secure links more realistic for commercial deployment.

title: Simulating cavity QED with spin-orbit coupled Bose-Einstein condensates revisited
abstract: Simulating cavity quantum electrodynamics in synthetic platforms offers a promising route to exploring light-matter interactions without real photons, while enabling the transfer of cavity-based techniques to other systems. Among such platforms, Bose-Einstein condensates with synthetic spin-orbit coupling provide a controllable setting where internal and motional degrees of freedom become coupled, mimicking aspects of cavity quantum electrodynamics. In this work, we critically assess the extent to which spin-orbit coupled Bose-Einstein condensates can emulate cavity quantum electrodynamics phenomena, with a focus on squeezing and entanglement generation. We show that spin-orbit coupled Bose-Einstein condensates can faithfully reproduce the physics of a single atom coupled to a quantized field, realizing an analogue of the quantum Rabi model but inherently fail to capture genuine collective effects characteristic of the Dicke model, such as cavity-mediated many-body entanglement. Our results clarify both the potential and the fundamental limitations of spin-orbit coupled Bose-Einstein condensates as analogue quantum simulators of cavity quantum electrodynamics, offering guidance for future strategies to generate and control non-classical states of matter in photon-free, highly tunable platforms.
one_liner: A spin-orbit-coupled Bose-Einstein condensate reproduces single-atom cavity-QED physics but not genuine Dicke-model collective entanglement.
why_it_matters: It sharpens where photon-free atomic simulators can replace cavity QED and where real cavity-mediated many-body effects remain indispensable.
detailed_summary: The paper re-examines synthetic cavity-QED simulation using spin-orbit-coupled Bose-Einstein condensates, where internal spin and motional modes emulate light-matter coupling. The authors show this platform faithfully captures single-emitter cavity physics equivalent to the quantum Rabi model, including squeezing-related features. They also show a fundamental failure to reproduce collective Dicke-model phenomena because the analogue lacks true cavity-mediated all-to-all interactions needed for many-body entanglement generation. The result is a clear separation between what cavity-QED observables are transferable to SOC-BEC simulators and what remains specific to real photonic cavities.
main_result: The work demonstrates accurate emulation of single-atom cavity-QED behavior in SOC-BECs while proving that collective cavity-induced entanglement and Dicke physics cannot be faithfully reproduced.
application_angle: This provides a practical boundary for using ultracold-atom analog simulators to prototype nonclassical light-matter dynamics before moving to true cavity-photonic hardware.

title: Learning unified control of internal spin squeezing in atomic qudits for magnetometry
abstract: Generating and preserving metrologically useful quantum states is a central challenge in quantum-enhanced atomic magnetometry. In multilevel atoms operated in the low-field regime, the nonlinear Zeeman (NLZ) effect is both a resource and a limitation. It nonlinearly redistributes internal spin fluctuations to generate spin-squeezed states within a single atomic qudit, yet under fixed readout it distorts the measurement-relevant quadrature and limits the accessible metrological gain. This challenge is compounded by the time dependence of both the squeezing axis and the effective nonlinear action. Here we show that physics-informed reinforcement learning can transform NLZ dynamics from a source of readout degradation into a sustained metrological resource. Using only experimentally accessible low-order spin moments, a trained agent identifies, in the $f=21/2$ manifold of $^{161}\mathrm{Dy}$, a unified control policy that rapidly prepares strongly squeezed internal states and stabilizes more than $4\,\mathrm{dB}$ of fixed-axis spin squeezing under always-on NLZ evolution. Including state-preparation overhead, the learned protocol yields a single-atom magnetic sensitivity of $13.9\,\mathrm{pT}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$, corresponding to an advantage of approximately $3\,\mathrm{dB}$ beyond the standard quantum limit. Our results establish learning-based control as a practical route for converting unavoidable intrinsic nonlinear dynamics in multilevel quantum sensors into operational metrological advantage.
one_liner: Reinforcement learning finds a unified control policy that prepares and stabilizes internal spin squeezing in dysprosium qudits for magnetometry.
why_it_matters: It turns unavoidable nonlinear Zeeman dynamics into a sustained metrological resource, improving magnetic sensitivity beyond the standard quantum limit.
detailed_summary: The authors use physics-informed reinforcement learning to control always-on nonlinear Zeeman evolution in the f=21/2 manifold of 161Dy. Using only experimentally accessible low-order spin moments, the learned policy both rapidly generates internal spin-squeezed states and keeps more than 4 dB of fixed-axis squeezing stable despite time-dependent squeezing axes. Including state-preparation overhead, they report a single-atom magnetic sensitivity of 13.9 pT/sqrt(Hz), corresponding to about 3 dB advantage beyond the standard quantum limit. The key technical advance is a unified feedback-free control strategy that co-optimizes state preparation and readout compatibility in a multilevel atomic sensor.
main_result: A learned control protocol achieves sustained >4 dB fixed-axis spin squeezing and 13.9 pT/sqrt(Hz) single-atom sensitivity in a dysprosium qudit sensor.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to deployable quantum magnetometers because it exploits intrinsic multilevel atomic dynamics rather than requiring idealized Hamiltonians or fragile entangled ensembles.

title: Phase statistics of a single qubit emission as a direct probe of its coherence
abstract: The emission of photon from an individual atom encodes the phase of its initialized quantum state. Using single-shot heterodyne detection, we measure the phase distribution of the emission from a superconducting transmon qubit in an open waveguide configuration and track its evolution over time. We demonstrate that the presence of a quantum superposition is encoded in the phase statistics of the emission and remains resolvable despite a high noise level. These phase statistics serve as a quantitative probe of the qubit coherence. The decay of the emission envelope with increasing integration time reveals the energy relaxation rate of the emitted wavepacket, while phase distribution broadening tracks pure dephasing processes. We thereby establish a direct link between the decoherence dynamics of an open quantum system and the statistical properties of its radiated field.
one_liner: Single-shot heterodyne phase statistics of photons emitted by a transmon directly reveal the qubit's coherence and dephasing dynamics.
why_it_matters: It offers a field-based, experimentally accessible diagnostic for open-system qubit coherence without relying only on conventional state tomography.
detailed_summary: This work measures the phase distribution of radiation emitted by a superconducting transmon coupled to an open waveguide using single-shot heterodyne detection. The authors show that a prepared qubit superposition is encoded in the emitted-field phase statistics and remains identifiable even under substantial noise. They link decay of the integrated emission envelope to energy relaxation of the emitted wavepacket and connect phase broadening to pure dephasing. The experiment therefore establishes a quantitative mapping from radiated microwave field statistics to the decoherence channels of the emitting qubit.
platform_or_system: superconducting transmon in an open waveguide
main_result: The experiment shows that emission phase statistics directly quantify both energy relaxation and pure dephasing of a single superconducting qubit.
application_angle: This gives superconducting-hardware teams a new inline diagnostic tool for monitoring qubit coherence through emitted microwave fields in waveguide-QED architectures.

title: $1/N^2$ Precision Interferometry with Collectively Enhanced Atomic Mirror
abstract: Quantum metrology exploits quantum resources to enhance measurement precision beyond the classical limit. Conventional protocols normally rely on the preparation of delicate quantum states to acquire these resources, posing a major challenge for scaling and robustness. Here we introduce a paradigm that circumvents this requirement with a collectively enhanced quantum mirror (CEAM), i.e., a mesoscopic array of $N$ atoms coupled to a semi-infinite waveguide. When injecting single photons into the waveguide and estimating the CEAM-boundary distance from the reflection phase, a $1/N^2$ precision scaling can be obtained, which surpasses the Heisenberg limit. In this protocol, the quantum resource stems from the cooperative optical response, requiring no entangled state preparation. Our scheme is robust against positional and coupling disorder, offering a practical route to ultra-sensitive quantum metrology in integrated photonic systems.
one_liner: A collectively enhanced atomic mirror in a semi-infinite waveguide enables single-photon interferometry with distance precision scaling as 1/N^2.
why_it_matters: It proposes a robust metrology route that beats standard scaling without requiring fragile entangled-state preparation.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a collectively enhanced quantum mirror formed by N atoms coupled to a semi-infinite waveguide and uses single-photon reflection phase to estimate the mirror-boundary distance. Cooperative optical response supplies the metrological resource, yielding a claimed 1/N^2 precision scaling without preparing entangled probe states. The authors analyze robustness to atomic position disorder and coupling disorder, finding the enhancement survives realistic imperfections. Technically, the proposal leverages waveguide-mediated collective scattering as an interferometric amplifier in an integrated photonic geometry.
platform_or_system: mesoscopic atomic array in a semi-infinite waveguide
main_result: The scheme achieves distance-estimation precision scaling as 1/N^2 from single-photon reflection off a collectively enhanced atomic mirror.
application_angle: It points to chip-scale ultra-sensitive interferometric sensors that use engineered collective light scattering instead of difficult many-body state preparation.

title: Emergent-Coupling-Based Ansatz Evaluated on a Superconducting Quantum Processor
abstract: The performance of the variational quantum eigensolver depends critically on the choice of ansatz. In this work, we experimentally evaluate the emergent-coupling-based ansatz (ECBA), a physically motivated variational ansatz for disordered systems. The ECBA is based on a renormalization (semi-)group approach to determine the dominant effective couplings, resulting in shallow circuits that capture the essential long-range entanglement structure while balancing local correlations. We implement the ECBA on superconducting quantum processors and benchmark it on disordered Heisenberg chain models. Using classically pre-optimized parameters and error mitigation techniques, we study systems of up to 30 qubits and observe an experimental relative energy accuracy of 96.47% for the largest system. Furthermore, we find that the ECBA can be efficiently embedded on hardware with two-dimensional square-lattice connectivity. We compare to commonly used hardware efficient ansätze and observe that the ECBA achieves significantly higher accuracy at a similar gate count.
one_liner: A renormalization-inspired variational ansatz is implemented on superconducting processors and reaches high accuracy on disordered Heisenberg chains up to 30 qubits.
why_it_matters: It shows that physics-motivated shallow circuits can outperform hardware-efficient ansätze at comparable cost on real superconducting devices.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally evaluate the emergent-coupling-based ansatz, which uses dominant effective couplings from a renormalization-style procedure to build shallow variational circuits for disordered systems. Implemented on superconducting quantum processors with classically pre-optimized parameters and error mitigation, the method is benchmarked on disordered Heisenberg chains up to 30 qubits. For the largest instance they report 96.47% relative energy accuracy and show the ansatz maps efficiently onto two-dimensional square-lattice connectivity. Compared with standard hardware-efficient ansätze, ECBA achieves substantially better accuracy at similar gate depth by targeting the problem's entanglement structure.
main_result: ECBA achieves 96.47% relative energy accuracy on a 30-qubit disordered Heisenberg problem while outperforming hardware-efficient ansätze at similar gate count.
application_angle: This is practically relevant for near-term quantum simulation because it reduces circuit overhead and improves solution quality on connectivity-constrained superconducting hardware.

title: Open-System Adiabatic Quantum Search under Dephasing
abstract: Adiabatic quantum algorithms must evolve slowly enough to suppress non-adiabatic transitions while remaining fast enough to be practical. In open systems, this trade-off is reshaped by decoherence. For Hamiltonians subject to dephasing Lindbladians, Avron et al. [1] showed that a unique timetable exists that maximizes the fidelity with a target state. This optimal schedule is characterized by a constant tunneling rate along the adiabatic path. In this work, we revisit their analysis and apply it to the adiabatic Grover search framework, obtaining closed-form expressions for the optimal evolution schedule, the minimum runtime, and the resulting achievable fidelity. Moreover, by invoking an energy-time uncertainty argument, we identify a critical dephasing threshold, beyond which further noise-assisted acceleration is prohibited, thereby defining the physically realizable boundaries for dephasing-based adiabatic quantum search protocols.
one_liner: Closed-form optimal schedules are derived for adiabatic Grover search under dephasing, including runtime, fidelity, and a critical noise threshold.
why_it_matters: It defines the physically realizable regime where dephasing can assist adiabatic search and where further acceleration becomes impossible.
detailed_summary: Building on prior results for dephasing Lindbladians, the paper revisits open-system adiabatic control in the context of Grover search. The authors derive closed-form expressions for the fidelity-maximizing schedule, minimum runtime, and achievable fidelity, with the optimal path characterized by constant tunneling rate along the adiabatic evolution. Using an energy-time uncertainty argument, they further identify a critical dephasing threshold beyond which noise-assisted speedup is no longer possible. The analysis maps out the operational boundary between useful decoherence engineering and irreducible open-system limitations.
main_result: The study derives exact optimal dephasing-aware adiabatic search schedules and identifies a critical dephasing level that bounds achievable acceleration.
application_angle: These results are useful for designing noisy analog or photonic adiabatic-search protocols where controlled dephasing may be present but cannot be treated as uniformly beneficial.

title: Probing excited-state quantum phase transitions with trapped cold ions
abstract: We propose concrete protocols to realize quantum criticality due to excited-state quantum phase transitions (ESQPTs) experimentally in presumably the simplest and most resilient system involving a single trapped ion oscillating in a radio-frequency Paul trap. We identify a specific class of excited states of the Extended Rabi Model (ERM) Hamiltonian, which occur between two critical ESQPT energies of the model in its (anti)Jaynes-Cummings superradiant phase. Properties of these states motivate the definition of several ESQPT witness observables. We study their critical scaling behaviors as well as various distinct state evolutions by driving the system across the quantum criticalities by changing the qubit-phonon coupling strength linearly in time at different finite rates. A mapping of the theoretical control parameters of the ERM to the experimental parameters of a trapped ion setup is provided, and simulations are performed for values referencing existing state-of-the-art setups, addressing both unitary state evolutions as well as relevant open-system corrections.
one_liner: A single trapped-ion protocol maps the Extended Rabi Model and exposes excited-state quantum phase transitions through measurable dynamical witnesses.
why_it_matters: It turns ESQPT physics into a realistic trapped-ion experiment, linking many-body critical phenomena to controllable qubit-phonon hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes an implementation of the Extended Rabi Model in a single ion confined in a radio-frequency Paul trap, targeting excited states located between two ESQPT critical energies in the (anti)Jaynes-Cummings superradiant regime. It defines experimentally accessible ESQPT witness observables and studies their critical scaling. The authors also simulate linear ramps of the qubit-phonon coupling across the critical points at finite rates, including both unitary dynamics and open-system corrections using parameters compatible with current trapped-ion setups.
platform_or_system: single trapped ion in a Paul trap
main_result: It shows that ESQPT signatures, critical scaling, and driven crossing dynamics can be observed in a realistic single-ion qubit-phonon platform.
application_angle: The work provides a concrete route to use trapped-ion hardware as a controllable analog simulator for non-equilibrium quantum critical phenomena.

title: SesQ: A Surface Electrostatic Simulator for Precise Energy Participation Ratio Simulation in Superconducting Qubits
abstract: An accurate and efficient numerical electromagnetic model for superconducting qubits is essential for characterizing and minimizing design-dependent dielectric losses. The energy participation ratio (EPR) is the commonly adopted metric used to evaluate these losses, but its calculation presents a severe multiscale computational challenge. Conventional finite element method (FEM) requires 3D volumetric meshing, leading to prohibitive computational costs and memory requirements when attempting to capture singular electric fields at nanometer-thin material interfaces. To address this bottleneck, we propose SesQ, a surface integral equation simulator tailored for the precise simulation of the EPR. By applying discretization on 2D surfaces, deriving a semi-analytical multilayer Green's function, and employing a dedicated non-conformal boundary mesh refinement scheme, SesQ accurately resolves singular edge fields without an explosive growth in the number of unknowns. Validations with analytically solvable models demonstrate that SesQ accelerates capacitance extraction by roughly two orders of magnitude compared to commercial FEM tools. While achieving comparable accuracy for capacitance extraction, SesQ delivers superior precision for EPR calculation. Simulations of practical transmon qubits further reveal that FEM approaches tend to significantly underestimate the EPR. Finally, the high efficiency of SesQ enables rapid iteration in the layout optimization, as demonstrated by minimizing the EPR of the qubit pattern, establishing the simulator as a powerful tool for the automated design of low-loss superconducting quantum circuits.
one_liner: SesQ is a surface-integral simulator that computes participation ratios in superconducting qubits far faster and more accurately than standard FEM workflows.
why_it_matters: Low-loss qubit design depends on precise dielectric-loss estimation, and this tool makes that optimization practical at layout-iteration speed.
detailed_summary: SesQ replaces 3D volumetric meshing with a 2D surface-integral formulation using semi-analytical multilayer Green's functions and non-conformal boundary mesh refinement to capture singular edge fields. Analytical benchmarks indicate roughly two orders of magnitude acceleration for capacitance extraction relative to commercial FEM while maintaining comparable capacitance accuracy. For practical transmon geometries, the method yields more accurate energy participation ratios and shows that FEM can substantially underestimate EPR.
main_result: The simulator delivers much faster capacitance extraction and higher-fidelity EPR estimation, enabling rapid optimization of low-loss superconducting qubit layouts.
application_angle: It is directly useful for CAD flows that aim to reduce dielectric loss and improve coherence in manufacturable superconducting quantum processors.

title: Tunable Nonlocal ZZ Interaction for Remote Controlled-Z Gates Between Distributed Fixed-Frequency Qubits
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires large-scale superconducting processors, yet monolithic architectures face increasing constraints from wiring density, crosstalk, and fabrication yield. Modular superconducting platforms offer a scalable alternative, but achieving high-fidelity entangling gates between distant modules remains a central challenge, particularly for highly coherent fixed-frequency qubits. Here, we propose a distributed hardware architecture designed to overcome this bottleneck by employing a pair of double-transmon couplers (DTCs). By synchronously controlling the two DTCs stationed at opposite ends of a macroscopic cable, our scheme strongly suppresses residual static inter-module coupling while enabling on-demand activation of a non-local cross-Kerr interaction with an on/off ratio exceeding $10^6$. Through comprehensive system-level numerical simulations incorporating realistic hardware parameters, we demonstrate that this mechanism can realize a remote controlled-Z (CZ) gate with a fidelity over 99.99\% between fixed-frequency transmons housed in separate packages interconnected by a 25 cm coaxial cable. These results establish a highly viable, hardware-efficient route toward high-performance distributed superconducting processors.
one_liner: A dual-coupler architecture enables tunable nonlocal ZZ coupling and 99.99% remote CZ gates between fixed-frequency transmons across a 25 cm cable.
why_it_matters: It addresses a central bottleneck in modular superconducting computing: high-fidelity entangling gates between physically separated, highly coherent qubits.
detailed_summary: The proposed architecture uses a pair of double-transmon couplers positioned at opposite ends of a macroscopic interconnect to activate a nonlocal cross-Kerr interaction on demand. Synchronous control suppresses residual static coupling while achieving an on/off ratio above 10^6. Numerical system-level simulations with realistic parameters predict a remote controlled-Z gate fidelity exceeding 99.99% between fixed-frequency transmons in separate packages connected by a 25 cm coaxial cable.
platform_or_system: distributed fixed-frequency transmons with double-transmon couplers
main_result: It demonstrates a viable mechanism for ultrahigh-fidelity remote CZ gates between packaged superconducting qubits linked over centimeter-scale distances.
application_angle: This supports scalable modular processors by reducing wiring-density pressure and enabling chip-to-chip entangling operations without sacrificing coherence.

title: The Power of Power-of-SWAP: Postselected Quantum Computation with the Exchange Interaction
abstract: We introduce Exchange Quantum Polynomial Time (XQP) circuits, which comprise quantum computation using only computational basis SPAM and the isotropic Heisenberg exchange interaction. Structurally, this sub-universal model captures decoherence-free subspace computation without access to singlet states. We show that XQP occupies an intermediate position between BPP and BQP, as its efficient multiplicative-error simulation would collapse the polynomial hierarchy to its third level. We further provide evidence that additive-error simulation of XQP would enable efficient additive-error simulation of arbitrary BQP computations. Remarkably, the restricted family of XQP circuits consisting solely of $\sqrt{\mathrm{SWAP}}$ gates remains hard to simulate to multiplicative error. We additionally prove that circuits generated by $\sqrt{\mathrm{SWAP}}$ gates are semi-universal, generate $t$-designs for the uniform distribution over $SU(2)$-invariant unitaries, and maximise the entangling power within XQP. Finally, we derive structural results linking computational basis states in XQP to the Gelfand-Tsetlin basis of the symmetric group, and expressing XQP output probabilities as partition functions of the six-vertex and Potts models. Our findings indicate that XQP circuits are naturally suited to near-term hardware and provide a promising platform for experimental demonstrations of quantum computational advantage.
one_liner: XQP circuits based only on exchange interactions and basis-state SPAM define a hardware-native, classically hard model centered on powers of SWAP.
why_it_matters: The work identifies a restricted interaction set that is both experimentally natural and strongly suggestive of near-term quantum advantage.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces Exchange Quantum Polynomial Time, a sub-universal model using only isotropic Heisenberg exchange and computational-basis state preparation and measurement. It proves multiplicative-error simulation hardness unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses, and argues that additive-error simulation would imply efficient simulation of general BQP circuits. The authors further show that circuits built solely from sqrtSWAP gates remain hard, are semi-universal, form SU(2)-invariant t-designs, and maximize entangling power within the model.
main_result: It establishes exchange-only XQP, including sqrtSWAP-only circuits, as a physically realistic computation model with strong complexity-theoretic evidence of classical intractability.
application_angle: The results point to simpler control stacks for spin-based hardware while preserving a credible path toward experimental demonstrations of computational advantage.

title: From Hole Theory to Quantum Field Theory: Relativistic Fermions and the Role of Ettore Majorana (1933-1937)
abstract: Between 1933 and 1937, the treatment of relativistic spin-1/2 particles, initially rooted in Hole theory, evolved into the modern framework of quantum field theory. This paper reconstructs the crucial stages of that transition by examining the formal and physical progress of the numerous authors who shaped the field's modern formalism. This historical study traces the development of fermionic field theory in full, beginning with the foundational work of the 1920s, focussing on the results of the 1930s, and concluding with the influential synthesis of Wolfgang Pauli in 1941, the content of which has shaped the subsequent tradition. Within this framework, particular emphasis is given to Ettore Majorana's 1937 quantisation procedure and argument for anti-commuting fermionic quantum fields. This study demonstrates that Majorana's work was not merely a technical variant, but the definitive rejection of the concept of negative energy solutions, whose conceptual clarity and educational value remain vital today.
one_liner: A historical analysis traces how relativistic fermion theory moved from hole theory to quantum field theory, emphasizing Majorana's 1937 quantization framework.
why_it_matters: It clarifies the conceptual foundations behind fermionic anticommutation and Majorana-style field descriptions that still underpin modern quantum theory.
detailed_summary: The article reconstructs the 1933-1937 transition from hole theory to the modern quantum-field-theoretic treatment of relativistic spin-1/2 particles. Its technical emphasis is on Majorana's 1937 quantization procedure and his argument for anticommuting fermionic fields, framed against contemporaneous developments by other authors. The paper argues that Majorana's formulation was a decisive conceptual break from negative-energy interpretations and helped shape the later formal synthesis associated with Pauli.
main_result: It identifies Majorana's 1937 treatment as a pivotal step in establishing anticommuting fermion fields and abandoning negative-energy hole-theory concepts.
application_angle: Its value is foundational rather than device-level, sharpening the conceptual basis for modern fermionic and Majorana-inspired quantum models.

title: Discrete Cavity Dynamics in Free-Space Brillouin Laser
abstract: Highly coherent lasers are central to modern photonics. To date, high-coherence operation has been achieved predominantly in microcavity and fiber-based platforms. More recently, free-space Brillouin-laser experiments have revealed unusually strong noise suppression whose physical origin cannot be explained by conventional continuous-medium models developed for those platforms. In conventional continuous-medium models, the optical and acoustic fields are assumed to remain continuously coupled throughout the cavity evolution, whereas in free-space implementations the coupling is confined to the nonlinear medium and interrupted by passive propagation over the rest of the round trip. To describe this interaction-propagation separation, we develop a discrete-cavity model in which the short Brillouin interaction inside the gain medium and the subsequent free-space propagation are treated as two separate stages of the round-trip evolution. This separation introduces a temporal asymmetry between optical storage and acoustic relaxation, which effectively enhances acoustic damping at the cavity level and strongly reduces pump-noise transfer to the Stokes field. If the cavity round-trip time is much longer than the interaction time in the nonlinear medium, the noise-suppression ratio scales with the ratio of the total cavity length to the nonlinear-medium length. Our discrete-cavity model further provides quantitative predictions for the lasing threshold, output power, phase-noise transfer, and fundamental linewidth, in good agreement with experiment. These results identify the discrete interaction-propagation structure as the physical origin of the unusually strong noise suppression in free-space Brillouin lasers systems.
one_liner: A discrete-cavity theory explains the unusually strong noise suppression seen in free-space Brillouin lasers by separating interaction and propagation stages.
why_it_matters: The model gives predictive design rules for building highly coherent free-space Brillouin lasers with low pump-noise transfer and narrow linewidths.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a round-trip discrete-cavity model in which Brillouin gain occurs only inside the nonlinear medium while the rest of the cavity evolution is passive propagation. This interaction-propagation separation creates an asymmetry between optical storage and acoustic relaxation, effectively increasing cavity-level acoustic damping and reducing pump-noise transfer to the Stokes field. The theory predicts threshold, output power, phase-noise transfer, and linewidth, and matches experimental observations. It also shows that the noise-suppression ratio scales with total cavity length divided by nonlinear-medium length when the round-trip time dominates the interaction time.
main_result: It attributes strong coherence enhancement in free-space Brillouin lasers to discrete cavity dynamics and provides quantitatively accurate performance predictions.
application_angle: The framework can guide low-noise laser engineering for coherent optical links, precision photonics, and communication systems requiring stable narrow-linewidth sources.

title: Shor's algorithm is possible with as few as 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits
abstract: Quantum computers have the potential to perform computational tasks beyond the reach of classical machines. A prominent example is Shor's algorithm for integer factorization and discrete logarithms, which is of both fundamental importance and practical relevance to cryptography. However, due to the high overhead of quantum error correction, optimized resource estimates for cryptographically relevant instances of Shor's algorithm require millions of physical qubits. Here, by leveraging advances in high-rate quantum error-correcting codes, efficient logical instruction sets, and circuit design, we show that Shor's algorithm can be executed at cryptographically relevant scales with as few as 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits. Increasing the number of physical qubits improves time efficiency by enabling greater parallelism; under plausible assumptions, the runtime for discrete logarithms on the P-256 elliptic curve could be just a few days for a system with 26,000 physical qubits, while the runtime for factoring RSA-2048 integers is one to two orders of magnitude longer. Recent neutral-atom experiments have demonstrated universal fault-tolerant operations below the error-correction threshold, computation on arrays of hundreds of qubits, and trapping arrays with more than 6,000 highly coherent qubits. Although substantial engineering challenges remain, our theoretical analysis indicates that an appropriately designed neutral-atom architecture could support quantum computation at cryptographically relevant scales. More broadly, these results highlight the capability of neutral atoms for fault-tolerant quantum computing with wide-ranging scientific and technological applications.
one_liner: A neutral-atom fault-tolerance analysis argues cryptographically relevant Shor attacks may fit within roughly 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits.
why_it_matters: It sharply lowers projected hardware requirements for useful fault-tolerant factoring and discrete-log attacks, making neutral-atom quantum computing a nearer-term security concern.
detailed_summary: The paper combines high-rate quantum error-correcting codes, efficient logical gate sets, and algorithm-level circuit optimization to estimate resources for Shor's algorithm on reconfigurable neutral-atom hardware. It claims cryptographically relevant workloads can fit in about 10,000 physical qubits, with larger machines trading qubit count for parallel execution speed. Under stated assumptions, discrete logarithms on P-256 could run in days on a 26,000-qubit system, while RSA-2048 factoring would take substantially longer. The analysis ties these projections to recent neutral-atom milestones in below-threshold logical operations and large coherent atom arrays.
main_result: The study estimates that Shor's algorithm at cryptographically relevant scales could be executed with as few as 10,000 reconfigurable atomic qubits.
application_angle: It directly informs realistic timelines for code-breaking quantum machines and prioritizes neutral-atom engineering targets for fault-tolerant hardware.

title: Hunting for quantum advantage in electronic structure calculations is a highly non-trivial task
abstract: In light of major developments over the past decades in both quantum computing and simulations on classical hardware, it is a serious challenge to identify a real-world problem where quantum advantage is expected to appear. In quantum chemistry, electronic structure calculations of strongly correlated, i.e. multi-reference problems, are often argued to fall into such category because of their intractability with standard methods based on mean-field theory. Therefore, providing state-of-the-art benchmark data by classical algorithms is necessary to make a decisive conclusion when such competing development directions are compared. We report cutting-edge performance results together with high accuracy ground state energy for the Fe$_4$S$_4$ molecular cluster on a CAS(54,36) model space, a problem that has been included quite recently among the list of systems in the {\it Quantum Advantage Tracker} webpage maintained by IBM and RIKEN. Pushing the limits even further, we also present CAS-SCF based orbital optimizations for unprecedented CAS sizes of up to 89 electrons in 102 orbitals [CAS(89,102)] for the Fe$_5$S$_{12}$H$_4^{5-}$ molecular system comprising twenty five open shell orbitals in its sextet ground state and an active spaces size of 331 electrons in 451 orbitals. We have achieved our results via mixed-precision spin-adapted \textit{ab initio} Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) electronic structure calculations interfaced with the ORCA program package and utilizing the NVIDIA Blackwell graphics processing unit (GPU) platform. We argue that DMRG benchmark data should be taken as a classical reference when quantum advantage is reported. In addition, full exploitation of classical hardware should also be considered since even the most advanced DMRG implementations are still in a premature stage regarding utilization of all the benefits of GPU technology.
one_liner: Large-scale GPU-accelerated DMRG benchmarks show that proving quantum advantage in hard electronic-structure problems is tougher than often assumed.
why_it_matters: It raises the classical baseline for quantum chemistry advantage claims by demonstrating state-of-the-art accuracy on strongly correlated transition-metal clusters.
detailed_summary: Using mixed-precision spin-adapted ab initio DMRG interfaced with ORCA on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs, the authors compute high-accuracy ground-state energies for Fe4S4 in a CAS(54,36) active space. They extend orbital-optimized CAS-SCF studies to extremely large spaces, including CAS(89,102) for an Fe5S12H4^{5-} system and an overall active-space size of 331 electrons in 451 orbitals. The core message is that aggressively optimized classical tensor-network methods remain highly competitive on problems currently cited as quantum-advantage candidates. The work argues DMRG benchmarks should be mandatory reference points before claiming practical quantum advantage in chemistry.
platform_or_system: GPU-accelerated spin-adapted DMRG for correlated molecular clusters
main_result: The authors deliver cutting-edge classical benchmark energies and orbital optimizations for challenging iron-sulfur clusters at active-space sizes relevant to quantum-advantage discussions.
application_angle: These benchmarks help experimental quantum computing and quantum simulation groups choose targets where a real application-level advantage is still plausible.

title: Kramers-Kronig causality in integrated photonics: The spectral tension between ultraviolet transition and mid-infrared absorption
abstract: Dispersion engineering via geometric confinement is essential to integrated photonics, enabling phenomena such as soliton microcombs, supercontinua, parametric oscillators, and entangled photons. However, prevailing methodologies rely on semi-empirical Sellmeier models that assume idealized material purity, neglecting the pronounced dispersion shifts induced by residual impurities like hydrogen-related bonds. Here, we demonstrate that these residual bonds fundamentally alter the dispersion landscape spanning from the ultraviolet (UV) to the mid-infrared (MIR) spectra. Specifically, they introduce MIR vibrational absorption while simultaneously modifying UV electronic transition, shifting the bandgap and UV pole. We show that the spectral tension between these UV and MIR modifications dictates the group velocity dispersion from the visible to the near-infrared (NIR) via the Kramers-Kronig causality. We experimentally validate this phenomenon through systematic characterization of broadband loss and dispersion in ultralow-loss silicon nitride photonic integrated circuits. By rigorously incorporating these effects, we bridge the gap between empirical fitting and predictive physical modelling. Our study resolves long-standing discrepancies in dispersion engineering, providing precision control essential for next-generation integrated photonics.
one_liner: Broadband measurements in ultralow-loss SiN chips reveal how residual bond absorption and UV band-edge shifts jointly set integrated-photonic dispersion.
why_it_matters: It provides a more predictive materials-and-dispersion model for designing combs, nonlinear photonics, and quantum light sources on chip.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that hydrogen-related residual bonds in silicon nitride simultaneously introduce mid-infrared vibrational absorption and shift ultraviolet electronic transitions. Through Kramers-Kronig causality, those spectral changes feed back into the visible and near-infrared group-velocity dispersion that designers usually treat with simplified Sellmeier fits. The authors experimentally characterize broadband loss and dispersion in ultralow-loss integrated SiN circuits and find that these impurity-driven effects explain longstanding discrepancies between model and device behavior. The result is a physics-grounded framework for predictive dispersion control across a wide spectral span.
platform_or_system: ultralow-loss silicon nitride photonic integrated circuits
main_result: The work experimentally validates that coupled UV-pole and MIR-absorption shifts from residual bonds control usable dispersion in integrated SiN photonics via Kramers-Kronig relations.
application_angle: Better predictive dispersion engineering improves yield and performance for microcombs, supercontinua, parametric devices, and chip-scale entangled-photon sources.

title: A mathematical description of the spin Hall effect of light in inhomogeneous media
abstract: We study Gaussian wave packet solutions for Maxwell's equations in an isotropic, inhomogeneous medium and derive a system of ordinary differential equations that captures the leading-order correction to geodesic motion. The dynamical quantities in this system are the energy centroid, the linear and angular momentum, and the quadrupole moment. Furthermore, the system is closed to first order in the inverse frequency. As an immediate consequence, the energy centroids of Gaussian wave packets with opposite circular polarisations generally propagate in different directions, thereby providing a mathematical proof of the spin Hall effect of light in an inhomogeneous medium.
one_liner: A Gaussian-wave-packet treatment of Maxwell dynamics gives a first-order closed model proving spin-dependent beam deflection in inhomogeneous media.
why_it_matters: It upgrades the spin Hall effect of light from heuristic ray corrections to a rigorous dynamical framework usable in precision photonics modeling.
detailed_summary: The authors derive ordinary differential equations for the evolution of a Gaussian electromagnetic wave packet in an isotropic inhomogeneous medium, including centroid, linear momentum, angular momentum, and quadrupole moment. The system is closed to first order in inverse frequency, capturing leading corrections beyond geodesic ray motion. Within this framework, opposite circular polarizations acquire different centroid trajectories, furnishing a mathematical proof of the spin Hall effect of light in such media. The treatment provides a controlled asymptotic bridge between full-wave Maxwell theory and spin-orbit corrected optical transport.
platform_or_system: Gaussian optical wave packets in isotropic inhomogeneous media
main_result: The paper proves that left- and right-circularly polarized Gaussian packets generally propagate along different paths in inhomogeneous media.
application_angle: This can support more accurate design of polarization-sensitive imaging, beam steering, and nanophotonic devices where spin-orbit optical effects matter.

title: Securing Elliptic Curve Cryptocurrencies against Quantum Vulnerabilities: Resource Estimates and Mitigations
abstract: This whitepaper seeks to elucidate implications that the capabilities of developing quantum architectures have on blockchain vulnerabilities and mitigation strategies. First, we provide new resource estimates for breaking the 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem, the core of modern blockchain cryptography. We demonstrate that Shor's algorithm for this problem can execute with either <1200 logical qubits and <90 million Toffoli gates or <1450 logical qubits and <70 million Toffoli gates. In the interest of responsible disclosure, we use a zero-knowledge proof to validate these results without disclosing attack vectors. On superconducting architectures with 1e-3 physical error rates and planar connectivity, those circuits can execute in minutes using fewer than half a million physical qubits. We introduce a critical distinction between fast-clock (such as superconducting and photonic) and slow-clock (such as neutral atom and ion trap) architectures. Our analysis reveals that the first fast-clock CRQCs would enable on-spend attacks on public mempool transactions of some cryptocurrencies. We survey major cryptocurrency vulnerabilities through this lens, identifying systemic risks associated with advanced features in some blockchains such as smart contracts, Proof-of-Stake consensus, and Data Availability Sampling, as well as the enduring concern of abandoned assets. We argue that technical solutions would benefit from accompanying public policy and discuss various frameworks of digital salvage to regulate the recovery or destruction of dormant assets while preventing adversarial seizure. We also discuss implications for other digital assets and tokenization as well as challenges and successful examples of the ongoing transition to Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC). Finally, we urge all vulnerable cryptocurrency communities to join the ongoing migration to PQC without delay.
one_liner: New resource estimates suggest elliptic-curve cryptocurrency attacks could be feasible on fast-clock fault-tolerant quantum machines well before million-qubit scales.
why_it_matters: It links concrete quantum hardware assumptions to blockchain risk, clarifying which architectures threaten live transaction security and when migration is urgent.
detailed_summary: The whitepaper estimates that solving the 256-bit elliptic-curve discrete logarithm problem may require under 1,200 logical qubits and under 90 million Toffoli gates, or under 1,450 logical qubits and under 70 million Toffoli gates. It maps those logical workloads onto hardware assumptions, arguing that superconducting platforms with approximately 10^-3 physical error rates and planar connectivity could execute the circuits in minutes using fewer than 500,000 physical qubits. The authors distinguish fast-clock architectures from slower neutral-atom and ion-trap systems and analyze implications for mempool attacks, abandoned assets, staking, and smart-contract ecosystems. They pair the technical estimates with mitigation guidance centered on rapid PQC migration and governance frameworks for dormant funds.
platform_or_system: fault-tolerant superconducting and other fast-clock quantum architectures
main_result: The paper concludes that fast-clock fault-tolerant quantum computers could mount practical attacks on some elliptic-curve-based cryptocurrency workflows with sub-million physical-qubit systems.
application_angle: It gives blockchain operators and protocol designers a concrete basis for prioritizing post-quantum upgrades and transaction-flow defenses.

title: Quantum Optical Neuron for Image Classification via Multiphoton Interference
abstract: The rapid growth of machine learning is increasingly constrained by the energy and bandwidth limits of classical hardware. Optical and quantum technologies offer an alternative route, enabling high-dimensional, parallel information processing directly in the physical layer, particularly suited for imaging tasks. In this context, quantum photonic platforms provide both a natural mechanism for computing inner products and a promising path to energy-efficient inference in photon-limited regimes. Here, we experimentally demonstrate a camera-free quantum-optical images classifier that performs inference directly at the measurement layer using Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference of spatially programmable single photons. Two-photon coincidences directly report the overlap between an input image mode and a learned template, replacing pixel-resolved acquisition with a single global measurement. We realize both a single-perceptron quantum optical neuron and a two-neuron shallow network, achieving high accuracy on benchmark datasets with strong robustness to experimental noise and minimal hardware complexity. With a fixed measurement budget, performance remains insensitive to input resolution, demonstrating intrinsic robustness to the number of pixels, which would be impossible in a classical framework. This approach paves the way toward neuromorphic quantum photonic processors capable of extracting task-relevant information directly from HOM interference, with promising applications in remote object recognition, low-signal sensing, and photon-starved biological microscopy.
one_liner: A camera-free quantum optical classifier uses Hong-Ou-Mandel interference of programmable single photons to perform image inference directly in hardware.
why_it_matters: It shows a realistic route to low-light optical inference where classification emerges from multiphoton interference rather than pixel-by-pixel sensing.
detailed_summary: The experiment encodes an input image mode and a learned template into spatially programmable single-photon states and uses two-photon Hong-Ou-Mandel interference to measure their overlap.
