This implements a single quantum optical perceptron and a shallow two-neuron network without conventional camera readout, relying on coincidence counts as the inference signal. The reported classifier achieves high accuracy on benchmark datasets and remains robust to noise and to changes in input resolution at fixed measurement budget. The work demonstrates task-specific optical processing directly at the measurement layer with minimal hardware overhead.
main_result: The authors experimentally realize single- and two-neuron quantum optical classifiers that perform image recognition directly from HOM interference measurements.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for remote recognition, photon-starved microscopy, and low-signal sensing where collecting full images is costly or impossible.

title: Central Limit Theorems for Outcome Records in Disordered Quantum Trajectories
abstract: We prove annealed central limit theorems for finite pattern counts in the measurement record of discrete-time quantum trajectories generated by repeated measurements in a disordered environment. Under summable mixing assumptions on the environment and an annealed trace-norm forgetting property for the associated non-selective channel cocycle, we first establish the CLT under the annealed law determined by the dynamically stationary state. This part applies to general disordered quantum instruments and, in particular, is not restricted to the perfect-measurement regime; it complements both the corresponding law of large numbers for disordered measurement records and the homogeneous central limit theorem. We then introduce a coupling-based notion of admissibility for initial states and show that the same Gaussian limit extends to every admissible initial law, with unchanged centering and asymptotic variance. In the perfect-measurement setting, we further identify a general condition ensuring admissibility for every initial state, and hence obtain a universal annealed central limit theorem. We also provide practical sufficient criteria for this condition and verify the assumptions across a broad family of examples, including disordered walk-type models generated by finite group actions
one_liner: Establishes Gaussian fluctuation laws for pattern counts in disordered quantum measurement records under broad mixing and forgetting conditions.
why_it_matters: It gives rigorous statistics for noisy monitored quantum systems, useful for validating trajectory-based photonic and open-system experiments in realistic disorder.
detailed_summary: The paper proves annealed central limit theorems for finite pattern counts in discrete-time quantum-trajectory measurement records generated by repeated measurements in disordered environments. The result covers general disordered quantum instruments beyond perfect measurements, assuming summable environmental mixing and annealed trace-norm forgetting of the non-selective channel cocycle. It further introduces an admissibility criterion for initial states and shows the same asymptotic Gaussian law extends beyond the dynamically stationary state. In the perfect-measurement regime, the authors provide conditions under which admissibility is universal, yielding a CLT for all initial states across broad walk-type examples.
main_result: The authors prove universal annealed Gaussian limits for measurement-record pattern statistics under explicit mixing, forgetting, and admissibility conditions.
application_angle: The theory can guide statistical benchmarking of monitored photonic or quantum-optical experiments where disorder and nonideal measurements affect observed detection sequences.

title: Iterative Optimization with Partial Convergence Guarantees on Neutral Atom Quantum Computers
abstract: Neutral atom quantum computers (NAQCs) have emerged as a promising platform for solving the maximum weighted independent set (MWIS) problem. However, analog quantum approaches face two key limitations: constraints of the atomic layout on realizable graph geometries and the absence of performance guarantees. We introduce Lp-Quts, a hybrid quantum-classical framework that integrates an NAQC sampler into a classical cutting-plane algorithm. At each iteration, a relaxed linear program (RLP) bounds the MWIS and induces a reduced graph from which independent sets are sampled using an analog quantum sampler. A novel sample-informed separation problem guides odd-cycle cut selection and accelerates convergence. For t-perfect graphs, Lp-Quts inherits polynomial-time convergence guarantees from the classical theory of cutting planes. We evaluate our approach on instances with up to 300 vertices -- a scale that exceeds the capabilities of current NAQC hardware. In this regime, Lp-Quts reaches solutions within 5--10\% of optimality, outperforming direct analog quantum protocols and greedy baselines under equal sampling budgets. As expected, simulated annealing remains the strongest sample-based solver at this scale. These results demonstrate how quantum samplers can be effectively embedded within classical optimization frameworks to deliver near-optimal solutions with reduced quantum resources while preserving formal guarantees.
one_liner: A hybrid neutral-atom/classical solver embeds analog sampling into cutting planes for MWIS with partial convergence guarantees.
why_it_matters: It shows how current neutral-atom hardware can be used inside guaranteed optimization loops rather than as a standalone heuristic sampler.
detailed_summary: Lp-Quts combines a neutral-atom quantum computer sampler with a relaxed linear-program cutting-plane method for the maximum weighted independent set problem. Each iteration solves an RLP, constructs a reduced graph, samples independent sets with the analog quantum device, and uses a sample-informed separation problem to select odd-cycle cuts more efficiently. For t-perfect graphs, the framework inherits polynomial-time convergence guarantees from classical cutting-plane theory. On simulated instances up to 300 vertices, it reaches solutions within 5-10% of optimum and outperforms direct analog protocols and greedy baselines at fixed sampling budget.
main_result: The method achieves near-optimal MWIS solutions at scales beyond current hardware while retaining formal partial convergence guarantees on structured graph classes.
application_angle: This hybrid architecture is practically relevant because it reduces quantum resource demands and fits near-term neutral-atom devices into usable combinatorial optimization workflows.

title: Bell's Inequality, Causal Bounds, and Quantum Bayesian Computation: A Unified Framework
abstract: Bell inequalities characterize the boundary of the local-realist correlation polytope -- the set of joint probability distributions achievable by classical hidden-variable models. Quantum mechanics exceeds this boundary through non-commutativity, reaching the Tsirelson bound $2\sqrt{2}$ for CHSH. We show that this polytope structure is not specific to quantum foundations: it appears identically in the causal inference literature, where the instrumental inequality, the Balke--Pearl linear programming bounds, and the Tian--Pearl probabilities of causation all arise as facets of the same marginal compatibility polytope. Fine's theorem -- that CHSH inequalities hold if and only if a joint distribution exists -- is precisely the pivot: the instrumental variable model in causal inference is structurally equivalent to the Bell local hidden-variable model, with the instrument playing the role of the measurement setting and the latent confounder playing the role of the hidden variable $λ$. We develop this correspondence in detail, extending it to algorithmic (Kolmogorov complexity) and entropic formulations of Bell inequalities, the NPA semidefinite programming hierarchy, and the MIP$^*$=RE undecidability result. We further show that the Born-rule / Bayes-rule duality underlying quantum Bayesian computation exploits the same non-commutativity that enables Bell violation, providing polynomial speedups for posterior inference. The framework yields a concrete dictionary between quantum information theory, causal econometrics, and Bayesian computation, and suggests new directions including NPA-based quantum causal inference algorithms and quantum architectures for function approximation.
one_liner: Unifies Bell inequalities, causal-inference bounds, and quantum Bayesian computation through a shared marginal-compatibility polytope.
why_it_matters: It links quantum nonlocality tools to causal inference and probabilistic computing, suggesting transferable algorithms across communication and inference tasks.
detailed_summary: The paper argues that Bell-local correlation constraints and classical causal-inference bounds arise from the same marginal compatibility polytope, with Fine's theorem as the central equivalence. It maps instrumental-variable models to Bell scenarios, connecting Balke-Pearl bounds, instrumental inequalities, and probabilities of causation to facets familiar from nonlocality studies. The framework is extended to entropic and algorithmic Bell inequalities, the NPA semidefinite hierarchy, and undecidability results from MIP*=RE. It also relates Born-rule/Bayes-rule duality in quantum Bayesian computation to the non-commutativity that enables Bell violations and potential polynomial inference speedups.
main_result: The authors provide a detailed mathematical dictionary showing Bell nonlocality, causal bounds, and quantum Bayesian inference are instances of one shared structural framework.
application_angle: The work could inform quantum-network verification and new inference algorithms that reuse nonlocality machinery for practical causal estimation and probabilistic computation.

title: Oxide-nitride heteroepitaxy for low-loss dielectrics in superconducting quantum circuits
abstract: Superconducting qubits show great promise for the realization of fault-tolerant quantum computing, but lossy, amorphous dielectrics limit current technology. Identifying highly crystalline and stoichiometric dielectrics with intrinsically low microwave loss is therefore a central materials challenge, yet experimentally validated platforms remain scarce. In this work, we integrate a crystalline dielectric into a heteroepitaxial TiN/$γ$-Al$_2$O$_3$/TiN trilayer grown via pulsed laser deposition. Correlative high-resolution imaging, diffraction, and spectroscopy measurements confirm the single-crystal quality and chemical integrity of all layers, with minimal defects and limited anion interdiffusion across the oxide-nitride interfaces. Using microwave lumped-element resonators with parallel-plate capacitors, we report the first direct measurement of the dielectric loss of epitaxial $γ$-Al$_2$O$_3$, for which we find a low intrinsic two-level system loss, $δ_{\text{TLS}}^0 = (2.8 \pm 0.1) \times 10^{-5}$. These results establish heteroepitaxial oxides on transition metal nitrides as an attractive materials platform for superconducting quantum circuits, particularly for integration into compact device architectures such as merged-element transmons and microwave kinetic inductance detectors.
one_liner: Demonstrates low-loss epitaxial gamma-Al2O3 dielectrics integrated in TiN/Al2O3/TiN superconducting trilayers for quantum circuits.
why_it_matters: Dielectric loss is a major coherence bottleneck in superconducting hardware, so a validated crystalline capacitor stack is directly useful for better qubits and detectors.
detailed_summary: The authors grow a heteroepitaxial TiN/gamma-Al2O3/TiN trilayer by pulsed laser deposition and verify high crystalline quality and chemical integrity using high-resolution imaging, diffraction, and spectroscopy. They then fabricate microwave lumped-element resonators with parallel-plate capacitors to directly extract dielectric loss in epitaxial gamma-Al2O3. The measured intrinsic two-level-system loss is delta_TLS^0 = (2.8 +/- 0.1) x 10^-5, indicating substantially reduced microwave dissipation. Minimal defects and limited anion interdiffusion support the viability of oxide-nitride interfaces for compact superconducting devices.
main_result: The work reports the first direct low-loss measurement of epitaxial gamma-Al2O3 in a superconducting trilayer capacitor platform.
application_angle: This materials platform is immediately relevant for merged-element transmons, compact resonators, and kinetic-inductance detectors that need low-loss integrated dielectrics.

title: Ether of Orbifolds
abstract: Whose world is this? The orbifold lattice has been proposed as a bridge to practical quantum simulation of Yang--Mills theory, claiming exponential speedup over all known approaches. Through analytical derivations, Monte Carlo simulation, and explicit circuit construction, we identify compounding hidden costs entirely absent in Kogut--Susskind formulations: a mass-dependent Trotter overhead that scales as $m^4$, gauge-violating dynamics that grow as $m^2$ and worsen with penalty terms, and a mandatory mass extrapolation. Monte Carlo simulations of SU(3) establish a universal scaling: the continuum limit forces $m^2 \propto 1/a$, binding the Trotter step to the lattice spacing through a cost unique to orbifolds. For a fiducial $10^3$ calculation, the orbifold is $10^4$--$10^{10}$ times more expensive than every published alternative. The bridge is not built. The gap is the foundation.
one_liner: Finds orbifold-lattice Yang-Mills simulation incurs severe hidden costs that erase claimed practical quantum advantages.
why_it_matters: It is a hardware-reality check for quantum simulation proposals, clarifying which lattice-gauge formulations are actually viable on quantum devices.
detailed_summary: Through analytic derivations, Monte Carlo simulations, and explicit circuit constructions, the paper identifies major hidden overheads in orbifold-lattice quantum simulation of Yang-Mills theory. The authors show a mass-dependent Trotter cost scaling as m^4, gauge-violating dynamics growing as m^2, and a required mass extrapolation absent from Kogut-Susskind approaches. For SU(3), they find the continuum limit enforces m^2 proportional to 1/a, tying simulation cost directly to lattice spacing. Under a representative 10^3-scale calculation, the resulting resource estimates are 10^4 to 10^10 times worse than published alternatives.
platform_or_system: orbifold-lattice quantum simulation of Yang-Mills theory
main_result: The study concludes that orbifold formulations become dramatically more expensive than competing lattice-gauge simulation methods once continuum-scaling costs are included.
application_angle: These resource estimates help steer quantum-hardware roadmaps toward simulation encodings with realistic scaling rather than misleading nominal speedups.

title: Large-scale nonlinear optical computing with incoherent light via linear diffractive systems
abstract: Nonlinear computation is essential for various information processing tasks. Optical implementations are attractive because passive light propagation can manipulate high-dimensional signals with extreme throughput and parallelism; yet realizing nonlinear mappings in optical hardware remains challenging due to the weak nonlinearity of optical materials and the large intensities required to induce nonlinear interactions. This challenge is further amplified in many systems that operate with incoherent illumination, motivating a coherence-aware framework for scalable optical nonlinear processing. Here, we show that linear optical systems, in particular, optimized diffractive processors comprising passive surfaces, can perform large-scale nonlinear function approximation under spatially incoherent or partially coherent illumination, when preceded by intensity-only input encoding. We quantify how the accuracy of the nonlinear function approximation varies with the degree of parallelism, the number of diffractive layers, and the number of trainable diffractive features. Numerical results demonstrate snapshot computation of up to one million distinct nonlinear functions in a single forward pass through a diffractive processor, with the function outputs spatially multiplexed and read out using densely packed detectors at the output. We further provide a proof-of-concept experimental demonstration under incoherent illumination from a liquid crystal display (LCD), enabled by a model-free in situ learning strategy that jointly optimizes the diffractive profile and detector readout geometry in the presence of hardware imperfections and misalignments. Our findings establish diffractive processors as a massively parallel universal function approximator for both spatially incoherent and partially coherent illumination.
one_liner: Shows passive diffractive optics can approximate nonlinear functions at massive scale even with incoherent illumination.
why_it_matters: It enables high-throughput optical computing without relying on strong material nonlinearities, opening a practical route to scalable analog photonic processors.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates that optimized linear diffractive systems can realize nonlinear function approximation when inputs are encoded in intensity under spatially incoherent or partially coherent illumination. It analyzes how approximation accuracy depends on parallelism, diffractive-layer count, and the number of trainable features, and numerically shows up to one million distinct nonlinear functions can be computed in a single forward pass with spatially multiplexed outputs. A proof-of-concept experiment using LCD-based incoherent illumination validates the approach. The experimental optimization uses model-free in situ learning to co-design the diffractive profile and detector geometry despite hardware misalignment and imperfections.
platform_or_system: passive diffractive optical processor with incoherent illumination
main_result: The authors demonstrate massively parallel universal nonlinear approximation using only linear diffractive optics plus intensity encoding, including experimental validation.
application_angle: This could translate into low-power, high-throughput optical accelerators for sensing, imaging, and machine-learning inference where coherent sources or material nonlinearities are impractical.

title: High-efficiency and noise-immune quantum battery
abstract: Nowadays, quantum batteries (QBs) have been designed to outperform their classical counterparts by leveraging quantum advantages. For instance, the charging power greatly benefits from the entanglement generation of a collective charging scheme (e.g., the Dicke QB), especially in the ultrastrong coupling (USC) regime or even larger. However, apart from the fragility of the QB under intrinsic decoherence effects, another critical drawback emerges inevitably. Specifically, the non-negligible counter-rotating (CR) term in the USC regime would induce coherence in the energy basis of QB, thus remarkably degrading the charging efficiency. To tackle these challenges, we propose a high-efficiency and noise-immune QB boosted by dynamical modulation. It is demonstrated that the time-varying modulation can effectively reduce the CR coupling, resulting in a notable improvement in charging efficiency. Particularly, for a judicious choice of modulation parameters that entirely eliminate the CR interaction, the Dicke QB can be charged optimally, resembling the behavior of the Tavis-Cummings QB. In the subsequent storage process, beyond the natural robustness to pure dephasing noise, our scenario is also highly resilient to the dissipation noise and thus can achieve perfect energy storage by effective bath engineering. While feasible with current experimental platforms, our proposal offers a solid foundation for the implementation of a powerful QB and may drastically promote the development of energy storage and delivery techniques in the future.
one_liner: A dynamically modulated Dicke-model quantum battery suppresses counter-rotating errors and enables efficient, noise-resilient charging and storage.
why_it_matters: It addresses two core obstacles for quantum batteries in ultrastrong coupling: degraded charging efficiency from counter-rotating terms and vulnerability to environmental noise.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a Dicke quantum battery in the ultrastrong-coupling regime, where counter-rotating interactions create energy-basis coherence that reduces charging efficiency. By applying time-dependent modulation, the authors effectively renormalize or fully cancel the counter-rotating term, recovering Tavis-Cummings-like optimal charging. They further show strong robustness during storage: the scheme is naturally insensitive to pure dephasing and can also suppress dissipation through effective bath engineering. The proposal is framed as experimentally feasible on current light-matter platforms.
main_result: Time-dependent modulation can eliminate counter-rotating coupling and yield optimal charging with near-perfect, noise-immune energy storage.
application_angle: The approach points toward practical high-power quantum energy storage modules that remain effective under realistic decoherence and dissipation.

title: Quantum heat transport in nonequilibrium anisotropic Dicke model
abstract: Nonequilibrium heat transport and quantum thermodynamics in light-matter interacting systems have received increasing attention. Quantum thermal devices, e.g., heat valve and head diode, have been realized. Recently, it has been discovered that the anisotropic light-matter interactions can greatly modify the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of hybrid quantum systems, leading to nontrivial quantum phase transitions, quantum metrology, and nonclassicality of photons. To explore the influences of anisotropic light-matter interactions on quantum transport, we investigate heat flow in the nonequilibrium anisotropic Dicke model. In this model, an ensemble of qubits collectively interacts with an anisotropic photon field. Each component interacts with bosonic thermal reservoirs. Quantum dressed master equation (DME) is included to properly study dissipative dynamics of the anisotropic Dicke model. Within the eigenbasis of the reduced anisotropic Dicke system, strong qubit-photon couplings can be properly handled. Our results demonstrate that anisotropic qubit-photon interactions are crucial for modulating steady-state heat flow. In particular, it is found that under strong coupling the heat flow is dramatically suppressed by a large anisotropic qubit-photon factor. While under moderate coupling, the anisotropic qubit-photon interactions enhance the heat flow. Moreover, the increase in the number of qubits amplifies the flow characteristics, with the peaks increasing and the valleys decreasing. Besides, we derive two analytical expressions of heat flows in thermodynamic limit approximation with limiting anisotropic factors. These heat currents exhibit the cotunneling heat transport pictures. They also serve as the upper boundaries for the heat flows in the finite-size anisotropic Dicke model. We also analyze the thermal rectification effect in the anisotropic Dicke model.
one_liner: An anisotropic Dicke system shows tunable nonequilibrium heat transport, with coupling-dependent suppression, enhancement, and rectification.
why_it_matters: It identifies anisotropic light-matter interaction as a practical control knob for quantum thermal devices such as heat valves and rectifiers in hybrid photonic-spin systems.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze nonequilibrium heat flow in an anisotropic Dicke model where many qubits collectively couple to a photon mode, with both subsystems connected to bosonic reservoirs. Using a dressed master equation in the eigenbasis of the reduced system, they capture strong-coupling dissipative transport beyond simpler weak-coupling treatments. They find that anisotropy strongly reshapes steady-state heat flow: it suppresses current at strong coupling but can enhance it at moderate coupling, while larger qubit number amplifies peaks and valleys. Analytical thermodynamic-limit expressions reveal cotunneling-like heat currents and bound the finite-size results, and thermal rectification is also characterized.
platform_or_system: Qubit ensemble coupled to an anisotropic cavity mode
main_result: Anisotropic qubit-photon coupling provides strong control over heat current magnitude and rectification, with distinct enhancement and suppression regimes.
application_angle: These transport signatures are directly relevant to engineering quantum heat valves, thermal diodes, and on-chip thermal management in hybrid quantum hardware.

title: Calculating the quantum Fisher information via the truncated Wigner method
abstract: In this work, we propose new methods of parameter estimation using stochastic sampling quantum phase-space simulations. We show that it is possible to compute the quantum Fisher information (QFI) from semiclassical stochastic samples using the Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA). This method extends the class of quantum systems whose fundamental sensitivity limit can be computed efficiently to any system that can be modelled using the TWA, allowing the analysis of more meteorologically useful quantum states. We illustrate this approach with examples, including a system that evolves outside the spin-squeezing regime, where the method of moments fails.
one_liner: Quantum Fisher information is extracted from truncated-Wigner stochastic trajectories, extending sensitivity analysis to larger nonlinear quantum systems.
why_it_matters: It gives a scalable route to estimate fundamental metrological limits in systems where exact quantum simulation is intractable and spin-squeezing metrics fail.
detailed_summary: This work introduces methods to compute quantum Fisher information directly from semiclassical stochastic samples generated by the truncated Wigner approximation. The approach broadens QFI evaluation to any platform well-described by TWA, including systems with nonlinear dynamics and large mode occupations. The authors demonstrate the method on examples that evolve beyond the spin-squeezing regime, where moment-based estimators no longer capture the true metrological resource. The result is a practical bridge between phase-space simulation and rigorous quantum-enhancement benchmarking.
main_result: The paper shows that QFI can be estimated efficiently from truncated-Wigner trajectories, even for dynamics outside the spin-squeezing regime.
application_angle: This enables realistic metrology design and performance forecasting for complex photonic and atomic sensors that cannot be treated exactly.

title: A Floer Theoretic Approach to Energy Eigenstates on one Dimensional Configuration Spaces
abstract: In this article we consider two classical problems in Quantum Mechanics, namely the 'particle on a ring' and the 'particle in a box' from the viewpoint of symplectic topology. Interpreting the solutions of the corresponding time independent Schrödinger equation as orbits in a suitably chosen time dependent Hamiltonian system allows us to investigate them using Floer theory. More precisely we extend the definition of Rabinowitz Floer homology to non-autonomous Hamiltonians on $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$ with its standard symplectic structure and show that compactness of the moduli space of J-holomorphic curves still holds. With this homology we are then able to prove existence results for energy $E$ eigenstates on the 'ring' or in the 'box' for a big range of exterior potentials.
one_liner: Floer-theoretic machinery is used to prove existence of energy eigenstates for 1D quantum systems on a ring or in a box with broad potentials.
why_it_matters: Although theoretical, it imports symplectic-topology tools into quantum spectral problems and may broaden rigorous methods for constrained-wave systems.
detailed_summary: The paper reformulates time-independent Schrödinger problems on one-dimensional configuration spaces as orbit problems in a time-dependent Hamiltonian system. It extends Rabinowitz Floer homology to non-autonomous Hamiltonians on standard symplectic space and proves compactness of the relevant moduli space of J-holomorphic curves. With this framework, the authors establish existence results for eigenstates at energy E for the particle-on-a-ring and particle-in-a-box settings over a broad class of exterior potentials. The contribution is primarily mathematical, but it gives a new rigorous route to spectral existence questions.
platform_or_system: 1D quantum systems on ring and box geometries
main_result: A non-autonomous Floer-homology framework is constructed and used to prove existence of energy eigenstates for broad 1D quantum potential classes.
application_angle: The method could inform rigorous analysis of engineered wave and confinement problems where geometry and boundary conditions play a central role.

title: Pointwise and dynamic programming control synthesis for finite-level open quantum memory systems
abstract: This paper is concerned with finite-level quantum memory systems for retaining initial dynamic variables in the presence of external quantum noise. The system variables have an algebraic structure, similar to that of the Pauli matrices, and their Heisenberg picture evolution is governed by a quasilinear quantum stochastic differential equation. The latter involves a Hamiltonian whose parameters depend affinely on a classical control signal in the form of a deterministic function of time. The memory performance is quantified by a mean-square deviation of quantum system variables of interest from their initial conditions. We relate this functional to a matrix-valued state of an auxiliary classical control-affine dynamical system. This leads to a pointwise control design where the control signal minimises the time-derivative of the mean-square deviation with an additional quadratic penalty on the control. In an alternative finite-horizon setting with a terminal-integral cost functional, we apply dynamic programming and obtain a quadratically nonlinear Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, for which a solution is outlined in the form of a recursively computed asymptotic expansion.
one_liner: Control laws for open finite-level quantum memories are synthesized using local optimization and dynamic programming on an auxiliary classical system.
why_it_matters: It offers a systematic route to preserve stored quantum observables against noise, which is central to deployable quantum memory and communication nodes.
detailed_summary: The work considers finite-level open quantum memories whose Heisenberg-picture observables obey quasilinear quantum stochastic differential equations with control-affine Hamiltonian parameters. Memory degradation is quantified by the mean-square deviation of selected observables from their initial values, and this cost is mapped to a matrix-valued state of an auxiliary classical system. On that basis, the authors derive a pointwise feedback design minimizing the instantaneous growth of memory error with a quadratic control penalty. For finite-horizon optimization they formulate a quadratically nonlinear Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation and outline a recursive asymptotic solution.
main_result: The paper derives both instantaneous and finite-horizon control synthesis methods for suppressing memory loss in noisy controlled quantum memories.
application_angle: These control tools are relevant to stabilizing quantum repeaters, memory registers, and networked quantum devices under realistic environmental noise.

title: Direct measurement of the energy spectrum of a quantum dot qubit
abstract: The mapping between gate voltages applied to a double quantum dot, and the parameters of a Hubbard-like Hamiltonian, is of utmost importance for understanding and operating spin qubits. State-of-the-art techniques for measuring Hamiltonian parameters (e.g., detuning axis pulsed spectroscopy, DAPS) provide details about energy levels; however, tunnel coupling estimates typically reveal only a small portion of the full Hamiltonian. Here, we demonstrate a Hamiltonian-agnostic technique for measuring the double dot energy spectrum over a wide energy range, at every value of the detuning, called delta-axis spectroscopy (DAXS). We apply the DAXS method to obtain the energy spectrum of a Si/SiGe double quantum dot and use this data to extract the diagonal and off-diagonal couplings of a 15-level Hubbard-like Hamiltonian, demonstrating very good agreement with the experimental measurements.
one_liner: Delta-axis spectroscopy directly maps a Si/SiGe double-dot qubit spectrum and identifies the couplings of a 15-level Hubbard-like model.
why_it_matters: It gives a more complete and Hamiltonian-agnostic calibration method for semiconductor spin qubits than standard spectroscopy approaches.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce delta-axis spectroscopy (DAXS), a measurement protocol that directly probes the energy spectrum of a double quantum dot over a wide energy range across detuning values. Applied to a Si/SiGe device, the method produces enough spectral information to extract both diagonal energies and off-diagonal tunnel-like couplings of a 15-level Hubbard-like Hamiltonian. This goes beyond conventional detuning-axis pulsed spectroscopy, which typically only constrains a limited subset of Hamiltonian parameters. The reconstructed model shows very good agreement with experiment, supporting DAXS as a practical qubit-characterization tool.
platform_or_system: Si/SiGe double quantum dot spin qubit
main_result: DAXS directly reconstructs the full measured spectrum and enables extraction of the key parameters of a 15-level double-dot Hamiltonian.
application_angle: Improved Hamiltonian calibration should accelerate tuning, modeling, and scalable operation of semiconductor spin-qubit processors.

title: Entanglement between an NV Center and Chiral Photons in a Topological SWCNT Plasmonic Microtoroid
abstract: We present a theoretical proposal for a hybrid solid-state quantum node based on a single nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center coupled to a topological single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) plasmonic microtoroid. The SWCNT ring supports deeply sub-wavelength whispering-gallery-like plasmonic modes that are naturally described within a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid framework. Owing to the closed-ring topology, the cavity spectrum contains a zero-mode sector that is tunable by an external magnetic flux through an Aharonov-Bohm shift. We show that the strongly confined CNT near field can exhibit chiral spin-momentum locking, enabling the two circularly polarized NV transitions to couple selectively to clockwise and counter-clockwise cavity modes, while the parasitic linearly polarized $π$-transition is strongly suppressed by the pronounced anisotropy of the local Purcell enhancement. Based on a tripod stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) scheme, the system can in principle map the NV spin onto a spin-photon entangled state in a deterministic manner, which is then emitted into a side-coupled tapered optical fiber as a tunable flying qubit. We derive the cavity spectrum, the chiral selection rules, the effective tripod Hamiltonian, and the open-system master equation. Quantitative estimates indicate that, under cryogenic conditions and in the overcoupled regime, high-fidelity spin-photon entanglement and in situ magnetic tuning of the emitted photon frequency are in principle achievable. We also discuss a realistic fabrication route for the CNT resonator and deterministic positioning strategies for a single NV center in the CNT near field.
one_liner: A theory proposal for deterministic NV-spin entanglement with chiral plasmonic photons in a topological carbon-nanotube microtoroid.
why_it_matters: It combines a solid-state spin qubit with a tunable chiral plasmonic cavity, pointing to compact spin-photon interfaces for networked quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a hybrid quantum node in which a single NV center is coupled to whispering-gallery-like plasmonic modes of a topological single-walled carbon nanotube microtoroid. The closed-ring CNT cavity supports a zero-mode sector that can be shifted by magnetic flux, while spin-momentum locking in the near field makes the two circular NV transitions couple selectively to clockwise and counter-clockwise modes. Using a tripod STIRAP protocol and an open-system master-equation treatment, the authors show that the NV spin can in principle be mapped deterministically to a spin-photon entangled state emitted into a tapered fiber. Quantitative estimates indicate high-fidelity operation under cryogenic, overcoupled conditions with in situ magnetic tuning of the emitted photon frequency.
platform_or_system: NV center coupled to a topological SWCNT plasmonic microtoroid
main_result: The main result is a concrete theoretical scheme for high-fidelity, frequency-tunable spin-photon entanglement generation from an NV center into a chiral plasmonic-fiber interface.
application_angle: This architecture is relevant for integrated quantum networking because it targets deterministic fiber-coupled flying qubits from a nanoscale solid-state spin node.

title: Multipartite controlled-NOT gates using molecules and Rydberg atoms
abstract: We propose high-fidelity controlled-NOT (CNOT) gates in a hybrid system of polar molecules and Rydberg atoms based on the unconventional Rydberg pumping mechanism. By combining the rich internal structure of polar molecules with the strong dipole-dipole interactions of Rydberg atoms, we realize both two-to-one and one-to-two gate configurations. Numerical simulations show that the gate performance is robust against spontaneous emission from Rydberg states. The approach naturally extends to larger systems, as demonstrated by four-qubit implementations achieving three-to-one and one-to-three CNOT gates with fidelities exceeding 99\%. These results highlight hybrid molecule-Rydberg atom architectures as a promising platform for scalable quantum information processing.
one_liner: Hybrid polar molecules and Rydberg atoms are used to realize high-fidelity multipartite CNOT gates beyond standard two-qubit settings.
why_it_matters: It offers a scalable route to multi-qubit logic by exploiting complementary molecular structure and strong Rydberg interactions in one platform.
detailed_summary: The work proposes multipartite controlled-NOT gates in a hybrid architecture that couples polar molecules to Rydberg atoms via unconventional Rydberg pumping. Two-to-one and one-to-two CNOT configurations are designed first, then extended to four-qubit three-to-one and one-to-three implementations. Numerical simulations show fidelities above 99% and robustness against spontaneous emission from the Rydberg states. The scheme uses molecular internal-state richness together with long-range dipole-dipole interactions to implement nontrivial multi-qubit control natively.
platform_or_system: Hybrid polar molecules and Rydberg atoms
main_result: The paper demonstrates theoretically that multipartite CNOT gates up to four qubits can be implemented with simulated fidelities exceeding 99%.
application_angle: Native high-fidelity multi-qubit gates could reduce circuit depth and control overhead in scalable neutral-atom and hybrid quantum processors.

title: Nonlinear hydrodynamic response of a quantum Hall system
abstract: The quantum Hall effect realizes a quantized Hall resistance $R_{xy} = h/(νe^2)$ whereas the longitudinal resistance vanishes. The quantized value consists of the fundamental physical quantities, the elementary charge $e$ and the Planck constant $h$, along with an integer or fractional constant $ν$. High precision measurements of $R_{xy}$ allude to a linear relation between the applied current $I$ and the Hall voltage $V_\mathrm{H}$. Here, we argue that a nonlinear relation between $I$ and $V_\mathrm{H}$ could arise when the electric field is spatially inhomogeneous. We first discuss that the linear $I$-$V_\mathrm{H}$ relation holds with Galilean invariance. Then we consider a hydrodynamic description of a quantum Hall liquid to deal with an axially symmetric electric field. It reveals a nonlinear electronic response arising from the centrifugal force exerted on a curved flow and the density gradient invoked by vorticity.
one_liner: A hydrodynamic theory predicts nonlinear Hall-voltage response in quantum Hall fluids under spatially inhomogeneous electric fields.
why_it_matters: It identifies a mechanism for nonideal transport in precision Hall systems and may matter for mesoscopic sensing and metrology geometries.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze when the usual linear relation between current and Hall voltage can break down in a quantum Hall liquid. They show that Galilean invariance preserves linearity, but an axially symmetric, spatially varying electric field yields nonlinear electronic response within a hydrodynamic description. The effect arises from centrifugal forces associated with curved flow together with density gradients induced by vorticity. The result links geometric field inhomogeneity directly to nonlinear transport corrections in otherwise quantized Hall systems.
platform_or_system: Quantum Hall liquid under inhomogeneous electric fields
main_result: The main result is a theoretical prediction that spatial electric-field inhomogeneity can generate nonlinear current-voltage behavior in quantum Hall transport.
application_angle: Understanding these nonlinearities is useful for high-precision Hall metrology and device designs where field curvature or confinement cannot be neglected.

title: PAEMS: Precise and Adaptive Error Model for Superconducting Quantum Processors
abstract: Superconducting quantum processor units (QPUs) are incapable of producing massive datasets for quantum error correction (QEC) because of hardware limitations. Thus, QEC decoders heavily depend on synthetic data from qubit error models. Classic depolarizing error models with polynomial complexity present limited accuracy. Coherent density matrix methods suffer from exponential complexity $\propto O(4^n)$ where $n$ represents the number of qubits. This paper introduces PAEMS: a precise and adaptive qubit error model. Its qubit-wise separation framework, incorporating leakage propagation, captures error evolvements crossing spatial and temporal domains. Utilizing repetition-code experiment datasets, PAEMS effectively identifies the intrinsic qubit errors through an end-to-end optimization pipeline. Experiments on IBM's QPUs have demonstrated a 19.5$\times$, 9.3$\times$, and 5.2$\times$ reduction in timelike, spacelike, and spacetime error correlation, respectively, surpassing all of the previous works. It also outperforms the accuracy of Google's SI1000 error model by 58$\sim$73\% on multiple quantum platforms, including IBM's Brisbane, Sherbrooke, and Torino, as well as China Mobile's Wuyue and QuantumCTek's Tianyan.
one_liner: PAEMS is an adaptive qubit error model that captures leakage and spacetime correlations more accurately for superconducting QEC workloads.
why_it_matters: Better synthetic error data directly improves decoder realism and hardware-aware QEC development on superconducting processors with limited experimental data.
detailed_summary: PAEMS introduces a qubit-wise separated, adaptive error model for superconducting QPUs that explicitly tracks leakage propagation and error evolution across space and time. The model parameters are learned end-to-end from repetition-code datasets, allowing intrinsic device errors to be inferred from limited hardware measurements. On IBM processors, the method reduces residual timelike, spacelike, and spacetime error correlations by factors of 19.5, 9.3, and 5.2 relative to prior approaches. The paper also reports 58-73% accuracy gains over Google's SI1000 model across IBM, China Mobile, and QuantumCTek platforms.
main_result: The main result is an experimentally validated error model that substantially improves correlated-noise fidelity over standard superconducting-qubit error models across multiple QPUs.
application_angle: More faithful noise models can improve decoder training, benchmark interpretation, and near-term QEC design without requiring prohibitively large hardware datasets.

title: Thomas-Fermi equation revisited: A variation on a theme by Majorana
abstract: Majorana found a way to exploit the scaling properties of the Thomas-Fermi equation for converting this second-order differential equation into one of first order. We explore his method for the familiar neutral-atom solution and extend it to the solution that is relevant for weakly ionized atoms. Various integrals and other quantities with importance for atomic physics are recalculated and their values compared with the ones obtained in the 1980s by more tedious numerical procedures.
one_liner: Majorana's scaling reduction of the Thomas-Fermi equation is extended to weakly ionized atoms with updated atomic-physics integrals.
why_it_matters: The work sharpens a classic computational tool for atomic models, though its quantum-technology relevance is more indirect than the other selections.
detailed_summary: The paper revisits Majorana's method for exploiting scaling properties of the Thomas-Fermi equation to reduce a second-order nonlinear differential equation to first order. It treats both the standard neutral-atom solution and an extension relevant to weakly ionized atoms. Several physically relevant integrals and derived quantities are recalculated and compared against older numerical benchmarks from the 1980s. The contribution is primarily methodological, providing a cleaner and more efficient route to established atomic-structure quantities.
main_result: The main result is an extension of Majorana's reduction method that reproduces and refines neutral-atom and weakly ionized-atom Thomas-Fermi solutions.
application_angle: Improved semi-analytical atomic calculations can support faster parameter estimation in atomic-physics modeling, but the immediate device relevance is limited.

title: Junction-Intrinsic Dissipation in Hybrid Superconductor-Semiconductor Gatemon Qubits
abstract: Superconducting transmon qubits based on hybrid superconductor-semiconductor Josephson junctions (gatemons) offer gate tunability, but their relaxation times remain well below those of state-of-the-art transmons, and the origin of this discrepancy is not fully understood. Here, we co-fabricate gatemons and SIS-junction transmons with nominally identical circuit layouts, gate dielectrics, and control lines, so that the Josephson element is the only intentional distinction. Across multiple chips, transmons in this architecture reach relaxation times in the tens of microseconds, whereas gatemons saturate in the few-microsecond range. Using the transmons as on-chip references, we construct a loss budget including Purcell decay, spontaneous emission through the control line, and internal dielectric loss, and find that the corresponding T1 limits exceed all measured gatemon values by more than an order of magnitude. Temperature-dependent T1 measurements follow a common quasiparticle-activation model and yield similar superconducting gaps for S-Sm-S and SIS junctions, indicating that the reduced gatemon coherence is dominated by additional temperature-independent, junction-intrinsic dissipation.
one_liner: A controlled gatemon-versus-transmon comparison isolates temperature-independent junction-intrinsic dissipation as a dominant gatemon loss channel.
why_it_matters: Pinpointing the coherence bottleneck in gatemons is essential for making voltage-tunable superconducting qubits competitive with standard transmons.
detailed_summary: The authors co-fabricate hybrid superconductor-semiconductor gatemons and conventional SIS-junction transmons with nominally identical layouts so that the junction type is the main intentional difference. Reference transmons reach relaxation times in the tens of microseconds, while gatemons saturate at only a few microseconds across multiple chips. Using an on-chip loss budget that includes Purcell decay, control-line emission, and dielectric loss, they show those known channels cannot explain the gatemon T1 deficit. Temperature-dependent measurements fit a common quasiparticle-activation model with similar extracted gaps, indicating the limiting mechanism is additional temperature-independent dissipation intrinsic to the hybrid junction.
main_result: The main result is experimental evidence that gatemon coherence is chiefly limited by junction-intrinsic dissipation rather than standard circuit loss channels.
application_angle: This directly informs materials and junction-engineering priorities for scalable voltage-tunable superconducting qubits and integrated control architectures.

title: Quantum Sensing with Triplet Pair States: A Theoretical Study
abstract: Molecular quantum sensors represent a promising frontier for the detection of nuclear magnetic resonance signals and alternating current magnetic fields at the nanoscale, potentially reaching single-proton sensitivity. Although the triplet states of molecular pentacene provide a viable sensing architecture, the triplet pair states produced by singlet fission of pentacene dimers could enable more flexible quantum manipulations through entanglement. In this work, we model the quantum sensing efficacy of a spin-polarized quintet manifold in a photoexcited pentacene dimer generated via intramolecular singlet fission. Using a Lindblad master equation approach, we simulate the evolution of the triplet pair state under standard dynamical decoupling sequences, including spin echo, XY4, and XY8 and provide a direct performance comparison to the traditional pentacene monomer benchmark. While both architectures exhibit comparable sensitivity for isolated single-spin detection, our findings indicate that the dimer architecture provides a superior interaction cross-section for detecting small ensembles of nuclear spins. Analytical expressions derived for fluorescence modulation demonstrate that sensitivity is optimized in the low-magnetic field regime and scales with the number of pulses in the sensing protocol. This study establishes a theoretical baseline for utilizing high-spin multi-excitonic states as chemically tunable, high-sensitivity quantum probes.
one_liner: A theory study shows singlet-fission triplet-pair states in pentacene dimers can act as chemically tunable nanoscale quantum sensors.
why_it_matters: It identifies a molecular sensing architecture that can outperform pentacene monomers for small nuclear-spin ensembles while remaining competitive for single-spin detection.
detailed_summary: The paper models a spin-polarized quintet manifold generated by intramolecular singlet fission in a pentacene dimer using a Lindblad master equation. It compares spin-echo, XY4, and XY8 sensing performance against a pentacene monomer benchmark and derives analytical expressions for fluorescence modulation. The analysis finds comparable sensitivity for isolated single nuclear spins, but a larger interaction cross-section for detecting small ensembles with the dimer architecture. Sensitivity is predicted to improve in low magnetic field and to scale with pulse number in the control sequence.
main_result: Theoretical simulations predict that quintet triplet-pair states in pentacene dimers can match monomer single-spin sensitivity while improving ensemble-spin detection.
application_angle: The work points to chemically engineered molecular probes for nanoscale NMR and AC magnetometry, with potential relevance to compact single-proton-scale sensing.

title: Non-perturbative CPMG scaling and qutrit-driven breakdown under compiled superconducting-qubit control: a single-qubit study
abstract: Decoherence in superconducting qubits emerges from the interplay of multilevel dynamics and structured environmental noise, yet perturbative models cannot capture all resulting signatures. Here, EmuPlat couples instruction-set-architecture-level waveform generation to the hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) under $1/f$ non-Markovian pure dephasing. In the resulting non-perturbative regime -- where filter-function predictions become quantitatively uninformative -- CPMG scaling of a three-level superconducting transmon yields one calibration result, two physical findings, and one structural null. Y-CPMG exhibits axis-dependent scaling-law breakdown -- non-monotonic decoherence, partial coherence revival, and pronounced X--Y population asymmetry ($0.204$ vs ${<}\,0.01$) -- driven by third-level anharmonicity amplified by bath memory; X-CPMG maintains well-behaved power-law scaling with a finite-$n$ transient excess consistent with non-Markovian bath-memory effects. The structural null is equally informative: waveform-level differences -- Standard versus VPPU realizations -- remain undetectable across all coupling strengths, establishing that rotating-frame pure-dephasing coupling renders control-layer detail invisible to scaling observables. These findings define testable predictions, the most experimentally accessible requiring only qualitative verification.
one_liner: A non-perturbative transmon study predicts axis-dependent CPMG breakdown from qutrit leakage and non-Markovian bath memory.
why_it_matters: It gives experimentally testable signatures showing when standard filter-function models fail in compiled superconducting-qubit control.
detailed_summary: The authors couple ISA-level waveform generation in EmuPlat to hierarchical equations of motion under 1/f pure dephasing for a three-level transmon. In this non-perturbative regime, Y-CPMG exhibits non-monotonic decoherence, partial coherence revival, and strong X-Y population asymmetry due to third-level anharmonicity amplified by bath memory. By contrast, X-CPMG retains a power-law-like scaling with a finite-pulse transient excess consistent with memory effects. They also find a structural null result: Standard and VPPU waveform realizations are indistinguishable in these scaling observables under rotating-frame pure dephasing coupling.
main_result: The study predicts that Y-axis CPMG control can break standard scaling laws through qutrit dynamics and bath memory, whereas X-axis CPMG remains comparatively well behaved.
application_angle: These signatures can guide calibration and noise diagnosis in superconducting processors by identifying when multilevel physics invalidates perturbative control models.

title: Adiabatic Ramsey Interferometry for Measuring Weak Nonlinearities with Super-Heisenberg Precision
abstract: We propose an adiabatic Ramsey interferometry technique for detecting weak nonlinearities with trapped ions. The method relies on using the quantum Rabi model as a probe, which is sensitive to nonlinear symmetry-breaking perturbations. We show that the couplings which arise either from anharmonic terms of the trapping potential or due to higher order terms in the Coulomb interaction expansion can be efficiently estimated by measuring the spin state probabilities alone. We show that the spin signal is amplified by the mean-phonon excitations, which results in the estimation precision reaching the super-Heisenberg limit. Notably, achieving such high-precision estimation does not require specific entangled state preparation and can be reached even for initial thermal motion state. Furthermore, we show that the super-Heisenberg scaling can be observed even in the presence of weak spin-dephasing.
one_liner: Adiabatic Ramsey interferometry with trapped ions is proposed to estimate weak nonlinearities at super-Heisenberg precision.
why_it_matters: It offers a high-precision sensing route that avoids bespoke entangled-state preparation and tolerates thermal motion and weak dephasing.
detailed_summary: This work proposes using the quantum Rabi model in trapped ions as a probe for weak symmetry-breaking nonlinear perturbations arising from trap anharmonicity or higher-order Coulomb terms. The protocol is an adiabatic Ramsey interferometer in which the target parameter is inferred from spin-state probabilities alone, with signal amplification set by the mean phonon excitation number. The resulting estimation precision is predicted to reach super-Heisenberg scaling without requiring specially prepared entangled states. The analysis further indicates that this scaling survives for initially thermal motional states and under weak spin dephasing.
main_result: The proposed protocol can estimate weak ion-trap nonlinearities from spin readout alone with predicted super-Heisenberg scaling.
application_angle: It could improve calibration and characterization of trapped-ion hardware by turning intrinsic weak nonlinearities into precisely measurable signals.

title: Rapid axial loading of a grating MOT with a cold-atom beam
abstract: Laser-cooled atoms are increasingly being used to realise practical quantum devices, motivating the development of compact and robust atom sources. Grating magneto-optical traps (gMOTs) simplify the cold-atom source architecture but are typically vapour-loaded and provide limited atomic flux. Here we explore the loading of gMOTs from cold-atom beams. We numerically simulate loading to show that unbalanced diffracted beams deflect incoming atoms away from the trap centre, thereby strongly constraining radial loading. In contrast, axial loading injects atoms directly into the trapping volume and largely avoids these effects. We experimentally demonstrate rapid axial loading of a gMOT, achieving loading rates of $2.1 \times 10^9$ atoms~s$^{-1}$ using a moving optical molasses to transfer atoms from a 2D MOT into the gMOT. These results establish axial loading as a robust route to high-flux gMOT operation for portable cold-atom systems.
one_liner: Axial injection from a cold-atom beam enables high-flux, rapidly loaded grating MOTs for compact atom-based devices.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical bottleneck in portable cold-atom systems by showing a robust loading geometry with much higher usable atomic flux.
detailed_summary: The paper studies how to load a grating MOT from a cold-atom beam, combining simulation with experiment. Numerical analysis shows radial loading is strongly limited because unbalanced diffracted beams deflect atoms away from the trap center, while axial loading largely avoids this problem by injecting atoms directly into the capture region. Experimentally, the authors use moving optical molasses to transfer atoms from a 2D MOT into the gMOT and achieve a loading rate of 2.1 x 10^9 atoms per second. The result establishes axial loading as a practical high-flux operating mode for grating-based cold-atom sources.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates axial gMOT loading at 2.1 x 10^9 atoms s^-1 using transfer from a 2D MOT via moving optical molasses.
application_angle: Higher-flux compact atom sources directly support deployable quantum sensors such as portable clocks, gravimeters, and inertial systems.

title: Quantum connectivity of quantum networks
abstract: The practical utility of a quantum network depends on its ability to establish entanglement between arbitrary node pairs with quality sufficient to execute entanglement enabled tasks. This capability can be assessed globally, through aggregate performance over all node pairs, as well as locally, at the level of individual nodes. Since entanglement-based connections form a layer above the underlying physical topology, quantum connectivity is not adequately captured by classical topological connectivity metrics. To enable characterisation of the quantum connectivity at the level of the network (or its subnetworks), we introduce the quantum connectivity measure (QCM), which quantifies the average connection quality between pairs of network nodes. Further, we describe two quantities, the quantum-connected fraction (QCF) and the quantum clustering coefficient (QCC), naturally derived from the QCM, which capture important features of the functional connectivity of the quantum network at the level of the network and an individual node, respectively. These metrics of quantum connectivity depend crucially on the entanglement distribution protocol and the quantum network parameters in addition to its physical topology. We demonstrate the crucial distinction between topological and quantum connectivity, showing that even a fully connected graph can be functionally disconnected for quantum tasks if average network edge-concurrence falls below a critical threshold. These quantum connectivity metrics thus provide important tools for the design, optimization, and benchmarking of future quantum networks.
one_liner: This paper defines quantum-native connectivity metrics for judging whether a network can actually distribute useful entanglement.
why_it_matters: It provides benchmarking tools that capture functional quantum-network performance beyond classical graph connectivity alone.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce the quantum connectivity measure, which quantifies average connection quality between node pairs based on entanglement-distribution performance rather than physical topology alone. They further define the quantum-connected fraction and quantum clustering coefficient to characterize global and node-level functional connectivity. The framework explicitly depends on protocol choice and network parameters, not just graph structure. A key result is that even a fully connected graph can become functionally disconnected for quantum tasks when average edge concurrence drops below a critical threshold.
main_result: The work establishes QCM, QCF, and QCC as protocol-aware metrics for evaluating whether a quantum network can support useful entanglement across nodes.
application_angle: These metrics can be used to design, optimize, and benchmark real quantum repeater and metropolitan-scale network deployments.

title: The Manipulate-and-Observe Attack on Quantum Key Distribution
abstract: Quantum key distribution is often regarded as an unconditionally secure method to exchange a secret key by harnessing fundamental aspects of quantum mechanics. Despite the robustness of key exchange, classical post-processing reveals vulnerabilities that an eavesdropper could target. In particular, many reconciliation protocols correct errors by comparing the parities of subsets between both parties. These communications occur over insecure channels, leaking information that an eavesdropper could exploit. Currently there is no holistic threat model that addresses how parity-leakage during reconciliation might be actively manipulated. In this paper we introduce a new form of attack, namely the Manipulate-and-Observe attack in which the adversary (1) partially intercepts a fraction $ρ$ of the qubits during key exchange, injecting the maximally tolerated amount of errors up to the 11 percent error threshold whilst remaining undetected and (2) probes the maximum amount of parity-leakage during reconciliation, and exploits it using a vectorised, parallel brute force filter to shrink the search space from 2n down to as few as a single candidate, for an n-bit reconciled key. We perform simulations of the attack, deploying it on the most widely used protocol, BB84, andthe benchmark reconciliation protocol, Cascade. Our simulation results demonstrate that the attack can significantly reduce the security below the theoretical bound and, in the worst case, fully recover the reconciled key material. The principles of the attack could threaten other parity-based reconciliation schemes, like Low Density Parity Check, which underscores the need for urgent consideration of the combined security of key exchange and post-processing.
one_liner: A manipulate-and-observe attack exploits parity leakage in QKD reconciliation to drastically reduce or recover the final key.
why_it_matters: It exposes a system-level weakness between quantum transmission and classical post-processing that standard QKD security narratives often treat separately.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a two-stage attack in which an adversary first partially intercepts qubits and injects errors up to the tolerated 11% threshold, then actively exploits parity information leaked during reconciliation. Using BB84 with Cascade as a benchmark, the attacker applies a vectorized parallel brute-force filter to shrink the key search space from 2^n to as few as one candidate for an n-bit reconciled key. Simulations show that the attack can substantially reduce practical security and, in worst cases, fully recover reconciled key material. The authors argue that similar risks may extend to other parity-based reconciliation methods such as LDPC-style schemes.
main_result: Simulations indicate that combined quantum-channel manipulation and reconciliation-side parity exploitation can in the worst case fully recover the reconciled QKD key.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to deployed QKD stacks because it motivates end-to-end security analysis of post-processing, not just the quantum exchange layer.

title: Gaussian sample model in in-line imaging
abstract: We investigate the gain in Shannon information that can be extracted from an X-ray image obtained after coherent free-space propagation of the transmitted beam and subsequent digital processing of the detected image. We show that simulated digital forward free-space propagation can produce a much higher formal information gain, both in projection imaging and in phase-contrast computed tomography, compared to conventional phase retrieval based on the Transport of Intensity equation. However, it appears that the extra information gained in the simulated free-space propagation may be due in part to superficial high-frequency content in the obtained images, rather than due to a genuine improvement of the spatial resolution. This points to the need to critically evaluate the performance of different types of image quality metrics and their relationship to the information content of the images.
one_liner: Analyzes how digital forward propagation changes information content in coherent X-ray in-line imaging and phase-contrast CT.
why_it_matters: It sharpens how quantum/coherent imaging gains should be judged, warning that apparent detail boosts may reflect artifacts rather than true resolution.
detailed_summary: The paper studies coherent in-line X-ray imaging after free-space propagation and compares digitally simulated forward propagation against transport-of-intensity-equation phase retrieval. Using Shannon information as a metric, the authors find substantially larger formal information gain in both projection imaging and phase-contrast computed tomography. They then show that part of this gain can come from boosted high-frequency structure that does not correspond to genuine spatial-resolution improvement. The work therefore focuses on the mismatch between image-quality metrics and physically meaningful information extraction in coherent imaging pipelines.
main_result: Simulated digital forward free-space propagation yields higher formal information gain than TIE-based retrieval, but some of that gain likely reflects superficial high-frequency content.
application_angle: This is relevant for practical coherent imaging and tomography because it guides which reconstruction metrics should be trusted when optimizing real diagnostic or sensing systems.

title: Optimal Control of Spin Squeezing in 2D Finite-Range Interacting Systems
abstract: Spin squeezing serves as both a fundamental witness of quantum entanglement and a critical resource for quantum-enhanced metrology. While generating substantial spin squeezing in finite-range interacting systems remains challenging, such capability is important for advancing quantum technologies. In this work, we develop an optimal control strategy for achieving enhanced spin squeezing in a two-dimensional XX model with dipolar interactions. Leveraging rotor-spin-wave theory for periodic boundary conditions, we circumvent computational bottlenecks to explore control strategies at unprecedented scales. Remarkably, optimizing a single collective transverse field is sufficient to achieve substantial squeezing enhancement, exceeding the two-axis-twisting benchmark. The optimized control field achieves this breakthrough by dynamically suppressing inter-subspace mixing induced by the finite-range interactions, thereby confining the system evolution predominantly within the maximal spin subspace. We further extend rotor-spin-wave theory to open boundary conditions and incorporate dephasing noise, providing a scalable framework for realistic systems. Under these conditions, the optimized protocol remains effective, highlighting its robustness and suitability for experimental implementation.
one_liner: Uses optimal control to generate strong spin squeezing in a 2D dipolar XX model with a single global transverse field.
why_it_matters: It provides a scalable, noise-aware route to metrologically useful entanglement in realistic finite-range spin platforms.
detailed_summary: The authors develop an optimal-control protocol for spin squeezing in a two-dimensional XX model with finite-range dipolar interactions. By combining the control search with rotor-spin-wave theory, they analyze large systems under periodic and open boundary conditions while including dephasing noise. A single collective transverse control field is sufficient to outperform the conventional two-axis-twisting benchmark. The mechanism is suppression of interaction-induced mixing out of the maximal-spin manifold, keeping the dynamics in the subspace that supports strong squeezing.
main_result: An optimized global transverse field robustly produces enhanced spin squeezing beyond the two-axis-twisting benchmark even with open boundaries and dephasing.
application_angle: The protocol is directly relevant to experimental quantum sensors and simulators that need simple global controls to create entangled states for precision measurements.

title: Strongly Nonlinear Slow Light Polaritons in Subwavelength Modulated Waveguides
abstract: Slow light is a regime of reduced group velocity, resulting in increased photon density in optical pulses and enhanced nonlinear effects. Here, we propose the realization of slow light in the regime of strong light-matter interaction between waveguide photons and semiconductor excitons. We design a dielectric superlattice structure with a nearly-flat band characterized by low group velocity and group velocity dispersion, both required for enhancing nonlinear effects with ultrashort pulses. Furthermore, by applying this general framework to a perovskite-based structure, we demonstrate an enhancement of the single-particle phase shift by a factor of more than 20, representing a significant step toward the few-photon quantum regime. Our results provide a blueprint for accessible strong interactions in solid-state integrated optics.
one_liner: Designs subwavelength modulated waveguides that create slow-light exciton-polaritons with strongly enhanced optical nonlinearity.
why_it_matters: It points to integrated solid-state devices where few-photon phase shifts become more accessible for quantum photonic processing.
detailed_summary: This work proposes slow-light operation in the strong-coupling regime between waveguide photons and semiconductor excitons using a dielectric superlattice waveguide. The engineered bandstructure is nearly flat, simultaneously reducing group velocity and group-velocity dispersion to support ultrashort pulses with enhanced nonlinear interaction. In a perovskite-based implementation, the authors estimate more than a 20-fold increase in the single-particle phase shift. The result is a concrete integrated-optics blueprint for pushing solid-state photonics toward the few-photon nonlinear regime.
main_result: A subwavelength-modulated polaritonic waveguide can realize slow light with low dispersion and over 20x enhancement of the single-particle phase shift.
application_angle: This could enable compact chip-scale nonlinear quantum photonic elements such as phase shifters, switches, and gates operating at very low photon numbers.

title: Weak-Field Expansion: A Time-Closed Solution of Quantum Three-Wave Mixing
abstract: We present a systematic derivation of the Heisenberg evolution of a trilinear bosonic Hamiltonian system in presence of a strong drive beyond the standard approximation of a classical, undepleted driving field. We employ a perturbative expansion of the Hamiltonian propagator in orders of the input field amplitudes, as opposed to the standard Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff (BCH) expansion of the propagator in orders of time. Our method automatically provides time-closed expressions; and converges considerably faster than BCH, especially in the regime of high parametric gain because the small parameter it uses is natural to the problem. We obtain the well-known quantum solution for optical parametric amplification of down-conversion simply as the first order of the expansion, and present the rigorous procedure to derive higher order corrections one by one. To demonstrate the utility of higher corrections, we discuss the 2nd order correction to the pump field as an ideal detector of time-energy entanglement in parametric down-conversion. We also use the 3rd order correction to calculate the limits on the fidelity of quantum state-transfer from one optical mode to another using sum/difference frequency generation, due to the quantum properties of the strong driving field.
one_liner: Introduces a weak-field, time-closed expansion for quantum three-wave mixing beyond the undepleted classical pump approximation.
why_it_matters: It gives a more practical theory tool for modeling parametric devices when pump quantum effects matter for fidelity and entanglement readout.
detailed_summary: The paper derives Heisenberg-picture evolution for a trilinear bosonic Hamiltonian driven strongly but treated beyond the usual classical undepleted-pump approximation. Instead of expanding in time via Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff, it expands the propagator in orders of input field amplitudes, yielding time-closed expressions that converge faster in high-gain regimes. First order reproduces the standard optical parametric amplification solution, while higher orders capture pump corrections. The second-order pump term is proposed as an ideal probe of time-energy entanglement, and the third-order term quantifies fidelity limits in frequency-conversion-based quantum state transfer.
main_result: The weak-field propagator expansion provides a fast-converging, time-closed solution for three-wave mixing and exposes higher-order pump-induced limits on entanglement probing and state transfer.
application_angle: The method is useful for designing realistic nonlinear quantum photonic hardware where pump depletion and pump quantum noise affect conversion efficiency and transfer fidelity.

title: Four Generations of Quantum Biomedical Sensors
abstract: Quantum sensing technologies offer transformative potential for ultra-sensitive biomedical sensing, yet their clinical translation remains constrained by classical noise limits and a reliance on macroscopic ensembles. We propose a unifying generational framework to organize the evolving landscape of quantum biosensors based on their utilization of quantum resources. First-generation devices utilize discrete energy levels for signal transduction but follow classical scaling laws. Second-generation sensors exploit quantum coherence to reach the standard quantum limit, while third-generation architectures leverage entanglement and spin squeezing to approach Heisenberg-limited precision. We further define an emerging fourth generation characterized by the end-to-end integration of quantum sensing with quantum learning and variational circuits, enabling adaptive inference directly within the quantum domain. By analyzing critical parameters such as bandwidth matching and sensor-tissue proximity, we identify key technological bottlenecks and propose a roadmap for transitioning from measuring physical observables to extracting structured biological information with quantum-enhanced intelligence.
one_liner: Proposes a four-generation framework that organizes quantum biomedical sensors from classical scaling to quantum-native adaptive inference.
why_it_matters: It turns a fragmented field into a practical roadmap for translating quantum sensing concepts into clinically useful devices.
detailed_summary: This perspective classifies quantum biomedical sensors into four generations based on how deeply they exploit quantum resources. The framework runs from devices using discrete quantum transitions but classical scaling, through coherence-limited and entanglement-enabled sensors, to a fourth generation coupling sensing with quantum learning and variational inference. The authors emphasize engineering bottlenecks including bandwidth matching and sensor-tissue proximity rather than only ideal sensitivity scaling. The paper therefore reframes the field around end-to-end information extraction from biological systems instead of isolated physical measurement performance.
main_result: The work defines a four-generation taxonomy and identifies concrete translation bottlenecks linking quantum resources, sensing hardware, and biomedical inference.
application_angle: It provides an application-facing roadmap for building clinically relevant quantum biosensors that integrate sensing, data processing, and adaptive decision-making.

title: High-fidelity entangled photon pairs from a quantum-dot-based single-photon source
abstract: Entangled photon pairs are a ubiquitous resource in quantum technologies, used in quantum key distribution and quantum networking as well as fundamental tests of non-locality. For scalable quantum networks, pairs that are indistinguishable in all unentangled degrees of freedom are essential, as they enable high-fidelity entanglement swapping across network nodes. To date the most-studied sources of "swappable" entangled photon pairs have been based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) in non-linear crystals. However, the probabilistic nature and unavoidable trade-off between brightness and unwanted multi-photon emission limits their performance in lossy channels. Here, we demonstrate a high-fidelity source of "swappable" entangled photon pairs using a semiconductor quantum dot (QD) coupled to a tunable microcavity. By actively modulating the QD emission between orthogonal polarisation states, delaying one path in a low-loss Herriott cell, and recombining the two on a balanced beam splitter, we generate entangled photon pairs with a fidelity of $96.1\pm0.5$ %. We identify and mitigate fidelity-limiting factors, achieving a maximum fidelity of $98.1\pm0.5$ % through time-resolved post-selection. The scheme suppresses residual multi-photon events concentrated near the excitation pulse and has only a modest impact on the rate. Furthermore, the photons are mutually indistinguishable, enabling efficient entanglement swapping. Our results establish semiconductor QDs as a viable platform for quantum network-compatible swappable entangled photon pair generation, with feasible entanglement generation rates exceeding 0.5 Gpairs/s.
one_liner: Demonstrates high-fidelity, swappable entangled photon pairs from a quantum dot in a tunable microcavity with strong indistinguishability.
