why_it_matters: It advances deterministic semiconductor sources toward quantum-network deployment where entanglement swapping and low multiphoton noise are essential.
detailed_summary: The authors build an entangled-pair source from a semiconductor quantum dot coupled to a tunable microcavity, using active polarization modulation, a low-loss Herriott-cell delay, and balanced recombination. The device produces swappable entangled photon pairs with 96.1 +/- 0.5% fidelity, rising to 98.1 +/- 0.5% with time-resolved post-selection. Residual multiphoton events near the excitation pulse are suppressed with only modest rate penalty, and the photons remain mutually indistinguishable for entanglement swapping. The architecture targets feasible entanglement generation rates above 0.5 Gpairs/s, addressing a key gap between probabilistic SPDC and scalable network nodes.
platform_or_system: quantum dot in a tunable microcavity
main_result: A semiconductor quantum-dot source generates highly indistinguishable swappable entangled photon pairs with up to 98.1% fidelity and projected rates exceeding 0.5 Gpairs/s.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to deployable quantum repeaters and network nodes because it offers a bright, low-noise solid-state source compatible with high-fidelity entanglement swapping.

title: Noise Inference by Recycling Test Rounds in Verification Protocols
abstract: Interactive verification protocols for quantum computations allow to build trust between a client and a service provider, ensuring the former that the instructed computation was carried out faithfully. They come in two variants, one without quantum communication that requires large overhead on the server side to coherently implement quantum-resistant cryptographic primitives, and one with quantum communication but with repetition as the only overhead on the service provider's side. Given the limited number of available qubits on current machines, only quantum communication-based protocols have yielded proof of concepts. In this work, we show that the repetition overhead of protocols with quantum communication can be further mitigated if one examines the task of operating a quantum machine from the service provider's point of view. Indeed, we show that the test rounds data, whose collection is necessary to provide security, can indeed be recycled to perform continuous monitoring of noise model parameters for the service provider. This exemplifies the versatility of these protocols, whose template can serve multiple purposes and increases the interest in considering their early integration into development roadmaps of quantum machines.
one_liner: This work reuses verification test rounds to continuously infer hardware noise while still supporting secure quantum-computation verification.
why_it_matters: It turns verification overhead into operational diagnostics, making quantum-communication-based verification more useful on near-term machines with limited qubits.
detailed_summary: The paper studies interactive verification protocols with quantum communication and shows that the mandatory test-round data can be repurposed to estimate noise-model parameters from the provider side. Rather than treating test rounds purely as security overhead, the authors frame them as a stream for continuous hardware monitoring. This creates a dual-use protocol layer that preserves verification while extracting calibration-relevant information about machine drift and noise. The result is especially relevant for early quantum services where qubit counts are too constrained to absorb unnecessary repetition overhead.
main_result: Test-round data in quantum-communication verification protocols can be recycled to continuously monitor hardware noise parameters, mitigating the effective repetition overhead.
application_angle: The approach makes verified cloud quantum computing more deployable by coupling trust guarantees with built-in device diagnostics and performance tracking.

title: LO-Free Phase and Amplitude Recovery of an RF Signal with a DC-Stark-Enabled Rydberg Receiver
abstract: We present a theoretical framework for recovering the amplitude and carrier phase of a single received RF field with a Rydberg-atom receiver, without injecting an RF local oscillator (LO) into the atoms. The key enabling mechanism is a static DC bias applied to the vapor cell: by Stark-mixing a near-degenerate Rydberg pair, the bias activates an otherwise absent upper optical pathway and closes a phase-sensitive loop within a receiver driven only by the standard probe/coupling pair and the received RF field. For a spatially uniform bias, we derive an effective four-level rotating-frame Hamiltonian of Floquet form and show that the periodic steady state obeys an exact harmonic phase law, so that the $n$th probe harmonic carries the factor $e^{inΦ_S}$. This yields direct estimators for the signal phase and amplitude from a demodulated probe harmonic, with amplitude recovery obtained by inverting an injective harmonic response map. In the high-SNR regime, we derive explicit RMSE laws and use them to identify distinct phase-optimal and amplitude-optimal bias-controlled mixing angles, together with a weighted joint-design criterion and a balanced compromise angle that equalizes the fractional phase and amplitude penalties. We then extend the analysis to nonuniform DC bias through quasistatic spatial averaging and show that bias inhomogeneity reduces coherent gain for phase readout while also reshaping the amplitude-response slope. Numerical examples validate the phase law, illustrate response-map inversion and mixing-angle trade-offs, and quantify the penalties induced by bias nonuniformity. The results establish a minimal route to coherent Rydberg reception of a single RF signal without an auxiliary RF LO in the atoms.
one_liner: A DC-Stark-enabled Rydberg receiver can recover RF amplitude and carrier phase without injecting an RF local oscillator into the atoms.
why_it_matters: Removing the atomic RF LO simplifies coherent Rydberg receivers and could reduce hardware complexity for compact atom-based RF sensing and communications.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a theoretical model for a vapor-cell Rydberg receiver where a static DC bias Stark-mixes a near-degenerate Rydberg pair and closes a phase-sensitive loop using only the probe, coupling, and received RF field. In the resulting effective four-level Floquet Hamiltonian, the nth probe harmonic acquires an exact phase factor e^{inPhi_S}, enabling direct carrier-phase estimation and amplitude recovery through inversion of a harmonic response map. They derive high-SNR RMSE expressions, identify bias-controlled operating points that separately optimize phase and amplitude readout, and quantify the penalties from spatially nonuniform DC bias. Numerical examples confirm the harmonic phase law and the gain-loss tradeoffs under bias inhomogeneity.
main_result: The work establishes a local-oscillator-free scheme for coherent single-RF-signal phase and amplitude recovery using DC-Stark-enabled Rydberg sensing.
application_angle: It points to simpler atom-based RF front ends for spectrum sensing, coherent demodulation, and secure wireless links without an injected RF reference.

title: Quantum memory on a nanophotonic silicon chip
abstract: Integrated photonic circuits offer great promise for quantum technologies. However, due to the rapid propagation of light, many envisioned applications require efficient on-chip quantum memories with a programmable delay, compact footprint, and high fidelity. Implementing this based on standard semiconductor processing technology is an outstanding challenge. Here, we realize such memories using erbium-doped silicon waveguides, fabricated as part of a multi-wafer project by a nanophotonic foundry. We demonstrate light storage with a $44.2(9)\ \text{MHz}$ bandwidth and a programmable delay exceeding $1\ μ\text{s}$ in a device with a footprint of only $1.5\times 10^{-2}\ \text{mm}^2$, outperforming on-chip delay lines by many orders of magnitude. The phase of the read-out light field is preserved with a visibility of $91.3(30)\ \%$. The efficiency of $1.89(28)\times 10^{-8}$ can be improved in future devices through resonator enhancement and higher dopant concentrations. With this, the demonstrated approach will pave the way towards applications in photonic quantum computing based on scalable silicon processing technology.
one_liner: Erbium-doped silicon waveguides fabricated in a foundry process realize an on-chip quantum memory with microsecond-scale programmable delay.
why_it_matters: This is a concrete route to compact, phase-preserving quantum memory in standard silicon photonics, a missing ingredient for scalable photonic quantum systems.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates quantum light storage in erbium-doped silicon waveguides produced through a nanophotonic foundry process, targeting compatibility with scalable semiconductor fabrication. The device achieves 44.2(9) MHz storage bandwidth, programmable delay beyond 1 microsecond, and an ultra-small footprint of 1.5x10^-2 mm^2, greatly exceeding conventional on-chip delay-line performance. Readout preserves optical phase with 91.3(30)% visibility, indicating coherent storage, although the present efficiency is low at 1.89(28)x10^-8. The authors argue that resonator enhancement and higher dopant concentration can substantially improve efficiency in future generations.
main_result: They realize a foundry-fabricated silicon-chip quantum memory with >1 microsecond programmable delay, 44.2 MHz bandwidth, and coherent phase-preserving readout.
application_angle: Such memories are directly relevant to photonic quantum processors and chip-scale quantum networks that need synchronization, buffering, and feedforward-compatible delays.

title: Bent optical waveguide finite element analysis with a 3D envelope Maxwell model
abstract: With the goal of accurately extracting the optical field losses in a three-dimensional (3D), circularly coiled waveguide (e.g., bent optical fiber), this effort presents the numerical methodologies that are implemented for an envelope Maxwell model that propagates electromagnetic fields as an entirely boundary value problem. Our unique modeling approach includes an ultraweak variational formulation of the envelope Maxwell model in the curved geometry of the bending, which is discretized by the discontinuous Petrov-Galerkin (DPG) method, which permits residual-driven mesh and polynomial-order adaptivity. This also, then, requires a unique approach for constructing perfectly matched layers (PMLs) as absorbing boundary conditions in both the direction of optical field propagation and in the tangential directions, where unguided energy escapes the waveguide. Our coiled waveguide modeling technology extracts the mode confinement losses from the propagation of the coherent optical field through the bent waveguide. We verify our simulations against the semi-analytical results from the analogous bent slab waveguide problem, and we successfully demonstrate stable convergence to loss values for the 3D coiled optical fiber problem, which has never been done previously for our specific modeling approach.
one_liner: A 3D envelope-Maxwell finite-element framework accurately extracts bend loss in circularly coiled optical waveguides using adaptive DPG methods.
why_it_matters: Reliable bend-loss modeling is essential for dense photonic integration, where compact routing and tight coils strongly affect device yield and system performance.
detailed_summary: This work formulates an ultraweak variational version of the envelope Maxwell equations in curved coordinates for three-dimensional bent and coiled waveguides. The discretization uses the discontinuous Petrov-Galerkin method with residual-driven mesh and polynomial adaptivity, paired with specially constructed perfectly matched layers in both propagation and tangential leakage directions. The framework is benchmarked against semi-analytical bent slab results and then applied to a fully 3D coiled optical fiber geometry. The simulations converge stably to confinement-loss values, addressing a difficult regime for previous implementations of this modeling approach.
platform_or_system: 3D bent optical waveguides and coiled fibers
main_result: The authors demonstrate stable, convergent extraction of optical confinement loss in fully 3D circularly bent waveguides with an adaptive envelope-Maxwell solver.
application_angle: The model can guide compact photonic-circuit and fiber-package design by predicting curvature-induced loss before fabrication.

title: Building Block For Universal Continuous Variables Computation In Superconducting Devices
abstract: Continuous variable (CV) quantum computation offers an alternative to qubit-based computing by exploiting the infinite-dimensional Hilbert space of bosonic modes. Despite recent progress, superconducting platforms have yet to demonstrate a scalable architecture capable of universal computation. Here, we design and numerically simulate a two-layer superconducting architecture that implements all five interactions of the universal CV gate set (rotation, displacement, squeezing, Kerr, and beam splitter) within experimentally accessible regimes. To this end, we employ a DC-SQUID as the bosonic mode, a fluxonium qubit to mediate nonlinear interactions, and two ancillary qubits that enable Gaussian and multi-mode operations. By tuning fluxes and frequencies, we achieve high fidelities ($\geq 98\%$) across all gates within state-of-the-art parameter ranges. The modular nature of the design allows straightforward scaling, establishing a feasible pathway toward high-fidelity, universal CV quantum computation based on superconducting circuits.
one_liner: A two-layer superconducting architecture numerically realizes the full universal continuous-variable gate set with at least 98% fidelity.
why_it_matters: Universal CV processing in superconducting hardware would open a scalable bosonic-computing path beyond standard qubit-only architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a modular superconducting design where a DC-SQUID hosts the bosonic mode, a fluxonium mediates nonlinear interactions, and two ancillary qubits enable Gaussian and multimode operations. By tuning flux biases and transition frequencies, the scheme implements rotation, displacement, squeezing, Kerr, and beam-splitter interactions, covering the standard universal CV gate set. Numerical simulations report fidelities of at least 98% for all gates under experimentally plausible parameters. The architecture is organized as a scalable building block aimed at larger bosonic processors rather than a one-off gate demonstration.
platform_or_system: superconducting bosonic circuit with DC-SQUID and fluxonium
main_result: The design achieves simulated high-fidelity implementation of all five universal continuous-variable gates in a superconducting platform.
application_angle: It provides a practical hardware blueprint for scalable bosonic quantum processors that could support error correction, simulation, and CV-native algorithms.

title: A Security-Aware Nonlinearity Study of FPGA-Based Time-to-Digital Converters for Quantum Key Distribution Systems
abstract: Intrinsic nonlinearity in FPGA-based time-to-digital converters (TDCs) is often treated as a calibration issue and evaluated mainly through post-correction metrics. In quantum key distribution (QKD), however, raw delay-line nonuniformity can affect coincidence timing and thereby influence accidental-coincidence rate and Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER). This paper analyzes how measured FPGA-TDC nonlinearity propagates to QKD timing metrics using a conservative system-level model that combines random timing uncertainty and deterministic nonlinearity. We also propose fabric-level mitigation strategies based on LUT-assisted delay shaping and placement constraints to reduce severe bin-width irregularities without statistical calibrations. The method is evaluated by reproducing two open-source TDCs implemented on a low-cost Zynq-7000 FPGA. We observe reductions of 14\%-21\% in integral nonlinearity (INL) compared with the non-optimized design, leading to a reduced QBER contribution and an improvement by 3.7\%-14.2\% in the estimated secret fraction. These results suggest that raw FPGA-TDC nonlinearity deserves explicit consideration in timing-sensitive QKD implementations.
one_liner: This study links raw FPGA TDC nonlinearity to QKD timing errors and shows fabric-level mitigation can improve the estimated secret fraction.
why_it_matters: It treats timing nonlinearity as a security-relevant hardware issue, not just a calibration nuisance, for low-cost practical QKD systems.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze how deterministic bin-width nonuniformity in FPGA-based time-to-digital converters propagates into coincidence timing, accidental counts, and QBER in quantum key distribution. Using measured nonlinearity from two open-source TDCs on a Zynq-7000 FPGA, they combine random timing jitter with deterministic delay-line errors in a conservative system-level model. They then propose LUT-assisted delay shaping and placement constraints that reduce integral nonlinearity by 14%-21% relative to non-optimized designs. In the modeled QKD setting, these hardware-level improvements translate into a 3.7%-14.2% increase in estimated secret fraction.
main_result: Fabric-level optimization of low-cost FPGA TDCs reduces nonlinearity and lowers the modeled QKD penalty, improving the estimated secret fraction.
application_angle: The work is directly relevant to deployable QKD receivers, where cheap digital timing hardware must be engineered with security and key-rate impact in mind.

title: Directly visualizing the energy level structure of quantum dot molecules
abstract: The orbital, spin and valley degrees of freedom in silicon quantum dots support many modes of spin qubit operation. However, it is generally challenging to obtain information about the energy level spectrum over large ranges of parameter space. We demonstrate a form of spectroscopy that is capable of mapping the energy level structure of a double quantum dot as a function of level detuning, interdot tunnel coupling, and magnetic field. In the one electron regime, we directly observe the transition from the atom like energy levels of isolated quantum dots to molecular like bonding and anti bonding states with increasing interdot tunnel coupling. We also resolve the Zeeman splitting of ground and excited valley states in a magnetic field. In the two electron regime, we gain access to the detuning dependent singlet triplet splitting. Our work may be extended to a broader class of systems, such as strong spin-orbit materials or proximitized quantum dots, allowing the direct extraction of various energy gaps.
one_liner: A spectroscopy method maps silicon double-quantum-dot molecular, valley, and spin levels across detuning, tunnel coupling, and magnetic field.
why_it_matters: It gives direct access to qubit-relevant energy gaps over broad tuning space, improving calibration and design of silicon spin-qubit devices.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates spectroscopy of a silicon double quantum dot that resolves the energy spectrum versus detuning, interdot tunnel coupling, and magnetic field. In the one-electron regime, it tracks the crossover from isolated-dot atomic-like levels to bonding and antibonding molecular states as tunnel coupling increases. It also resolves Zeeman splitting for ground and excited valley states, while in the two-electron regime it extracts the detuning-dependent singlet-triplet splitting. The approach is positioned as a general tool for directly reading out energy gaps in more complex qubit materials and proximitized dot systems.
main_result: The work directly visualizes molecular-state formation, valley-spin Zeeman structure, and singlet-triplet splitting in a tunable silicon double quantum dot.
application_angle: This provides an experimentally practical route to faster qubit tuning, device characterization, and Hamiltonian extraction in semiconductor quantum processors.

title: Characterization of non-classical particle propagation using superpositions of position and momentum
abstract: The uncertainty principle suggests a quantitative trade-off between the control of position and the control of momentum in particle propagation. However, a superposition of two states with very different uncertainty trade-offs introduces an interference term that seems to combine precise statements about position and about momentum, allowing us to study how quantum mechanics describes the propagation of individual particles in free space. Here, we present a detailed experimental study of photons prepared in a superposition of position and momentum generated in a Sagnac interferometer. The transverse distribution of photons was obtained with three different measurement settings at the output port of the interferometer, corresponding to the initial position distribution, the initial momentum distribution, and an intermediate propagation time at which the contributions of initial position and momentum uncertainties are approximately equal to each other. We show that the interference effect localizes the photons in narrow intervals of position and momentum, resulting in a quantitative violation of Newton's first law as the interference pattern spreads out at the intermediate position. The data obtained can be used to demonstrate the negativity of the Wigner function in regions outside the position and momentum intervals in which the position and momentum contributions are confined.
one_liner: A photon interferometry experiment probes nonclassical free-space propagation using superposed position- and momentum-localized states.
why_it_matters: It experimentally characterizes propagation signatures tied to Wigner-function negativity, relevant for validating nonclassical photonic state engineering.
detailed_summary: Photons are prepared in a Sagnac interferometer in superpositions that combine sharply different position and momentum uncertainty trade-offs. The experiment measures transverse distributions corresponding to initial position, initial momentum, and an intermediate propagation plane where both contributions are comparable. The observed interference localizes photons within narrow position and momentum intervals while producing a broadened intermediate pattern, enabling quantitative analysis beyond classical free-particle intuition. The measured data can be used to infer Wigner-function negativity outside the confined intervals.
main_result: The experiment shows nonclassical propagation behavior from position-momentum superpositions and links the measured distributions to Wigner-function negativity.
application_angle: Such propagation-sensitive diagnostics can support verification of nonclassical photonic states for communication, metrology, and quantum optical signal processing.

title: 1-Mbps Twin-Field Quantum Key Distribution over 200 km Using Independent Dissipative Kerr Solitons
abstract: Twin-field quantum key distribution (TF-QKD) dramatically enhances the secure key rate (SKR) over inter-city distances through its square-root scaling. Further improvements in aggregate SKR can be achieved by wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) of parallel QKD channels. However, direct implementation in TF-QKD poses significant challenges, as each wavelength channel requires an independent ultra-stable seed laser, narrow-linewidth transmitters, and optical phase-locked loops (OPLLs), which are not easily scalable. Here, we circumvent these limitations by employing two independent, integrated dissipative Kerr soliton (DKS) microcombs at Alice and Bob as multi-wavelength sources. High-visibility single-photon interference across all wavelength channels is achieved by stabilizing the frequencies of every comb line - requiring only the stabilization of the pump wavelength and repetition rates of the two microcombs. Based on this architecture, we perform a full TF-QKD experiment using the sending-or-not-sending protocol, achieving a total SKR of 1.57 Mbps over 201.1 km of fiber using 16 DWDM channels. This result represents more than an order-of-magnitude enhancement compared with single-wavelength TF-QKD at the same distance. Given that a single DKS comb can support over 100 coherent lines across the C-band, this approach offers a scalable pathway toward high-rate quantum key distribution over inter-city distances.
one_liner: Independent Kerr microcombs enable 16-channel twin-field QKD with a total secure key rate of 1.57 Mbps over 201.1 km fiber.
why_it_matters: It shows a scalable path to high-rate inter-city TF-QKD by replacing many ultrastable lasers with integrated comb sources.
detailed_summary: The authors implement sending-or-not-sending twin-field QKD using two independent integrated dissipative Kerr soliton microcombs as multiwavelength sources at Alice and Bob. By stabilizing pump wavelength and comb repetition rates, they achieve high-visibility single-photon interference across all wavelength channels without requiring independent ultra-stable seed lasers and OPLLs per channel. In a full experiment over 201.1 km of fiber, 16 DWDM channels deliver an aggregate secure key rate of 1.57 Mbps, more than an order of magnitude above single-wavelength TF-QKD at the same distance. The architecture is inherently scalable because a single DKS comb can provide many coherent C-band lines.
main_result: The system achieves 1.57 Mbps total secure key rate over 201.1 km using 16 wavelength-multiplexed twin-field QKD channels from independent microcombs.
application_angle: This directly advances deployable metropolitan and inter-city quantum networks by combining integrated photonics with high-throughput secure-key distribution.

title: Improvement of entanglement generation rate in frequency-multiplexed quantum repeaters using cavity-enhanced SPDC source
abstract: High-rate entanglement generation is essential for the realization of practical quantum repeaters. To this end, frequency multiplexing of the photons employed is an effective approach. In particular, schemes using cavity-enhanced spontaneous parametric down-conversion (cSPDC) as a photon-pair source have been proposed. In this study, toward a theoretical performance evaluation of frequency-multiplexed quantum repeaters based on cSPDC, we derive an approximate expression for the quantum state of the frequency-multiplexed photons, where each frequency mode is treated as an independent two-mode squeezed vacuum (TMSV) state. Using this expression, we calculate the heralding probability and fidelity of entanglement generation for several cases in a single-photon interference scheme using frequency multiplexing with cSPDC. Our results demonstrate that by multiplexing approximately 100 modes, the heralding probability improves to approximately 98% for an elementary link distance $L_\mathrm{EL}=25 \mathrm{km}$, even in scenarios where the fidelity exceeds 0.9 across all modes. Furthermore, for $L_\mathrm{EL}=100 \mathrm{km}$, we show that the heralding probability, which was approximately 0.7% in the single-mode case, increases to about 38% under the condition that the fidelity remains 0.9 or higher. These analytical results demonstrate the effectiveness of employing cSPDC as a photon-pair source for quantum repeaters.
one_liner: A cSPDC-based frequency-multiplexed repeater analysis predicts near-unity heralding over short links and large gains at 100 km.
why_it_matters: It quantifies how cavity-enhanced SPDC and frequency multiplexing can dramatically raise repeater entanglement rates while maintaining usable fidelity.
detailed_summary: This work derives an approximate quantum-state model for frequency-multiplexed photons generated by cavity-enhanced SPDC, treating each frequency bin as an independent two-mode squeezed-vacuum mode. Using that model, it computes heralding probabilities and entanglement fidelities for single-photon-interference repeater links under multiplexing. The analysis predicts that multiplexing about 100 modes can raise heralding probability to about 98% at 25 km while keeping fidelity above 0.9 across all modes. For 100 km elementary links, the same framework increases heralding from roughly 0.7% in the single-mode case to about 38% at fidelity 0.9 or better.
main_result: The paper shows analytically that frequency multiplexing with cSPDC can boost entanglement-generation heralding by orders of magnitude while preserving high fidelity.
application_angle: These results provide concrete performance targets for building practical, high-rate quantum repeater links with multiplexed photonic sources.

title: Quantum-Safe Code Auditing: LLM-Assisted Static Analysis and Quantum-Aware Risk Scoring for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
abstract: The impending arrival of cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs) threatens the security foundations of modern software: Shor's algorithm breaks RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, and Diffie-Hellman, while Grover's algorithm reduces the effective security of symmetric and hash-based schemes. Despite NIST standardising post-quantum cryptography (PQC) in 2024 (FIPS 203 ML-KEM, FIPS 204 ML-DSA, FIPS 205 SLH-DSA), most codebases lack automated tooling to inventory classical cryptographic usage and prioritise migration based on quantum risk. We present Quantum-Safe Code Auditor, a quantum-aware static analysis framework that combines (i) regex-based detection of 15 classes of quantum-vulnerable primitives, (ii) LLM-assisted contextual enrichment to classify usage and severity, and (iii) risk scoring via a Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) model implemented in Qiskit 2.x, incorporating qubit-cost estimates to prioritise findings. We evaluate the system across five open-source libraries -- python-rsa, python-ecdsa, python-jose, node-jsonwebtoken, and Bouncy Castle Java -- covering 5,775 findings. On a stratified sample of 602 labelled instances, we achieve 71.98% precision, 100% recall, and an F1 score of 83.71%. All code, data, and reproduction scripts are released as open-source.
one_liner: An automated auditor finds quantum-vulnerable crypto in software and prioritizes migration with LLM context and quantum-aware scoring.
why_it_matters: It turns post-quantum migration into an actionable engineering workflow by combining code discovery, contextual triage, and risk prioritization.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces Quantum-Safe Code Auditor, a static-analysis framework for identifying quantum-vulnerable cryptographic usage in real codebases. It combines regex detection over 15 primitive classes, LLM-based contextual enrichment for usage classification and severity, and a VQE-based quantum-aware risk model in Qiskit 2.x that incorporates qubit-cost estimates. Evaluated on five open-source libraries, the system reports 5,775 findings and reaches 71.98% precision, 100% recall, and 83.71% F1 on a stratified labeled sample of 602 instances. The authors release code, data, and reproduction scripts as open source.
main_result: The framework automatically inventories vulnerable cryptography and prioritizes remediation with high recall across multiple real-world libraries.
application_angle: It is directly useful for organizations planning post-quantum migration of communication and authentication software stacks under limited engineering bandwidth.

title: Decay of the survival probability of a local excitation in multi-qubit platforms
abstract: We present a theoretical study of the survival probability of a state initially prepared in the one-particle sector of a multi-qubit system. The motivation for our work is the ongoing laboratory development of multi-qubit platforms based on superconducting circuits. Using elementary concepts of random matrix theory, we obtain analytic expressions for the survival probability in mathematical models of platforms which, albeit stylized, have been previously shown to provide relevant benchmarks for experimental data. In particular, we show that the decay properties are sensitive to the property of the Hamilton operator to have extended states. The survival probability does not appear instead to depend on whether the interaction between qubits is described by a Gaussian orthogonal ensemble (often interpreted as a model of ''chaotic'' dynamics) or is modeled by an analytically solvable chain. We interpret this phenomenon as a manifestation of a general mechanism for the emergence of equilibration in purely unitary dynamics. Finally, under the same hypothesis of an initial preparation with projection on a large fraction of the extended eigenstates of the Hamilton operator, we show how to extend the classical Kac-Mazur-Montroll estimate of the return time to the quantum survival probability.
one_liner: A theory of excitation survival in multi-qubit systems links decay behavior to eigenstate extension rather than interaction-model chaos.
why_it_matters: It provides measurable dynamical benchmarks for superconducting-qubit platforms and clarifies when equilibration signatures should emerge.
detailed_summary: The authors derive analytic expressions for the survival probability of a local one-particle excitation in stylized multi-qubit Hamiltonians motivated by superconducting-circuit platforms. Using random-matrix methods, they show that the decay is governed primarily by whether the Hamiltonian supports extended eigenstates, not by whether interactions are modeled by a GOE ensemble or an exactly solvable chain. Under initial states overlapping many extended eigenstates, they interpret the resulting behavior as a generic route to equilibration in closed-unitary dynamics. They also extend the Kac-Mazur-Montroll return-time estimate to the quantum survival-probability setting.
main_result: The main result is that excitation-survival decay tracks eigenstate extension and yields analytic equilibration benchmarks largely independent of chaotic-versus-integrable interaction modeling.
application_angle: These predictions can guide experimental diagnosis of transport, equilibration, and disorder effects in large superconducting-qubit devices and related sensing architectures.

title: Learning Hidden Structures in Open Quantum Dynamics
abstract: We introduce a machine-learning approach for identifying hidden structural features of open quantum dynamics under restricted experimental access. Unlike most existing data-driven methods which focus on detection or prediction of dynamical behavior, our framework targets the inference of invariant algebraic structures underlying the effective Markovian evolution. Measurement limitations, symmetries, and superselection rules are incorporated through a $*$-algebraic description of accessible observables. The learning problem is formulated as maximum-likelihood estimation from multi-time measurement sequences, where the algebraic type of an invariant subalgebra - articularly a decoherence-free subalgebra - is treated as a discrete structural hypothesis. The feasibility of the approach is illustrated on multiple synthetic models and a waveguide quantum electrodynamics system, where nontrivial intermediate algebraic structures are identified directly from measurement data.
one_liner: A maximum-likelihood ML framework infers hidden invariant structures in open quantum dynamics from limited multi-time measurement data.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical route to diagnose decoherence-free structure and symmetry constraints from realistic partial measurements in open quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates structural learning for open quantum dynamics as a discrete hypothesis test over invariant *-subalgebras consistent with restricted observable access. Using maximum-likelihood estimation on multi-time measurement sequences, it infers algebraic structure rather than only predicting trajectories or labels. Synthetic examples and a waveguide-QED case study show recovery of nontrivial intermediate invariant structures, including decoherence-free subalgebras, directly from accessible data.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that hidden invariant algebraic structure in effective Markovian evolution can be identified from restricted measurement records.
application_angle: This could become a diagnostics tool for photonic quantum hardware, helping identify protected subspaces and operational constraints without full tomography.

title: Tunable information insulation induced by constraint mismatch
abstract: We study a composite model of two $1D$ $PXP$ chains with dual constraints, forming a junction that acts as an infinite kinematic barrier to quantum information exchange. Moreover, the hard wall at the junction which acts as a perfect reflector, preventing any quantum information leakage between the two sides of the composite chain, can be made permeable by relaxing the constraint at the junction sites. Multiple frozen junctions shatter the Hilbert space into disjoint Krylov fragments, the number of which increases exponentially with the engineered defects. Furthermore, the energy level statistics in each symmetry-resolved sector are strictly Poissonian, demonstrating that the tensor sum of the disjoint Hamiltonians results in a pure superposition of the chaotic spectra of the sub- $PXP$ chains. We also find that a chirally protected zero-energy mode can exist which has local peaks at the physical edges and within the bulk near the junction sites. This state is protected from hybridization with bulk states induced by any chirality preserving disorder. Due to the tensor product structure of the eigenfunctions, the non-zero energy scar states also multiply in number. Finally, we introduce novel Fock states with spatially tunable thermal and athermal regions. This architecture can be readily realized in programmable Rydberg atom platforms using optical tweezers, addressing beams and facilitation techniques.
one_liner: Constraint-mismatched coupled PXP chains create tunable junctions that can fully block or selectively permit quantum information flow.
why_it_matters: It proposes a directly programmable Rydberg architecture for engineered insulation, fragmentation, protected zero modes, and controllable thermalization patterns.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes two 1D PXP chains with different constraints joined at a junction that acts as an infinite kinematic barrier under strict constraints. Relaxing the junction constraint makes the wall permeable, while multiple frozen junctions exponentially increase Krylov fragmentation and produce strictly Poissonian level statistics in symmetry sectors. The model also supports a chirally protected zero-energy mode localized at edges and near junctions, and multiplies nonzero-energy scar states through tensor-product structure.
main_result: The authors show that engineered constraint mismatch yields tunable quantum-information insulation, fragmentation, protected zero modes, and spatially patterned thermal/athermal regions.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant for configurable quantum simulators and hardware-level isolation of information pathways using optical-tweezer Rydberg platforms.

title: Engineering a Phase-Noise-Based Quantum Random Number Generator for Real-Time Secure Applications: Design, Validation, and Scalability
abstract: Random Number Generators (RNGs) are crucial for applications ranging from cryptography to simulations. Depending on the source of randomness, RNGs are classified into Pseudo-Random Number Generators (PRNGs), True Random Number Generators (TRNGs), and Quantum Random Number Generators (QRNGs). This work presents the end-to-end development of a high-speed, high-efficiency, phase-noise-based QRNG system that taps into the quantum phase noise of a single-frequency laser, with randomness originating from spontaneous emission. Using a self-heterodyne measurement with a semiconductor laser (linewidth $\approx$ 5.23 $GHz$) operated near threshold and a $\sim$48 $cm$ fiber delay line, a raw data generation rate of 2.0 $Gbps$ is achieved. To ensure uniform randomness in the QRNG output, robust extraction techniques developed in-house, such as the Toeplitz Strong Extractor (TSE), are used. Randomness validation using the NIST and Diehard test suites confirms that all statistical tests pass at standard confidence levels. The developed system achieves a post-processed generation rate of 1.0 $Gbps$ in operation and attains a Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of 7, approaching TRL 8, making it suitable for real-time secure applications such as cryptographic key generation and stochastic modeling.
one_liner: A phase-noise QRNG based on a near-threshold semiconductor laser delivers 2.0 Gbps raw and 1.0 Gbps extracted randomness.
why_it_matters: It moves QRNGs toward deployment by combining GHz-rate generation, in-house extraction, statistical validation, and reported TRL-7 maturity.
detailed_summary: The system exploits spontaneous-emission-driven phase noise from a single-frequency semiconductor laser operated near threshold and measured with a self-heterodyne setup using an about 48 cm fiber delay. It achieves 2.0 Gbps raw bit generation and 1.0 Gbps post-processed output using an in-house Toeplitz strong extractor. Output streams pass NIST and Diehard statistical tests, and the implementation is presented as reaching TRL 7 and approaching TRL 8.
main_result: The paper demonstrates a validated real-time photonic QRNG with gigabit-per-second extracted randomness suitable for secure operation.
application_angle: This is directly applicable to cryptographic key generation and other deployed systems that need high-rate trusted entropy sources.

title: Probing topological edge states in a molecular synthetic dimension
abstract: Engineering synthetic dimensions, where the physics of additional spatial dimensions is simulated within the internal states of a quantum system, allows the realisation of phenomena not otherwise accessible in experiments. Ultracold ground-state polar molecules are an ideal platform to encode synthetic dimensions, offering access to large Hilbert spaces of long-lived internal states associated with the rotational and hyperfine degrees of freedom, that can be coupled together with microwave fields to simulate tunnelling. Here, to benchmark the advantages of ultracold molecules, we encode a 1D synthetic lattice in the rotational states of ultracold RbCs molecules and use it to investigate the well-known Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model, a minimal model displaying topological properties. To probe the system, we perform spectroscopy using an auxiliary rotational state and study the time dynamics after deterministic state preparation. We demonstrate long coherence times, typically ~500 times the lattice tunnelling period, even for a synthetic lattice using 8 rotational states. Observations of dynamics at long times with full site-resolved readout of the synthetic dimension allow us to test the effects of chiral and non-chiral perturbations on the topologically protected edge states. Our work lays the foundation for further quantum simulations using the rich internal structure of molecules, including dipolar string phases in interacting samples of molecules, and adiabatic state preparation of many-body Hamiltonians.
one_liner: Ultracold RbCs molecules realize an 8-site SSH synthetic lattice with long coherence and site-resolved probing of topological edge states.
why_it_matters: It establishes molecules as a scalable, coherent synthetic-dimension platform for topological simulation and future interacting many-body protocols.
detailed_summary: The authors encode a 1D synthetic lattice in rotational states of ultracold ground-state RbCs molecules, with microwave-driven couplings implementing SSH tunneling. Spectroscopy via an auxiliary rotational state and deterministic state preparation enable direct probing of edge-state dynamics and perturbation response. Even with 8 rotational states, coherence times reach roughly 500 tunneling periods, allowing long-time site-resolved observation of topologically protected behavior under chiral and non-chiral perturbations.
main_result: The work demonstrates coherent topological edge-state spectroscopy and dynamics in a molecular SSH synthetic lattice with full site-resolved readout.
application_angle: This opens a route to molecule-based quantum simulators for topological phases, dipolar many-body states, and adiabatic preparation of complex Hamiltonians.

title: Lower Bounds on Coherent State Rank
abstract: The approximate coherent state rank is the minimal number of (classical) coherent states required to approximate a continuous-variable bosonic quantum state and directly relates to the classical complexity of simulating bosonic computations. Despite its importance, little is known about lower bounds on this quantity, even for basic families of states. In this work, we initiate a systematic study of lower bounds on the approximate coherent state rank. Our contributions are as follows. (i) We introduce a technique based on low-rank approximation theory yielding generic lower bounds on the approximate coherent state rank of arbitrary single-mode states. (ii) Using this technique, we find a complete characterization of all single-mode states of finite approximate coherent state rank, and we obtain in particular analytical expressions for the approximate coherent state rank of squeezed states and of finite superpositions of Fock states. (iii) We further show that our single-mode lower bounds can be lifted to multimode lower bounds for finite superpositions of multimode Fock states. (iv) Finally, we prove a super-polynomial lower bound on the approximate coherent state rank of the $n$-mode Fock state $|1\rangle^{\otimes n}$, by exploiting a connection to the permanent. To do so, we show that the algebraic complexity of approximate multi-linear formulas for the permanent is super-polynomial, building upon the proof of a lower bound for exact formulas due to [Raz, JACM 2009]. Our results establish an unconditional barrier to efficient classical simulation of Boson Sampling via coherent state decompositions and connect non-classicality of bosonic quantum systems to central questions in algebraic complexity.
one_liner: New lower bounds on approximate coherent-state rank quantify when bosonic states resist efficient classical simulation.
why_it_matters: The results give unconditional complexity barriers for coherent-state decompositions, sharpening the computational hardness case for bosonic quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a low-rank-approximation method to bound the approximate coherent-state rank of arbitrary single-mode bosonic states. It fully characterizes when single-mode states have finite approximate coherent-state rank and derives analytic expressions for squeezed states and finite Fock superpositions. The authors then lift these bounds to multimode settings and prove a super-polynomial lower bound for the rank of the n-mode state |1>^⊗n via a connection to approximate multilinear formulas for the permanent.
main_result: The main result is a set of generic and explicit lower bounds, including a super-polynomial bound for |1>^⊗n, limiting efficient classical simulation via coherent-state decompositions.
application_angle: These bounds strengthen the practical motivation for photonic bosonic processors by identifying state families with provable classical simulation difficulty.

title: Stern-Gerlach interferometry in three dimensions: the role of transverse fields
abstract: We show that superficially similar implementations of Stern-Gerlach Interferometers (SGIs) are expected to differ dramatically in their sensitivity to fields transverse to the primary acceleration direction. These transverse fields unavoidably accompany any static magnetic or electric field gradients, and have been shown by Comparat [Phys. Rev. A 101, 023606 (2020)] to limit the precision application of SGIs. As a concrete example, we consider SGIs with ultracold Rb Rydberg atoms accelerated by spatially-varying electric fields. We find that the deleterious effect of transverse fields imply that only some implementations (sequences of field gradients, internal state swaps, and so-on) may exhibit fringes with high visibility.
one_liner: A 3D analysis of Stern-Gerlach interferometers shows transverse fields can strongly suppress fringe visibility in some implementations.
why_it_matters: It provides concrete design guidance for atom-interferometric hardware by identifying which SGI protocols remain viable under unavoidable transverse fields.
detailed_summary: The study compares superficially similar SGI protocols in full three dimensions and shows that unavoidable transverse magnetic or electric field components affect them very differently. Focusing on ultracold Rb Rydberg atoms accelerated by spatially varying electric fields, it evaluates how sequences of gradients and internal-state swaps alter coherence and interference. The analysis finds that only certain implementations can retain high-visibility fringes once transverse-field effects are included.
main_result: The paper shows that transverse fields impose protocol-dependent limits on SGI performance, with only selected implementations supporting strong interference fringes.
application_angle: This is directly useful for designing precision quantum sensors and interferometric hardware that rely on Stern-Gerlach splitting under realistic field conditions.

title: Highly-Parallel Atom-Detection Accelerator for Tweezer-Based Neutral Atom Quantum Computers
abstract: Neutral atom quantum computers (NAQCs) are among the most promising computational platforms for quantum computing. Controlling and measuring individual atoms and their states, which often requires multiple imaging and image-analysis procedures, is typically the most time-consuming task during computation and contributes significantly to overall cycle times. To resolve this challenge, we propose a highly-parallel atom-detection accelerator for tweezer-based NAQCs. Our design builds on an existing state-reconstruction method and combines an algorithm-level optimization with a Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) implementation to maximize parallelism and reduce the run time of the image-analysis process. We identify and overcome several challenges for an FPGA implementation, such as introducing a prefetching mechanism to improve scalability and customizing bus transfers to support large bandwidths. Tested on a Xilinx UltraScale+ FPGA, our design can analyze a 256x256-pixel fluorescence image in just 115mus, achieving 34.9x and 6.3x speedups over the original and optimized CPU baseline, respectively. Moreover, our accelerator can maintain consistent resource utilization across various atom array sizes, contributing to the ongoing efforts toward scalable and fully integrated FPGA-based control systems for NAQCs.
one_liner: An FPGA accelerator cuts neutral-atom fluorescence image analysis to 115 microseconds for 256x256 frames.
why_it_matters: Fast atom-state reconstruction directly reduces neutral-atom cycle time and supports tightly integrated real-time control stacks.
detailed_summary: The work targets the image-analysis bottleneck in tweezer-based neutral-atom quantum computers, where repeated fluorescence imaging and atom-state reconstruction dominate cycle time. It combines an algorithmic optimization with an FPGA implementation, adding prefetching and custom high-bandwidth bus transfers to preserve scalability. On a Xilinx UltraScale+ FPGA, the design processes a 256x256 fluorescence image in 115 us, yielding 34.9x speedup over the original CPU method and 6.3x over an optimized CPU baseline. Resource usage remains stable across different atom-array sizes, which is important for scaling integrated control hardware.
platform_or_system: tweezer-based neutral-atom QC with FPGA control
main_result: A Xilinx UltraScale+ implementation analyzes 256x256 fluorescence images in 115 us with up to 34.9x CPU speedup.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to neutral-atom hardware because faster atom detection can shorten experimental dead time and enable larger real-time feedback loops.

title: Ground-state solution of quantum droplets in Bose-Bose mixtures
abstract: In this paper, we present a systematic study on the ground state computation of quantum droplets in homonuclear Bose-Bose mixtures, governed by the extended Gross-Pitaevskii equations (eGPEs) with Lee-Huang-Yang (LHY) corrections. This model captures the formation of self-bound droplets stabilized by the delicate balance between the attractive mean-field interaction and the repulsive quantum fluctuations. We formulate dimensionless energy functionals for both the general two-component system and the reduced single-component density-locked model. To compute the ground states efficiently, we adapt and benchmark various gradient flow discretization schemes, identifying a backward-forward sine-pseudospectral scheme based on the gradient flow with Lagrange multiplier method (GFLM-BFSP) as the robust solver for our simulations. Utilizing this method, we report three main numerical observations: (i) the density-locked model is quantitatively validated as a reliable approximation for ground state properties; (ii) the dimension-dependent convergence rates of the Thomas-Fermi approximation are established in the strong-coupling regime; and (iii) the critical particle number for self-binding in free space is numerically determined, providing a precise correction to the analytical prediction by Petrov [Phys. Rev. Lett. 115, 155302 (2015)].
one_liner: A robust pseudospectral gradient-flow solver maps ground states and self-binding thresholds of Bose-Bose quantum droplets.
why_it_matters: Reliable droplet-state computation improves quantitative design of ultracold-atom platforms where self-bound quantum fluids are engineered and probed.
detailed_summary: The paper studies ground states of homonuclear Bose-Bose quantum droplets using extended Gross-Pitaevskii equations with Lee-Huang-Yang corrections. It formulates dimensionless energy functionals for both the full two-component model and a reduced density-locked model, then benchmarks gradient-flow discretizations and identifies a backward-forward sine-pseudospectral GFLM-BFSP scheme as the most robust solver. Using that method, the authors validate the density-locked approximation, establish dimension-dependent Thomas-Fermi convergence in the strong-coupling regime, and numerically determine the critical particle number for self-binding in free space. The computed threshold sharpens the analytical prediction of Petrov for droplet formation.
main_result: The study delivers a robust numerical route to droplet ground states and refines the critical self-binding particle number beyond prior analytical estimates.
application_angle: Accurate droplet modeling is useful for planning and interpreting ultracold-atom experiments that exploit self-bound states and interaction engineering.

title: The effect of staggered nonlinearity on the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model
abstract: We investigate the spectral properties of the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model with sublattice-dependent onsite nonlinearity. Two complementary approaches are employed in our studies. First, Bloch state solutions under periodic boundary conditions are assumed to enable semi-analytical treatment, which allows us to obtain the system's energy band structure and further derive a general expression of the Zak phase that incorporates nonlinearity-induced correction (referred to as nonlinear Zak phase). This analysis reveals that, at sufficiently high nonlinearities, a nonlinearity-induced topological phase transition occurs, marked by a discontinuity in the nonlinear Zak phase. The second approach amounts to numerically obtaining other (non-Bloch) solutions under open boundary conditions, employing the Self-Consistent Field Iterative Method. Its main results include the observation of an edge state's energy that is independent of a nonlinear parameter, a persisting band touching point that only shifts in the presence of perturbations reminiscent of Weyl points in a Weyl semimetal, as well as delocalized solutions that persist even at extreme nonlinearity strengths. These findings illuminate the rich interplay between topology and nonlinearity in lattice models with potential realization in optical/acoustic waveguide settings.
one_liner: Staggered onsite nonlinearity drives a topological phase transition in a nonlinear SSH lattice and preserves unusual edge features.
why_it_matters: It identifies nonlinear topological behavior that could be engineered in waveguide or acoustic lattices for robust mode control.
detailed_summary: This work analyzes an SSH model with sublattice-dependent onsite nonlinearity using both Bloch-state treatment under periodic boundary conditions and self-consistent numerical solutions under open boundaries. The semi-analytical approach produces a nonlinear correction to the Zak phase and shows a nonlinearity-induced topological phase transition marked by a discontinuity at sufficiently large nonlinearities. Under open boundaries, the authors find an edge-state energy independent of the nonlinear parameter, a shifted but persistent band-touching point reminiscent of Weyl physics, and delocalized solutions that survive even at extreme nonlinearity. The predicted effects are framed as accessible in optical or acoustic waveguide realizations.
main_result: The model exhibits a nonlinearity-driven topological transition and robust open-boundary features including nonlinear-insensitive edge-state energy.
application_angle: The results point to tunable topological transport and localization in photonic or acoustic lattices with engineered nonlinear response.

title: Two-Qubit Implementation of QAOA for MAX-CUT on an NV-Center Quantum Processor
abstract: We report a proof-of-principle implementation of the quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) for the smallest nontrivial MAX-CUT instance on an NV-center-based quantum processor operating at room temperature. The two-qubit register is encoded in the electron spin and the ${}^{14}\mathrm{N}$ nuclear spin of a single NV$^-$ center. Using a minimization formulation of MAX-CUT, we implement a single-layer QAOA ansatz with native entangling and single-qubit control operations. Because the optical readout of the NV$^-$ center is not projective in the computational basis, we reconstruct computational-basis populations from averaged fluorescence signals and use them to determine the experimental QAOA cost landscape by scanning the variational parameters. These results show that the core elements of QAOA can be realized on this platform and establish a baseline for future improvements in phase tracking, coherence-preserving control, and scaling to larger problem sizes.
one_liner: A room-temperature NV center processor runs a two-qubit QAOA instance for MAX-CUT using native electron-nuclear spin control.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical optimization primitive on solid-state spins under ambient conditions, clarifying what is needed to scale variational algorithms on NV hardware.
detailed_summary: The experiment encodes a two-qubit register in the electron spin and the 14N nuclear spin of a single NV- center and implements a single-layer QAOA ansatz for the smallest nontrivial MAX-CUT problem. Native single-qubit and entangling operations are used, while non-projective optical readout is handled by reconstructing computational-basis populations from averaged fluorescence. By scanning the variational parameters, the authors obtain the experimental QAOA cost landscape on the hardware. The study establishes a proof-of-principle baseline for improving phase tracking, coherence-preserving control, and future scaling.
platform_or_system: single NV- center electron-nuclear spin processor
main_result: The team experimentally realized a two-qubit, single-layer QAOA for MAX-CUT on an NV-center quantum processor at room temperature.
application_angle: This demonstrates that ambient solid-state spin hardware can execute variational optimization subroutines relevant to near-term quantum applications.

title: Distilling Unitary Operations: A No-Go Theorem and Minimal Realization
abstract: Quantum gates executed on physical hardware are inevitably degraded by environmental noise. While state purification effectively distills static quantum resources, the dynamic execution of quantum algorithms requires a higher-order approach to mitigate errors on the operations themselves. In this work, we investigate unitary purification: the task of utilizing a quantum higher-order operation to partially restore the ideal action of an unknown unitary corrupted by a known noise model. Focusing on canonical depolarizing noise, we first reveal a fundamental operational obstruction. We prove that within the indefinite causal order framework, no nontrivial 2-slot higher-order operation can universally purify the set of single-qubit unitaries. Overcoming this strict limitation, we establish that a 3-slot architecture provides the minimal realization for non-trivial universal purification. We analytically derive the optimal average fidelity for the 3-slot regime, demonstrating that it strictly surpasses trivial strategies by systematically utilizing ancillary qubits as a quantum memory to absorb errors. Furthermore, we provide a concrete quantum circuit construction for this optimal higher-order operation. Our results establish the strict theoretical boundaries of distilling clean operations from noisy gates, offering immediate architectural insights for robust gate design.
one_liner: A no-go theorem shows two uses of a noisy gate cannot universally purify single-qubit unitaries, while three uses can.
why_it_matters: It sets a hard lower bound for gate-purification protocols and gives a concrete minimal architecture for improving noisy operations.
detailed_summary: The paper studies unitary purification, where a higher-order quantum operation attempts to recover the action of an unknown unitary affected by known depolarizing noise. It proves that no nontrivial universal 2-slot protocol within the indefinite causal order framework can purify arbitrary single-qubit unitaries, establishing a strict no-go result. The authors then show that 3 slots are the minimal nontrivial realization, derive the optimal average fidelity analytically, and provide an explicit circuit construction that uses ancillas as quantum memory to absorb errors. This gives both a foundational limit and a constructive protocol for operation-level error mitigation.
main_result: Universal purification of noisy single-qubit unitaries is impossible with 2 slots but achievable nontrivially with an optimal 3-slot architecture.
application_angle: The result informs practical gate-mitigation and networked-operation designs by specifying the minimal circuit resources needed to distill cleaner effective operations.

title: Tsim: Fast Universal Simulator for Quantum Error Correction
abstract: We present Tsim, an open-source high-throughput simulator for universal noisy quantum circuits targeting quantum error correction. Tsim represents quantum circuits as ZX diagrams, where Pauli channels are modeled as parameterized vertices. Diagrams are simplified via parameterized ZX rules, and then compiled for vectorized sampling with GPU acceleration. After the one-time compilation, one can sample detector or measurement shots in linear time in the number of Clifford gates and exponentially only in the number of non-Clifford gates. Tsim implements the Stim API and fully supports the Stim circuit format, extending it with T and arbitrary single-qubit rotation instructions. For low-magic circuits, Tsim throughput can match the sampling performance of Stim.
one_liner: Tsim uses ZX-diagram compilation and GPU sampling to simulate noisy QEC circuits with support for non-Clifford gates.
why_it_matters: Fast realistic simulation is essential for designing and benchmarking fault-tolerant protocols before committing hardware resources.
detailed_summary: Tsim is an open-source simulator for universal noisy quantum circuits aimed at quantum error-correction workloads. It represents circuits as ZX diagrams with Pauli noise channels encoded as parameterized vertices, simplifies them with ZX rewrite rules, and compiles the result for vectorized GPU-accelerated sampling. After compilation, sampling scales linearly with the number of Clifford gates and exponentially only in the number of non-Clifford gates, making low-magic circuits especially efficient. The tool implements the Stim API and extends the Stim circuit format with T gates and arbitrary single-qubit rotations.
main_result: The simulator achieves high-throughput sampling for noisy universal circuits, with low-magic performance that can match Stim.
application_angle: This provides a practical software stack for rapid QEC protocol iteration, decoder benchmarking, and hardware-informed fault-tolerance studies.

title: Simultaneous operation of an 18-qubit modular array in germanium
abstract: Utility-scale quantum computing requires the integration and operation of a large-scale qubit register. Semiconductor spin qubits are a primary candidate for this, due to the prospects of building integrated hybrid quantum-classical architectures. However, scaling spin-qubit systems while preserving performance and control has remained a challenge. Here, we demonstrate the operation of an 18-qubit array in germanium based on an extendable 2xN architecture. We achieve simultaneous initialization, control, and readout across the entire array, enabled by parallel operation of modular unit cells. Across the array, we achieve average and median single-qubit gate fidelities of 99.8% and 99.9%, respectively. Finally, we characterize the nearest-neighbor exchange couplings throughout the device and implement high-quality controlled-Z gates to generate a three-qubit Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state. These results demonstrate that spin-qubit arrays can be scaled while maintaining high-fidelity operation and establish a modular, extendable architecture for planar semiconductor quantum processors.
one_liner: An 18-qubit germanium spin-qubit array is operated in parallel with high-fidelity gates and modular control.
why_it_matters: It is a credible scaling step for semiconductor quantum processors, showing array-wide control without sacrificing gate quality.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates simultaneous initialization, single-qubit control, and readout across an 18-qubit germanium device built in an extendable 2xN modular architecture. The authors report average and median single-qubit fidelities of 99.8% and 99.9% across the array, indicating uniform performance under parallel operation. They also map nearest-neighbor exchange couplings and use them to implement controlled-Z gates, culminating in a three-qubit GHZ-state demonstration. The work directly addresses the scaling bottleneck for semiconductor spin qubits by combining modularity with array-wide operability.
main_result: The system achieves full-array parallel operation of 18 qubits with ~99.8-99.9% single-qubit fidelity and entangling-gate capability sufficient for a three-qubit GHZ state.
application_angle: The modular planar layout and parallel control/readout are directly relevant to building larger hybrid quantum-classical semiconductor processors.

title: FerBo: a noise resilient qubit hybridizing Andreev and fluxonium states
abstract: We propose a novel superconducting quantum circuit that should be robust against both relaxation and dephasing over a wide and experimentally accessible parameter range. The circuit consists of a parallel arrangement of a large inductance, a small capacitor, and a well-transmitting Josephson weak link. Protection against relaxation arises from the hybridization between the fermionic degree of freedom associated with Andreev levels in the weak link and the bosonic electromagnetic mode of the LC circuit, hence its name: FerBo. Furthermore, as in the fluxonium qubit, delocalization of the wavefunctions in phase space provides resilience against dephasing.
one_liner: FerBo is a proposed superconducting qubit that hybridizes Andreev and fluxonium physics for simultaneous relaxation and dephasing resilience.
why_it_matters: If realized experimentally, it could offer a practical protected qubit design with reduced sensitivity to both major superconducting noise channels.
detailed_summary: This theory paper introduces FerBo, a superconducting circuit comprising a large inductance, a small capacitance, and a highly transparent Josephson weak link in parallel. Its protection mechanism combines fermionic Andreev-level degrees of freedom with a bosonic LC mode, which suppresses relaxation through hybridization. In parallel, fluxonium-like phase-space delocalization reduces dephasing sensitivity over a broad and experimentally accessible parameter regime. The design aims to unify two distinct protection ideas in a single qubit modality.
main_result: The authors propose a qubit architecture predicted to be robust against both relaxation and dephasing across a wide practical parameter range.
application_angle: A qubit with built-in resilience to dominant noise sources could lower hardware overheads for error correction and simplify superconducting processor design.

title: High Performance Quantum Emulation for Chemistry Applications with Hyperion
abstract: The strategic demand for quantum hardware currently outpaces the availability of near-term devices, necessitating high-performance software emulators to validate novel protocols. We introduce Hyperion, a massively parallel, GPU-accelerated quantum emulator architected to bypass the classical memory walls inherent in strongly correlated quantum chemistry simulations. Hyperion leverages custom-optimized Sparse Matrix-Sparse Vector (SpMspV) kernels to natively accelerate exact matrix-vector multiplications, enabling strictly accurate State-Vector (SV) ADAPT-VQE simulations for up to 32 qubits on multi-node platforms. To scale beyond this hardware limit, we address the trade-off in pure Matrix Product State (MPS) emulators, where standard compression yields severe truncation errors and strict compression triggers intractable tensor rank explosions. We propose a novel partitioned emulation, namely the SV-MPS strategy: by routing non-interacting terms into an exact sparse SV core and delegating interacting terms to the MPS engine, this approach achieves emulation of 36 to 40 qubits with controlled approximations. This partitioning significantly reduces GPU resource requirements while maintaining robust accuracy across ADAPT-VQE iterations. Ultimately, Hyperion offers a high-fidelity platform dedicated to the development of new quantum algorithms for chemistry, enabling the modeling of realistic chemical systems at accuracies approaching the exact Full Configuration Interaction (FCI) / Complete Basis Set (CBS) limit.
one_liner: Hyperion is a GPU-accelerated quantum emulator that pushes chemistry-oriented ADAPT-VQE simulations to 32 exact and 36-40 approximate qubits.
why_it_matters: It expands the practical validation range for chemistry algorithms before sufficient quantum hardware exists, enabling more realistic benchmarking.
detailed_summary: Hyperion is presented as a massively parallel emulator optimized for strongly correlated quantum chemistry workloads using custom sparse matrix-sparse vector kernels. It performs exact state-vector ADAPT-VQE simulations up to 32 qubits on multi-node GPU platforms, explicitly targeting the memory bottlenecks that limit classical emulation. To go larger, the authors introduce an SV-MPS partitioned scheme that routes non-interacting terms to an exact sparse state-vector core and interacting terms to an MPS engine, reaching 36 to 40 qubits with controlled approximation. The framework is positioned as a high-fidelity development platform for chemistry algorithms approaching FCI/CBS accuracy.
main_result: The emulator enables exact 32-qubit ADAPT-VQE chemistry simulations and extends to 36-40 qubits via a hybrid SV-MPS strategy with controlled error.
application_angle: It gives algorithm developers a practical way to test chemistry workloads and resource tradeoffs at scales beyond today's quantum devices.

title: Learning and Generating Mixed States Prepared by Shallow Channel Circuits
abstract: Learning quantum states from measurement data is a central problem in quantum information and computational complexity. In this work, we study the problem of learning to generate mixed states on a finite-dimensional lattice. Motivated by recent developments in mixed state phases of matter, we focus on arbitrary states in the trivial phase. A state belongs to the trivial phase if there exists a shallow preparation channel circuit under which local reversibility is preserved throughout the preparation. We prove that any mixed state in this class can be efficiently learned from measurement access alone. Specifically, given copies of an unknown trivial phase mixed state, our algorithm outputs a shallow local channel circuit that approximately generates this state in trace distance. The sample complexity and runtime are polynomial (or quasi-polynomial) in the number of qubits, assuming constant (or polylogarithmic) circuit depth and gate locality. Importantly, the learner is not given the original preparation circuit and relies only on its existence. Our results provide a structural foundation for quantum generative models based on shallow channel circuits. In the classical limit, our framework also inspires an efficient algorithm for classical diffusion models using only a polynomial overhead of training and generation.
one_liner: A new learning framework reconstructs mixed states in the trivial phase from measurements by outputting shallow local channel circuits.
why_it_matters: It provides a concrete route to efficient mixed-state tomography and generative modeling under realistic shallow-circuit structure assumptions.
detailed_summary: The authors study learning of lattice mixed states that can be prepared by shallow local channel circuits while preserving local reversibility, i.e. mixed states in the trivial phase. From measurement access alone and without knowing the original preparation circuit, their algorithm outputs a shallow local channel circuit that approximately reproduces the target state in trace distance. They prove polynomial or quasipolynomial sample and runtime complexity under constant or polylogarithmic depth and locality assumptions. The work also draws a parallel to classical diffusion models, suggesting efficient classical generative schemes inspired by the quantum framework.
main_result: The paper proves efficient learnability and generation of a broad class of mixed states using only measurements and structural shallow-circuit assumptions.
application_angle: Efficient mixed-state reconstruction could support scalable characterization, verification, and generative modeling for noisy quantum platforms.

title: Numerically Optimizing Shortcuts to Adiabaticity: A Hybrid Control Strategy
abstract: Achieving fast, excitation-free quantum control is a vital challenge in modern quantum technologies. In many cases, shortcuts to adiabaticity enable fast adiabatic-like protocols, yet determining control parameters that satisfy practical constraints is often challenging in complex systems. Here, we combine an analytical shortcut to adiabaticity approach with several numerical optimization methods to boost the performance of the protocol. As a proof-of-principle for this hybrid approach, we study a particularly intricate control problem, the separation of two trapped ions. We show that this analytical-numerical approach, along with the physical insight gained through the variety of suboptimal solutions, leads to the exploration of new solutions in a complex landscape that yield improvements of up to 3 orders of magnitude. Moreover, this improvement comes with no additional cost from an experimental point of view.
one_liner: A hybrid analytical-numerical shortcut-to-adiabaticity method dramatically improves fast ion-separation control without extra experimental complexity.
why_it_matters: Better nonadiabatic control directly benefits trapped-ion hardware by reducing motional excitation in a key transport primitive.
detailed_summary: The work combines analytic shortcut-to-adiabaticity constructions with several numerical optimization methods to improve constrained quantum-control protocols. As a demanding test case, it considers the separation of two trapped ions, where fast transport typically induces unwanted excitation. The hybrid strategy explores the control landscape more effectively than either approach alone and yields solutions with performance improvements of up to three orders of magnitude. Importantly, these gains are reported to require no additional experimental overhead.
main_result: The hybrid STA-plus-optimization approach finds ion-separation protocols that suppress excitation by up to three orders of magnitude better than baseline solutions.
application_angle: High-fidelity fast transport primitives can raise trapped-ion processor throughput while preserving gate quality and reducing idle-time overhead.

title: Quasi-bandgap behavior in non-Hermitian photonic crystals
abstract: We investigate non-Hermitian photonic crystals in which the lossy and lossless constituents share the same real permittivity and differ only in their imaginary part. We characterize the complex band structure and reflection response of both one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) systems, and show that introducing even a small amount of material loss opens a quasi bandgap at the Brillouin-zone boundary. This quasi bandgap, absent in the lossless limit of the same structure, gives rise to sharp reflectivity peaks whose origin we explain through second-order perturbation theory. As an application of this behavior, we demonstrate a selective reflector combining a conventional photonic-crystal waveguide with a non-Hermitian photonic crystal, achieving wavelength-selective reflection with broadband absorption.
one_liner: Loss-only contrast in non-Hermitian photonic crystals creates quasi-bandgaps that produce sharp, selective reflection peaks.
why_it_matters: It identifies a simple route to wavelength-selective photonic functionality using engineered loss rather than index contrast alone.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze 1D and 2D non-Hermitian photonic crystals in which the real permittivity is identical between constituents and only the imaginary part differs. They show that even small material loss opens a quasi-bandgap at the Brillouin-zone boundary, a feature absent in the corresponding Hermitian structure. This produces sharp reflectivity peaks, which they explain using second-order perturbation theory on the complex band structure. As a device example, they combine a standard photonic-crystal waveguide with a non-Hermitian crystal to realize wavelength-selective reflection with broadband absorption.
main_result: Introducing loss contrast alone is shown to induce quasi-bandgaps and sharp reflection features in 1D and 2D photonic crystal structures.
