application_angle: The effect can be used for compact selective reflectors, filters, and absorptive photonic routing elements based on engineered non-Hermitian materials.

title: Approximating the Permanent of a Random Matrix with Polynomially Small Mean: Zeros and Universality
abstract: We study algorithms for approximating the permanent of a random matrix when the entries are slightly biased away from zero. This question is motivated by the goal of understanding the classical complexity of linear optics and \emph{boson sampling} (Aaronson and Arkhipov '11; Eldar and Mehraban '17). Barvinok's interpolation method enables efficient approximation of the permanent, provided one can establish a sufficiently large zero-free region for the polynomial $\mathrm{per}(zJ + W)$, where $J$ is the all-ones matrix and $W$ is a random matrix with independent mean-zero entries. We show that when the entries of $W$ are standard complex Gaussians, all zeros of the random polynomial $\mathrm{per}(zJ + W)$ lie within a disk of radius $\tilde{O}(n^{-1/3})$, which yields an approximation algorithm when the bias of the entries is $\tildeΩ(n^{-1/3})$. Previously, there were no efficient algorithms at biases smaller than $1/\mathrm{polylog}(n)$, and it was unknown whether there typically exist zeros $z$ with $|z| \ge 1$. As a complementary result, we show that the bulk of the zeros, namely $(1 - ε)n$ of them, have magnitude $Θ(n^{-1/2})$. This prevents our interpolation method from contradicting the conjectured average-case hardness of approximating the permanent. We also establish analogous zero-free regions for the hardcore model on general graphs with complex vertex fugacities. In addition, we prove universality results establishing zero-free regions for random matrices $W$ with i.i.d. subexponential entries.
one_liner: Shows new zero-free regions that enable faster approximation of random-matrix permanents at much smaller bias, sharpening the classical boundary around boson sampling.
why_it_matters: It tightens the known regime where boson-sampling-like linear-optics instances may be classically tractable, informing quantum advantage claims and hardware benchmarking.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes the random polynomial per(zJ+W) for W with i.i.d. complex Gaussian entries and proves all zeros lie inside a disk of radius roughly n^{-1/3} up to polylog factors. This yields an efficient Barvinok-style interpolation algorithm when the matrix entries have mean bias of order at least n^{-1/3} up to polylog factors, substantially below prior polylog-scale guarantees. It also shows that most zeros remain at magnitude Theta(n^{-1/2}), preserving room for average-case hardness conjectures, and extends the zero-free results to subexponential ensembles and a related hardcore-model setting.
main_result: It proves a near n^{-1/3} zero-free regime for random permanent polynomials, enabling polynomial-time approximation at correspondingly small entry bias.
application_angle: The results help delineate which photonic sampling regimes remain hard versus classically simulable, a practical issue for validating quantum photonic processors.

title: Programmable recirculating bricks mesh architecture for quantum photonics
abstract: General-purpose programmable photonic processors offer a flexible foundation for integrating various functionalities within a single chip. A two-dimensional hexagonal waveguide mesh of Mach Zehnder interferometers has been shown to have great potential in the field of microwave photonics. Additionally, they are a promising platform for the creation of unitary linear transformations, which are key elements in photonic neural networks, In this article, we expand the portfolio of available applications for recirculating bricks mesh architecture to quantum technologies. We will show that a single programmable optical system is capable of performing various functions depending on the requirements. In particular, we will focus in this work on boson sampling, a task that best demonstrates quantum advantage, as well as on tasks that enable the determination of photon indistinguishability, which plays a key role in photonic quantum technologies. We will also show that, in addition to spatial modes, the same optical system can be equally well-suited for work on temporal modes through the implementation of an appropriate number of loops.
one_liner: Proposes a programmable recirculating bricks mesh that can implement boson sampling and photon-indistinguishability tests in spatial or temporal modes.
why_it_matters: A single reconfigurable photonic architecture covering multiple quantum tasks could reduce hardware specialization and accelerate deployment of chip-scale quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: The work extends a 2D hexagonal Mach-Zehnder interferometer waveguide mesh, previously used in microwave photonics, into a quantum-photonic processing architecture. The recirculating bricks mesh is described as a general-purpose linear-optical processor that can be programmed for boson-sampling interferometers and circuits for assessing photon indistinguishability. By adding suitable loop resources, the same platform can operate over temporal as well as spatial modes, increasing effective connectivity and functional density.
main_result: It presents a unified recirculating photonic mesh architecture capable of implementing multiple quantum-photonic primitives on one programmable device.
application_angle: This architecture is relevant for adaptable photonic testbeds and future integrated processors that must switch between benchmarking, characterization, and computation workloads.

title: Resource Estimation via Efficient Compilation of Key Quantum Primitives
abstract: Resource estimation is a significant challenge in evaluating fault tolerant quantum computers. Existing approaches often rely on either fixed architectural assumptions or coarse analytical models that fail to capture the interaction between hardware constraints and circuit compilation. This challenge is particularly acute for neutral atom quantum computers, where architectural features such as atom movement, measurement zones, and multi-species arrays introduce a broad design space for implementing fault tolerant computation. Addressing the need for a tighter feedback loop between hardware design and practical application development, we present a compilation-driven framework for quantum resource estimation that translates arbitrary quantum circuits into logical primitive operations with known physical resource costs. This framework allows for easily configurable hardware assumptions that enable rapid comparison of different architectural design choices. We apply our approach to two early fault tolerant quantum simulation and optimization workloads, assuming the use of the surface code, revealing several architectural trends. While the production of magic states continues to be the dominant source of overhead for these benchmarks, access to movement can save time on cultivation and important transversal gates. As problem size grows, routing and qubit movement become dominant bottlenecks, highlighting the need for movement-aware compiler optimizations and frugal routing strategies. Finally, our results suggest that neutral atom architectures combining dual-species arrays with controlled qubit movement offer a promising path toward near-term advantage on fault tolerant devices.
one_liner: Introduces a compilation-driven resource estimator linking fault-tolerant circuit primitives to hardware costs, with a focus on neutral-atom architectures.
why_it_matters: It gives hardware designers a concrete way to compare architectural choices and exposes where movement, routing, and magic-state overhead dominate real workloads.
detailed_summary: The paper builds a configurable framework that compiles arbitrary circuits into logical primitive operations with assigned physical resource costs, rather than relying on fixed analytic models. Using early fault-tolerant simulation and optimization workloads under surface-code assumptions, it studies how movement, measurement zones, and dual-species layouts affect execution. The analysis finds magic-state production remains dominant, but movement can reduce cultivation and transversal-gate cost, while routing and transport bottlenecks grow with problem size.
main_result: It demonstrates that compilation-aware estimation reveals dual-species neutral-atom systems with controlled movement as a favorable architecture for early fault-tolerant applications.
application_angle: The framework is directly useful for co-designing neutral-atom hardware and compilers around realistic throughput, layout, and routing constraints.

title: Quantum polymorphism characterisation of commutativity gadgets in all quantum models
abstract: Commutativity gadgets provide a technique for lifting classical reductions between constraint satisfaction problems to quantum-sound reductions between the corresponding nonlocal games. We develop a general framework for commutativity gadgets in the setting of quantum homomorphisms between finite relational structures. Building on the notion of quantum homomorphism spaces, we introduce a uniform notion of commutativity gadget capturing the finite-dimensional quantum, quantum approximate, and commuting-operator models. In the robust setting, we use the weighted-algebra formalism for approximate quantum homomorphisms to capture corresponding notions of robust commutativity gadgets. Our main results characterize both non-robust and robust commutativity gadgets purely in terms of quantum polymorphism spaces: in any model, existence of a commutativity gadget is equivalent to the collapse of the corresponding quantum polymorphisms to classical ones at arity $|A|^2$, and robust gadgets are characterized by stable commutativity of the appropriate weighted polymorphism algebra. We use this characterisation to show relations between the classes of commutativity gadget, notably that existence of a robust commutativity gadget is equivalent to the existence of a corresponding non-robust one. Finally, we prove that quantum polymorphisms of complete graphs $K_n$ have a very special structure, wherein the noncommutative behaviour only comes from the quantum permutation group $S_n^+$. Combining this with techniques from combinatorial group theory, we construct separations between commutativity-gadget classes: we exhibit a relational structure admitting a finite-dimensional commutativity gadget but no quantum approximate gadget, and, conditional on the existence of a non-hyperlinear group, a structure admitting a quantum approximate commutativity gadget but no commuting-operator gadget.
one_liner: Develops a general characterization of commutativity gadgets via quantum polymorphisms across finite-dimensional, approximate, and commuting-operator models.
why_it_matters: It clarifies which reductions between nonlocal games remain quantum-sound, strengthening the formal toolkit behind quantum information complexity and verification protocols.
detailed_summary: The work gives a unified framework for commutativity gadgets using quantum homomorphism spaces of finite relational structures. It proves that gadget existence is equivalent to collapse of the associated quantum polymorphism space to a classical one at arity |A|^2, and characterizes robust gadgets through stable commutativity in weighted polymorphism algebras. The authors also derive separations between finite-dimensional, quantum-approximate, and commuting-operator gadget classes, including examples based on complete-graph polymorphisms and group-theoretic constructions.
main_result: It provides necessary-and-sufficient polymorphism-based criteria for both robust and non-robust commutativity gadgets across major quantum correlation models.
application_angle: Although theoretical, the framework can inform protocol design and complexity reductions used to certify or analyze quantum-network and nonlocal-computation behavior.

title: Revealing Strain and Disorder in Transition-Metal Dichalcogenides Using Hyperspectral Photoluminescence Imaging
abstract: Hyperspectral photoluminescence (HSPL) imaging provides spatially resolved spectral information for monolayer transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), enabling the detection of subtle variations in excitonic features that are not accessible with conventional optical or photoluminescence intensity imaging. We employ HSPL to map the microscopic spatial distribution of strain and disorder in hBN-encapsulated MoSe$_2$ and WSe$_2$ samples. Quantitative extraction of exciton, trion, and biexciton energies and linewidths reveals strain gradients and localized deformations, such as wrinkles and ripples. The technique allows for characterization of regions with uniform optical properties and identification of areas affected by micro-scale disorder, which may be missed by optical microscopy. Measurements on samples with different device architectures and fabrication processes demonstrate the general utility of hyperspectral PL imaging for assessing spatial heterogeneity and optoelectronic quality in two-dimensional materials.
one_liner: Uses hyperspectral photoluminescence imaging to map strain, disorder, and excitonic inhomogeneity in encapsulated monolayer MoSe2 and WSe2.
why_it_matters: Spatially resolving optical quality at the microscale is essential for building reliable 2D-material photonic and quantum-optoelectronic devices.
detailed_summary: The study applies hyperspectral photoluminescence imaging to hBN-encapsulated MoSe2 and WSe2 and extracts spatial maps of exciton, trion, and biexciton peak energies and linewidths. These maps reveal strain gradients, localized wrinkles, ripples, and microscale disorder not visible in standard optical microscopy or intensity-only PL images. By comparing device architectures and fabrication routes, the paper shows HSPL can identify uniform high-quality regions and diagnose process-induced heterogeneity.
main_result: It demonstrates quantitative HSPL mapping of excitonic energies and linewidths as a sensitive probe of strain and disorder in encapsulated TMD devices.
application_angle: The method is directly useful for screening, process control, and failure analysis in 2D-material emitters, photodetectors, and nanophotonic quantum-device stacks.

title: Twisted Fiber Bundle Codes over Group Algebras
abstract: We introduce a twisted fiber-bundle construction of quantum CSS codes over group algebras \(R=\mathbb F_2[G]\), where each base generator carries a generator-dependent \(R\)-linear fiber twist satisfying a flatness condition. This construction extends the untwisted lifted product code, recovered when all twists are identities. We show that invertible twists (satisfying a flatness condition) give a complex chain-isomorphic to the untwisted one, so the resulting binary CSS codes have the same blocklength \(n\) and encoded dimension \(k\). In contrast, singular chain-compatible twists can lower boundary ranks and increase the number of logical qubits. Examples over \(R=\mathbb F_2[D_3]\) show that the twisted fiber bundle code can outperform the corresponding untwisted lifted-product code in \(k\) while keeping the same \(n\) and, in our examples, the same minimum distance \(d\).
one_liner: Introduces twisted fiber-bundle CSS codes over group algebras that can raise encoded qubit count without increasing blocklength.
why_it_matters: Better code rate at fixed n can improve fault-tolerant hardware efficiency if distance is preserved, making these constructions relevant for practical code design.
detailed_summary: The paper generalizes lifted-product codes by assigning generator-dependent R-linear twists over the group algebra R=F2[G], constrained by a flatness condition. For invertible twists the resulting chain complex is isomorphic to the untwisted case, preserving blocklength and encoded dimension, while singular compatible twists can reduce boundary ranks and increase logical-qubit count. Concrete examples over F2[D3] show improved k relative to the untwisted lifted-product code at the same n and, in the presented cases, the same minimum distance d.
platform_or_system: quantum CSS code construction over group algebras
main_result: It shows twisted fiber-bundle constructions can outperform corresponding untwisted lifted-product codes in encoded dimension without enlarging blocklength.
application_angle: These code families could improve storage efficiency and architectural tradeoffs in future fault-tolerant quantum processors implementing LDPC-like codes.

title: Low frequency electric field sensing with a Rydberg beam
abstract: We present a method for performing low frequency electric field sensing via ionization detection of Rydberg atoms in a collimated atomic beam. A collimated beam avoids much of the electric field screening effects that are common in warm vapor cells due to the accumulation of alkali-metal atoms on glass surfaces. Further, a beam facilitates a spatially separated region for high signal-to-noise readout via ionization detection. Using this approach, we measure DC Stark shifts from external fields with frequencies as low as 1 Hz. The sensor demonstrates a sensitivity of better than 1 mV/m$\sqrt{\rm {Hz}}$ for frequencies above 20 Hz and $0.14(4)$ mV/m$\sqrt{\rm {Hz}}$ above 500 Hz with a linear dynamic range of over 50 dB.
one_liner: A collimated Rydberg atomic beam enables low-frequency electric-field sensing down to 1 Hz with sub-mV/m/sqrt(Hz) sensitivity.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical route to precise LF/DC electrometry without the surface-screening limits of warm vapor cells, useful for quantum-device diagnostics.
detailed_summary: The work uses a collimated beam of Rydberg atoms with spatially separated ionization detection to sense low-frequency electric fields while avoiding field screening from alkali buildup on vapor-cell walls. The authors resolve DC Stark shifts from fields as low as 1 Hz and report better than 1 mV/m/sqrt(Hz) sensitivity above 20 Hz, improving to 0.14(4) mV/m/sqrt(Hz) above 500 Hz. The sensor also maintains a linear dynamic range exceeding 50 dB, indicating utility across weak and moderate field regimes.
main_result: The sensor measures electric fields down to 1 Hz and achieves 0.14(4) mV/m/sqrt(Hz) sensitivity above 500 Hz with over 50 dB linear dynamic range.
application_angle: This platform is relevant for low-frequency electrometry near quantum hardware, RF systems, and environments where vapor-cell screening would limit accuracy.

title: Universal computational thermal imaging overcoming the ghosting effect
abstract: Thermal imaging is crucial for night vision but fundamentally hampered by the ghosting effect, a loss of detailed texture in cluttered photon streams. While conventional ghosting mitigation has relied on data post-processing, the recent breakthrough in heat-assisted detection and ranging (HADAR) opens a promising frontier for hyperspectral computational thermal imaging that produces night vision with day-like visibility. However, universal anti-ghosting imaging remains elusive, as state-of-the-art HADAR applies only to limited scenes with uniform materials, whereas material non-uniformity is ubiquitous in the real world. Here, we propose a universal computational thermal imaging framework, TAG (thermal anti-ghosting), to address material non-uniformity and overcome ghosting for high-fidelity night vision. TAG takes hyperspectral photon streams for nonparametric texture recovery, enabling our experimental demonstration of unprecedented expression recovery in thus-far-elusive ghostly human faces -- the archetypal, long-recognized ghosting phenomenon. Strikingly, TAG not only universally outperforms HADAR across various scenes, but also reveals the influence of material non-uniformity, shedding light on HADAR's effectiveness boundary. We extensively test facial texture and expression recovery across day and night, and demonstrate, for the first time, thermal 3D topological alignment and mood detection. This work establishes a universal foundation for high-fidelity computational night vision, with potential applications in autonomous navigation, reconnaissance, healthcare, and wildlife monitoring.
one_liner: TAG uses hyperspectral computational thermal imaging to recover texture and suppress ghosting across materially non-uniform night scenes.
why_it_matters: It advances photon-limited thermal imaging toward robust, real-world sensing tasks such as navigation, surveillance, and health monitoring.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces TAG, a universal thermal anti-ghosting framework that operates on hyperspectral photon streams to recover texture in scenes with material non-uniformity, where prior HADAR approaches degrade. Experimentally, it demonstrates improved recovery of facial texture and expressions in difficult night-vision scenarios and consistently outperforms HADAR across varied scenes. The system also supports thermal 3D topological alignment and mood detection, indicating that the recovered information is useful for downstream inference rather than only visualization.
main_result: TAG experimentally achieves high-fidelity anti-ghosting thermal imaging in heterogeneous scenes, including facial texture and expression recovery beyond prior HADAR limits.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to practical low-light sensing for autonomous vehicles, reconnaissance, healthcare screening, and wildlife observation.

title: Distribution of Bell State Entanglement in Qubit Networks via Collision Models
abstract: We propose a general scheme to controllably distribute pairwise entanglement in a quantum network of qubits by exploiting environmental ancilla qubits interacting with the network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians. Our approach leverages collision models, in which a quantum syatem interacts sequentially with ancilla units. We explore two distinct scenarios within this framework: one in which the ancilla is reset to its initial coherent state after each interaction (the traditional collision model), and another where the ancilla is not reset but its state is simply carried over to the next interaction, which we dub the repeated interaction model. In both scenarios, we ensure that the system-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. We also demonstrate how varying the ancilla-system interaction patterns enables selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes that do not directly interact. The scheme is analyzed in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, revealing the genaration of maximally entangled bell pairs even in configurations where the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Our results provide a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution, offering potential applications in quantum communication, metrology and modular quantum computing.
one_liner: Collision and repeated-interaction models can selectively generate Bell-pair entanglement across small qubit networks, even between non-neighboring nodes.
why_it_matters: It provides a physically implementable mechanism for on-demand entanglement routing in modular quantum networks and communication architectures.
detailed_summary: The authors study ancilla-mediated entanglement distribution in qubit networks using sequential system-ancilla interactions with tunable Hamiltonians. They compare standard collision models, where the ancilla is reset after each interaction, with repeated-interaction dynamics, where the ancilla state is carried forward, while discarding system-ancilla correlations between steps. In networks of up to three qubits, both settings enable selective entanglement generation between chosen pairs, including non-neighboring qubits and cases where the ancilla directly couples to only one node.
main_result: The scheme can generate maximally entangled Bell pairs between selected qubit pairs under controllable sequential ancilla interactions.
application_angle: This offers a scalable control primitive for entanglement distribution in quantum communication links, modular processors, and networked sensing nodes.

title: Compressive hyperspectral phasor imaging with single-pixel detection for spectral tasks
abstract: Spectral vision task plays a pivotal role in extracting discriminative spectral-spatial features from high-dimensional data, enabling fine-grained identification beyond human vision. Traditional methods usually involve first collecting rich spectral-spatial information and then using complex algorithms to digitally process it into scene classification and recognition. However, the complexity of processing massive three-dimensional (3D) hyperspectral datasets poses challenges for algorithms. Here, we demonstrate a compressive Hyperspectral Phasor Imaging with Single-pixel detection (HyPIS) that leverages highly compressed spatial-spectral data to achieve spectral task. Two optical encoders are used for wavelength-dependent sine- and cosine-encoding that transforms spectral signals into a two-dimensional (2D) phasor plot. By applying spatial-temporal illumination patterns, a single-pixel detector is enough to reconstruct the phasor image of the object. This allows to directly generate pixel-wise spectral task, bypassing 3D hyperspectral data. Our experiments show that HyPIS can perform real-time classification and recognition tasks of different scenes, reducing the required amount of data by two orders of magnitude, and it can still accurately classify under low light and uneven lighting conditions. This work develops a completely new spectral technology that enables spectral tasks to be performed without obtaining high-resolution hyperspectral datasets, holding promise for spectral applications in mobile devices, robotics, and satellite technologies.
one_liner: HyPIS performs hyperspectral classification and recognition with single-pixel detection by mapping spectra directly into a phasor representation.
why_it_matters: It cuts hyperspectral data demands by about two orders of magnitude, making spectral sensing more viable in constrained hardware platforms.
detailed_summary: HyPIS replaces full 3D hyperspectral acquisition with wavelength-dependent sine and cosine encoders that project spectra into a 2D phasor space. Spatial-temporal illumination patterns allow a single-pixel detector to reconstruct phasor images and perform pixel-wise spectral tasks directly, bypassing heavy hyperspectral datacube processing. Experiments show real-time classification and recognition with roughly 100x less data while retaining accuracy under low-light and uneven illumination conditions.
main_result: The system enables direct spectral task execution from compressed measurements, reducing data volume by two orders of magnitude while maintaining robust classification performance.
application_angle: The reduced detector and data-processing burden is attractive for mobile spectral cameras, robotic vision, and remote or satellite sensing.

title: Topology-Hiding Path Validation for Large-Scale Quantum Key Distribution Networks
abstract: Secure long-distance communication in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks depends on trusted repeater nodes along the entire transmission path. Consequently, these nodes will be subject to strict auditing and certification in future large-scale QKD deployments. However, trust must also extend to the network operator, who is responsible for fulfilling contractual obligations -- such as ensuring certified devices are used and transmission paths remain disjoint where required. In this work, we present a path validation protocol specifically designed for QKD networks. It enables the receiver to verify compliance with agreed-upon policies. At the same time, the protocol preserves the operator's confidentiality by ensuring that no sensitive information about the network topology is revealed to users. We provide a formal model and a provably secure generic construction of the protocol, along with a concrete instantiation. For long-distance communication involving 100 nodes, the protocol has a computational cost of 1-2.5s depending on the machine, and a communication overhead of less than 70kB - demonstrating the efficiency of our approach.
one_liner: A topology-hiding protocol lets QKD users verify path-policy compliance without exposing the operator’s confidential network layout.
why_it_matters: It addresses a real deployment bottleneck in large QKD networks: proving trusted-path compliance while preserving operational secrecy.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a formally modeled and provably secure path-validation protocol tailored to trusted-repeater QKD networks. It allows a receiver to verify that contractual routing policies are met, such as use of certified devices or path disjointness, without revealing sensitive topology details. A concrete instantiation is benchmarked for 100-node long-distance settings, showing 1-2.5 s computation time and communication overhead below 70 kB.
main_result: The protocol efficiently validates routing-policy compliance in large QKD networks while hiding the underlying topology from users.
application_angle: This is directly useful for audited commercial or national-scale QKD deployments where compliance guarantees and operator confidentiality must coexist.

title: Transversal non-Clifford gates on almost-good quantum LDPC and quantum locally testable codes
abstract: We exhibit nontrivial transversal logical multi-controlled-$Z$ gates on $[\![N,Θ(N),\tildeΘ(N)]\!]$ quantum low-density parity-check codes and $[\![N,Θ(N),\tildeΘ(N)]\!]$ quantum locally testable codes with soundness $\tildeΘ(1)$, combining nearly optimal code parameters with fault-tolerant non-Clifford gates for the first time. Remarkably, our proofs are almost entirely algebraic-topological, showing that such presumably intricate logical gates naturally arise as a fundamental topological phenomenon. We develop a general framework for constructing a rich new family of homological invariant forms which we call ''cupcap gates'' that induce transversal logical multi-controlled-$Z$ and, building on insights from [Li et al., arXiv:2603.25831], covering space methods to certify their nontriviality. The claimed almost-good code results follow immediately as examples.
one_liner: The paper constructs almost-good quantum LDPC and locally testable codes with transversal logical multi-controlled-Z non-Clifford gates.
why_it_matters: Combining strong code parameters with transversal non-Clifford operations is a major step toward lower-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The authors show that [N,Theta(N),tildeTheta(N)] quantum LDPC codes and quantum locally testable codes with tildeTheta(1) soundness can support nontrivial transversal logical multi-controlled-Z gates. Their construction is based on an algebraic-topological framework of homological invariant forms, termed cupcap gates, together with covering-space arguments that certify nontrivial logical action. The result is notable because it joins nearly optimal code families with fault-tolerant transversal non-Clifford gates in a single framework.
platform_or_system: quantum LDPC and qLTC code families
main_result: The work establishes explicit almost-good quantum codes that admit transversal logical multi-controlled-Z gates while retaining strong asymptotic parameters.
application_angle: These constructions could reduce fault-tolerance overhead in scalable quantum processors by bringing useful non-Clifford gates into high-rate, high-distance code families.

title: Topology-Hiding Connectivity-Assurance for QKD Inter-Networking
abstract: While QKD ensures information-theoretic security at the link level, real-world deployments depend on trusted repeaters, creating potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we thus introduce a topology-hiding connectivity assurance protocol to enhance trust in quantum key distribution (QKD) network infrastructures. Our protocol allows network providers to jointly prove the existence of a secure connection between endpoints without revealing internal topology details. By extending graph-signature techniques to support multi-graphs and hidden endpoints, we enable zero-knowledge proofs of connectivity that ensure both soundness and topology hiding. We further discuss how our approach can certify, e.g., multiple disjoint paths, supporting multi-path QKD scenarios. This work bridges cryptographic assurance methods with the operational requirements of QKD networks, promoting verifiable and privacy-preserving inter-network connectivity.
one_liner: A zero-knowledge protocol lets QKD providers prove endpoint connectivity without exposing internal network topology.
why_it_matters: It addresses a real deployment trust gap in repeater-based QKD by making inter-network path assurance verifiable yet privacy-preserving.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a topology-hiding connectivity-assurance protocol for QKD inter-networking, where providers jointly prove that two endpoints are securely connected without disclosing intermediate topology. It extends graph-signature methods to multigraphs and hidden endpoints, enabling sound zero-knowledge proofs over realistic network structures. The framework also supports certification of multiple disjoint paths, which is relevant for multipath QKD resilience and assurance.
main_result: It demonstrates a cryptographic protocol for proving secure endpoint connectivity and even disjoint-path existence in QKD networks while keeping topology hidden.
application_angle: This can support auditable QKD service-level guarantees between operators without forcing them to reveal sensitive infrastructure details.

title: Quantum Networking Fundamentals: From Physical Protocols to Network Engineering
abstract: The realization of the Quantum Internet promises transformative capabilities in secure communication, distributed quantum computing, and high-precision metrology. However, transitioning from laboratory experiments to a scalable, multi-tenant network utility introduces deep orchestration challenges. Current development is often siloed within physics communities, prioritizing hardware, while the classical networking community lacks architectural models to manage fragile quantum resources. This tutorial bridges this divide by providing a network-centric view of quantum networking. We dismantle idealized assumptions in current simulators to address the "simulation-reality gap," recasting them as explicit control-plane constraints. To bridge this gap, we establish Software-Defined Quantum Networking (SDQN) as a prerequisite for scale, prioritizing a symbiotic, dual-plane architecture where classical control dictates quantum data flow. Specifically, we synthesize reference models for SDQN and the Quantum Network Operating System (QNOS) for hardware abstraction, and adapt a Quantum Network Utility Maximization (Q-NUM) framework as a unifying mathematical lens for engineers to reason about trade-offs between entanglement routing, scheduling, and fidelity. Furthermore, we analyze Distributed Quantum AI (DQAI) over imperfect networks as a case study, illustrating how physical constraints such as probabilistic stragglers and decoherence dictate application-layer viability. Ultimately, this tutorial equips network engineers with the tools required to transition quantum networking from a bespoke physics experiment into a programmable, multi-tenant global infrastructure.
one_liner: This tutorial reframes quantum networking as an engineering stack built around control-plane constraints, abstraction layers, and utility optimization.
why_it_matters: It gives network engineers concrete models for scaling quantum links, routing, and applications beyond idealized physics-only simulators.
detailed_summary: The article synthesizes a network-centric architecture for quantum networking, emphasizing a dual-plane design in which classical control orchestrates fragile quantum resources. It formalizes Software-Defined Quantum Networking and a Quantum Network Operating System as abstraction layers bridging hardware heterogeneity and application requirements. A Quantum Network Utility Maximization framework is then proposed to reason about routing, scheduling, fidelity, and resource allocation under nonideal conditions, with distributed quantum AI used as a case study for application-level constraints.
main_result: It provides an integrated engineering framework linking physical quantum protocols to programmable control, abstraction, and optimization for scalable quantum networks.
application_angle: The work is useful as a design blueprint for deployable multi-tenant quantum communication and distributed sensing infrastructure.

title: Understanding Intrinsic Loss in Thin-Film Lithium Niobate Ring Resonators via Adiabatic Coupling
abstract: Thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) has emerged as a versatile integrated photonics platform, combining strong electro-optic and nonlinear effects. Among TFLN devices, ring resonators play a central role in filtering, modulation, and nonlinear optical processes. However, intrinsic loss, which ultimately limits ring performance, is most often summarized by single-valued metrics, and its statistical variability across resonances has received limited attention. Here, we show that intrinsic loss rates in monolithic TFLN ring resonators follow a statistical distribution, comprising a baseline loss and a tail arising from discrete loss events. This behavior is revealed by characterizing 2233 resonances, using an adiabatic waveguide-ring coupling architecture that selectively excites the fundamental mode and yields clean spectra in the ultra-high-Qi regime. We find the most probable intrinsic loss rate ki = 2 pi x 10.4 MHz, indicating operation in a low-loss regime comparable to state-of-the-art thick silicon nitride platforms.
one_liner: A large-scale resonance study shows intrinsic loss in TFLN rings is statistically distributed, with a low-loss baseline and rare discrete-loss tail.
why_it_matters: It gives a more realistic loss model for lithium-niobate resonators, which are central to modulators, filters, and nonlinear quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: Using an adiabatic waveguide-ring coupling geometry, the authors selectively excite the fundamental mode and obtain clean spectra from 2233 resonances in monolithic thin-film lithium niobate ring resonators. Rather than a single intrinsic-loss value, they find a statistical distribution composed of a baseline loss plus a tail associated with discrete loss events. The most probable intrinsic loss rate is reported as ki = 2 pi x 10.4 MHz, placing the devices in a low-loss regime comparable to leading thick silicon nitride platforms.
main_result: It establishes, from 2233 measured resonances, that intrinsic loss in monolithic TFLN rings follows a distributed model and reaches a most probable rate of 2 pi x 10.4 MHz.
application_angle: These results help engineers set realistic yield and performance expectations for scalable TFLN photonic circuits used in modulation and quantum nonlinear optics.

title: Compact system development of efficient quantum-entangled photon sources towards deployable and industrial devices
abstract: Entangled photon pair sources are a key enabling technology for quantum communication and networking, yet their deployment beyond laboratory environments is hindered by system-level complexity, limited operational stability, and insufficient industry compatibility. Here, we demonstrate a rack-based, mobile quantum light source architecture based on a semiconductor quantum dot emitter that directly addresses these challenges through modular system integration and automated operation. The source generates polarization-entangled photon pairs with an entanglement negativity 2n of up to $0.98(1)$, confirming near-maximal entanglement quality. In continuous, hands-off operation over a six-hour time window, the system achieves an average single-photon emission rate of $697(8)$ kHz and a maximum rate of $740(7)$ kHz, while maintaining 2n-value of more than $95$ $\%$. These results are enabled by the integration of optical excitation, collection, cryogenic operation, and control electronics within a standardized rack footprint, together with automated monitoring. By demonstrating simultaneously high entanglement quality, sustained brightness, and long-term operational stability in an industry-aligned system architecture, this work advances semiconductor quantum dot sources toward deployable entangled photon sources for applied quantum photonics.
one_liner: A rack-mounted quantum-dot source delivers near-maximally entangled photon pairs with automated, stable operation over hours.
why_it_matters: It moves entangled-photon hardware from lab setups toward industrially deployable systems with strong brightness, fidelity, and uptime.
detailed_summary: The work integrates optical excitation, collection optics, cryogenics, and control electronics into a modular rack-based architecture built around a semiconductor quantum-dot emitter. The source achieves polarization-entangled photon pairs with entanglement negativity up to 0.98(1), indicating near-maximal entanglement quality. In automated hands-off operation over six hours, it sustains an average single-photon rate of 697(8) kHz, peaks at 740(7) kHz, and keeps the 2n metric above 95 percent.
main_result: It demonstrates a mobile, standardized rack system that combines near-unit entanglement quality with roughly 700 kHz brightness and multi-hour autonomous stability.
application_angle: The architecture is directly relevant for fieldable quantum communication, networking, and measurement systems that need industrial packaging and unattended operation.

title: High-threshold decoding of non-Pauli codes for 2D universality
authors: Julio C. Magdalena de la Fuente
abstract: Topological codes have many desirable properties that allow fault-tolerant quantum computation with relatively low overhead. A core challenge for these codes, however, is to achieve a low-overhead universal gate set with limited connectivity. In this work, we explore a non-Pauli stabilizer code that can be used to complete a universal gate set on topological toric and surface codes in strictly two dimensions. Fault-tolerant syndrome extraction for the non-Pauli code requires mid-circuit $X$ corrections, a key difference to conventional Pauli codes. We construct and benchmark a just-in-time (JIT) matching decoder to reliably decide these corrections. Under a phenomenological error model with equally likely physical and measurement errors, we find a high threshold of $\approx 2.5\,\%$, close to the $\approx 2.9\,\%$ of a decoder with access to the full syndrome history. We also perform a finite-size scaling analysis to estimate how the logical error rate scales below threshold and verify an exponential suppression in both physical error rate and in the system size. A second global decoding step for $Z$ errors is required and the non-Clifford gates in the circuit reduce the threshold from $\approx 2.9\,\%$ to $\approx 1.8\,\%$ with a naive decoder. We show how $Z$ decoding can be improved using knowledge of the $X$ corrections, pushing the threshold to $\approx 2.2\,\%$. Our results suggest non-Clifford logic in 2D codes could perform comparably to 2D quantum memory. Our formalism for efficient benchmarking and decoding directly generalizes to a broader family of CSS codes whose $X$ stabilizers are twisted by diagonal Clifford operators, and spacetime versions thereof, defined by CSS-like circuits enriched by $CCZ$, $CS$, and $T$ gates.
one_liner: A just-in-time matching decoder enables high-threshold operation of non-Pauli topological codes for 2D universal fault tolerance.
why_it_matters: It strengthens the case that universal non-Clifford logic may be achievable in strictly 2D layouts without severe threshold loss.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a non-Pauli stabilizer code that augments toric and surface codes with a route to universal gates in two dimensions, but requires mid-circuit X corrections during syndrome extraction. To handle this, the authors design a just-in-time matching decoder and benchmark it under a phenomenological noise model with equal data and measurement error rates. They find an approximately 2.5 percent threshold, close to the approximately 2.9 percent achievable with full syndrome history, and improve Z-error decoding from about 1.8 percent to about 2.2 percent by conditioning on inferred X corrections.
main_result: It shows that efficient decoding can preserve a high error threshold for non-Pauli 2D codes supporting universal gate sets.
application_angle: This reduces the practical barrier to implementing universal fault-tolerant quantum processors on planar hardware with limited connectivity.

title: A time grating approach to ultrahigh-Q guided mode resonance
abstract: Guided mode resonance (GMR), the resonant coupling of free-space light into leaky waveguide modes, is traditionally achieved with periodic patterned structures. However, this approach makes its key properties such as quality factor (Q-factor) fabrication-dependent and non-tunable. Here, we introduce a time grating platform, i.e., a homogeneous waveguide whose refractive index is modulated periodically in time, that allows tunable GMRs through temporal modulation engineering rather than spatial structural redesign. We show that the Q-factors of these GMRs diverge as the modulation depth vanishes. Furthermore, unconstrained by energy conservation, the resonances exhibit near-unity reflection for fundamental harmonics and values exceeding 40 for first-order harmonics. Our findings not only apply to yield a giant Goos-Hänchen shift over 103 times wavelength without sacrificing the reflection magnitude, but also open new avenues for related phenomena such as bound states in the continuum, unidirectional GMRs and beyond.
one_liner: Temporal refractive-index modulation in a homogeneous waveguide produces tunable ultrahigh-Q guided-mode resonances without patterned gratings.
why_it_matters: It replaces fixed nanofabricated resonance control with a dynamic knob, opening a new route to reconfigurable high-Q photonic devices.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a time-grating platform in which a uniform waveguide is modulated periodically in time rather than patterned in space to realize guided-mode resonances. They show theoretically that the resonance Q-factor diverges as the modulation depth approaches zero, enabling ultrahigh-Q operation through temporal control. The scheme yields near-unity reflection for fundamental harmonics and reflection exceeding 40 for first-order harmonics, and predicts giant Goos-Hanchen shifts larger than 10^3 wavelengths.
main_result: It demonstrates that time-periodic index modulation can generate tunable ultrahigh-Q guided-mode resonances with extreme reflection and beam-shift responses.
application_angle: This could enable actively tunable filters, reflectors, and photonic interfaces for sensing and communication without redesigning spatial nanostructures.

title: Photonic qubit encoding interconversion for heterogeneous quantum networking
abstract: Quantum information processing, communication, and sensing networks are being developed with various qubit platforms that use different encoding schemes. Connecting quantum network nodes to distribute entanglement requires matching photon qubit basis encoding. In this work, we implement an interconversion protocol which converts photon qubit encoding from the polarization basis to the time-bin basis, transmits the photons through a transport fiber with large fluctuations in polarization, and converts back to polarization encoding for ease of measurement. This interconversion scheme faithfully transmits a polarization Bell state across the transport fiber by converting sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate. These results illustrate a practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms to enable modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks.
one_liner: A polarization-to-time-bin converter preserves Bell-state transmission through polarization-unstable fiber for heterogeneous quantum links.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical interoperability bottleneck in quantum networks by bridging incompatible photonic qubit encodings while tolerating real fiber polarization drift.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates a photonic interconversion protocol that maps qubits from polarization encoding to time-bin encoding before fiber transport, then converts them back to polarization for measurement. This architecture exploits the robustness of time-bin qubits against polarization fluctuations in deployed fiber while retaining convenient polarization-based analysis at the endpoints. The authors show faithful transmission of a polarization Bell state, with channel-induced polarization instability appearing primarily as transmission loss rather than state-infidelity. The result is a concrete interface layer for connecting dissimilar quantum nodes in modular networks.
main_result: The authors experimentally transmit a polarization Bell state across polarization-fluctuating fiber by converting to time-bin encoding in flight and back to polarization at the receiver.
application_angle: This provides a practical middleware for heterogeneous quantum networking, where different memories, processors, and links prefer different photonic encodings.

title: Q2NS Demo: A Quantum Network Simulator Based on ns-3
abstract: Q2NS is an open-source quantum network simulator built on ns-3, the de facto standard for classical network simulation. By inheriting ns-3's mature classical stack and event-driven execution model, Q2NS enables faithful co-simulation of quantum-network dynamics and classical signaling, a core requirement for the functioning of any quantum network. Its modular architecture is designed for extensibility, with pluggable quantum-state backends (state-vector, density matrix, stabilizer) and a clean separation between network control and node-level operations. Q2NS comes with a quantum network visualizer Q2NSViz, supporting interactive inspection of both physical- and entanglement-induced connectivity graphs, helping users interpret protocol behavior and entanglement manipulation processes. We present a demonstration of Q2NS, highlighting its ability to capture and simulate the coexistence of quantum and classical communication. The proposed demonstration presents quantum communication scenarios of increasing complexity: from entanglement distribution basics to multipartite graph-state manipulation, complemented by pre-loaded examples in Q2NSViz that require no prior quantum communication or coding experience.
one_liner: Q2NS brings quantum-network simulation into ns-3, enabling co-simulation of entanglement dynamics with classical control traffic.
why_it_matters: A usable ns-3-based simulator lowers the barrier to realistic quantum-network design, where protocol performance depends on tight quantum-classical interaction.
detailed_summary: Q2NS is an open-source simulator built atop ns-3, reusing its event-driven execution model and mature classical networking stack to model hybrid quantum-classical protocols. The framework separates network control logic from node-level quantum operations and supports multiple quantum-state backends, including state-vector, density-matrix, and stabilizer formalisms. It also includes Q2NSViz for interactive views of physical connectivity and entanglement graphs, helping users inspect protocol evolution. The demo scenarios span basic entanglement distribution to multipartite graph-state manipulation, targeting both research and educational use.
main_result: The work delivers a modular ns-3-based simulator and visualizer that model quantum communication protocols together with their required classical signaling.
application_angle: It is directly useful for prototyping, benchmarking, and teaching quantum-network protocols before committing to hardware deployments.

title: The Phase Quantum Walk: A Unified Framework for Graph State Distribution in Quantum Networks
abstract: Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is a central challenge for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication. I introduce the phase quantum walk (PQW), a discrete-time quantum walk in which the conventional position-permuting shift operator is replaced by a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states, not merely GHZ states, from elementary two-qubit resources. The Byproduct Lemma shows that each walk step teleports edge entanglement with a correctable Pauli byproduct; the Coin Invariance Theorem proves that the optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions F_dep = (1 - 3p/4)^k and F_pd = ((1 + sqrt(1 - p))/2)^k. Analytical correction formulas are derived for tree graphs (general theorem) and ring graphs (C4 case study), with F = 1.0 verified across eight topologies (up to 4096 outcomes). Hardware validation on ibm marrakesh (IBM Heron r2, CZ-native) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4> and 0.922 for |L4>, statistically identical, providing the first experimental confirmation that fidelity is independent of graph topology as predicted by the Coin Invariance Theorem.
one_liner: A phase-quantum-walk protocol distributes arbitrary graph states with topology-independent fidelity, backed by IBM hardware results.
why_it_matters: Graph-state distribution is central to modular quantum computing and networked MBQC, and this framework gives both analytic guarantees and hardware validation.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces the phase quantum walk, a discrete-time walk where the usual shift is replaced by conditional CZ phases, allowing arbitrary graph-state distribution from two-qubit resources. A Byproduct Lemma and Coin Invariance Theorem show that each step teleports edge entanglement up to correctable Pauli byproducts and that optimal fidelity is independent of the unitary coin choice, with closed-form expressions under depolarizing and phase-damping noise. The author derives explicit correction formulas for tree and ring graphs and verifies perfect corrected fidelity across eight topologies in simulation. Experimental runs on IBM marrakesh report corrected fidelities of 0.924 for GHZ4 and 0.922 for a 4-node linear cluster, supporting topology-independent performance.
main_result: The work establishes and experimentally validates a protocol for distributing arbitrary graph states whose corrected fidelity is predicted, and observed, to be independent of graph topology.
application_angle: This could simplify graph-state delivery for modular quantum processors and quantum-network services that rely on cluster or graph-state resources.

title: Shot-to-shot noise cancellation for parametric oscillators
abstract: Powerful approaches to squeeze the motional state of a harmonic oscillator rely on the stepwise modulation of its resonance frequency. Such protocols can be limited by forces that vary slowly between experimental runs but are constant during a single experimental shot. Such shot-to-shot noise gives rise to a spread in experimental outcomes that masks the uncertainty intrinsic to quantum theory. Taking inspiration from spin-echo protocols, we propose a decoupling technique that, under ideal conditions, perfectly cancels shot-to-shot force noise in squeezing experiments based on parametric modulation. We implement the protocol using an optically levitated nanoparticle, where shot-to-shot force noise arises from slowly varying stray fields acting on the charge carried by the particle. Using our oscillator-echo protocol, we demonstrate shot-to-shot noise suppression to the measurement-backaction limit.
one_liner: An oscillator-echo protocol cancels slow shot-to-shot force noise in parametric squeezing of a levitated nanoparticle.
why_it_matters: Suppressing technical noise down to the measurement-backaction limit is essential for using mechanical photonic platforms in precision quantum sensing and state engineering.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze and implement an echo-like decoupling method for harmonic-oscillator squeezing protocols based on stepwise modulation of the resonance frequency. The target noise is shot-to-shot force drift that stays constant within a run but varies between runs, which otherwise broadens the measured distribution and obscures intrinsic quantum uncertainty. In an optically levitated nanoparticle, the dominant source is slowly varying stray electric fields acting on residual charge. The demonstrated oscillator-echo suppresses this technical spread to the measurement-backaction limit, isolating the quantum-limited response.
main_result: The experiment shows that an echo-style parametric-modulation sequence can remove run-to-run force noise and recover squeezing measurements limited by measurement backaction.
application_angle: The technique improves robustness of levitated-optomechanical experiments for quantum sensing, force metrology, and nonclassical motional-state preparation.

title: Theory of Lineshapes in Optical-Optical Double Resonance Spectroscopy
abstract: This paper presents lineshapes for molecular Optical-Optical Double Resonance (DR) Spectroscopy with arbitrary strength for both pump and probe field using the steady-state solutions for the 3-level density matrix. When the Doppler broadening can be neglected, the results are analytical, and the probe spectrum is a pair of Lorentzian lines that display Autler-Townes splitting, and each has an angular frequency half-width half maximum equal to the relaxation rates, which are all assumed equal. When Doppler broadening is introduced, one must resort to numerical integration except for the limit of weak pump and probe fields. When the Doppler width is assumed much larger than the pump and probe Rabi Frequencies, the calculated DR lineshapes are found to be Lorentzian with a strong pump field limit that is proportional to the pump Rabi frequency, what is commonly known as power broadening. However, the width does not equal the Rabi frequency and is different for co- and counter-propagating pump and probe fields. Furthermore, that broadening is largely inhomogeneous, despite the Lorentzian shape. The saturation power is found to be about 4 times higher than for the bare probe transition with the same relaxation rate, dramatically lower than that expected if the width is interpreted as homogeneous.
one_liner: A density-matrix treatment derives optical-optical double-resonance lineshapes from Autler-Townes splitting to Doppler-dominated power broadening.
why_it_matters: Accurate DR lineshape theory is important for extracting molecular parameters and designing spectroscopy systems without misidentifying inhomogeneous broadening as homogeneous.
detailed_summary: The paper derives steady-state three-level density-matrix lineshapes for optical-optical double-resonance spectroscopy with arbitrary pump and probe strengths. In the Doppler-free regime, the probe response is analytic and consists of two Lorentzians showing Autler-Townes splitting, with widths set by relaxation rates under the equal-rate assumption. With Doppler broadening, the authors use numerical integration except in the weak-field limit and show that when Doppler width dominates the Rabi frequencies, the observed Lorentzian broadening scales with pump strength but differs for co- and counter-propagating beams. They further show that this apparent power broadening is largely inhomogeneous and that the saturation power is about four times that of the bare probe transition.
main_result: The study provides analytical and numerical DR lineshapes that clarify how strong driving and Doppler effects reshape widths, splitting, and saturation behavior.
application_angle: This improves interpretation and design of high-resolution molecular spectroscopy setups used in sensing, diagnostics, and precision optical measurement.

title: Space-Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms with Resource Estimation
abstract: Solving the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is critical for evaluating the quantum security of widely deployed elliptic-curve cryptosystems. Consequently, minimizing the number of logical qubits required to execute this algorithm is a key object. In implementations of Shor's algorithm, the space complexity is largely dictated by the modular inversion operation during point addition. Starting from the extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA), we refine the register-sharing method of Proos and Zalka and propose a space-efficient reversible modular inversion algorithm. We use length registers together with location-controlled arithmetic to store the intermediate variables in a compact form throughout the computation. We then optimize the stepwise update rules and give concrete circuit constructions for the resulting controlled arithmetic components. This leads to a modular inversion circuit that uses $3n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ logical qubits and $204n^2\log_2 n + O(n^2)$ Toffoli gates. By inserting this modular inversion component into the controlled affine point-addition circuit, we obtain a space-efficient algorithm for the ECDLP with $5n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ qubits and $O(n^3)$ Toffoli gates. In particular, for a 256-bit prime-field curve, our estimate reduces the logical-qubit count to 1333, compared with 2124 in the previous low-width implementation of Häner et al.
one_liner: A low-width reversible modular inversion cuts logical qubits for quantum ECDLP, reaching 1333 qubits for a 256-bit curve.
why_it_matters: Reducing logical-qubit demand makes cryptographically relevant resource estimates more realistic and sharpens timelines for when ECC-based systems become vulnerable.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a space-efficient reversible modular inversion routine derived from the extended Euclidean algorithm, using length registers and location-controlled arithmetic to compactly encode intermediate values. The resulting inversion circuit uses 3n + 4 floor(log2 n) + O(1) logical qubits and 204n^2 log2 n + O(n^2) Toffoli gates. Integrating this block into controlled affine point addition yields an ECDLP algorithm using 5n + 4 floor(log2 n) + O(1) logical qubits with O(n^3) Toffoli complexity. For a 256-bit prime-field curve, the estimate drops to 1333 logical qubits, substantially below the 2124-qubit prior low-width baseline.
platform_or_system: fault-tolerant quantum circuit for elliptic-curve cryptanalysis
main_result: The authors present a lower-width ECDLP quantum algorithm that significantly reduces logical-qubit requirements while preserving asymptotically cubic gate complexity.
application_angle: These estimates are directly relevant to quantum hardware roadmaps and to assessing the practical security margin of deployed elliptic-curve cryptography.

title: Towards High-Brightness Perfect Photon Blockade
abstract: Single-photon sources with high single-photon purity and high brightness are key elements of many future quantum technologies. While photon blockade (PB) is widely exploited in the development of such sources, achieving the coexistence of high purity and high brightness remains a long-standing challenge. Here, we identify a novel mechanism for high-brightness PB and demonstrate that near-ideal purity and near-ideal brightness can be simultaneously achieved in an extended nondegenerate two-photon Jaynes-Cummings model with two-body and three-body interactions. This mechanism is underpinned by a distinctive energy-level structure arising from the combined action of the two interactions. The energy levels in the multi-excitation manifold essentially retain a harmonic ladder of degenerate doublets, whereas in the single-excitation subspace the doublet degeneracy is lifted, with a finite splitting between the two levels. Consequently, when one bosonic mode is driven by a coherent continuous-wave pump, the former degeneracy enables the other bosonic mode to exhibit near-perfect PB even in the strong driving regime, while the latter splitting allows the mean photon number of that mode to approach unity. Our proposed scheme overcomes the outstanding challenge and offers a promising pathway toward realizing ideal single-photon sources.
one_liner: A two-mode nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings scheme is shown to deliver near-unity photon blockade purity and brightness at the same time.
why_it_matters: It addresses the core single-photon-source tradeoff between antibunching and output rate, a major bottleneck for deployable photonic quantum systems.
detailed_summary: The paper studies an extended nondegenerate two-photon Jaynes-Cummings model with both two-body and three-body interactions. The combined nonlinearities produce an unusual spectrum: multi-excitation manifolds retain nearly harmonic degenerate doublets, while the single-excitation doublet is split. Under continuous-wave driving of one bosonic mode, this spectral structure lets the other mode show near-perfect photon blockade even at strong drive while keeping its mean photon number close to one. The mechanism is technically significant because it avoids the usual brightness collapse associated with conventional blockade schemes.
main_result: They identify a spectral-engineering mechanism that enables simultaneous near-ideal single-photon purity and near-ideal source brightness under strong driving.
application_angle: If realizable in cavity or circuit platforms, the scheme could improve deterministic single-photon generation for quantum photonics, metrology, and low-noise optical sensing.

title: Localized Entanglement Purification
abstract: Entanglement purification protocols are fundamental primitives in quantum communication, enabling the distillation of high-quality entanglement using only local operations and classical communication. For large multipartite states, however, existing purification schemes typically require substantial resources and become progressively inefficient as system size increases. We introduce a new type of multipartite entanglement purification, Localized Entanglement Purification (LEP), a family of protocols that purify entanglement at the level of network regions rather than globally. By exploiting spatial noise asymmetries, LEP reduces resource consumption and enables scalable purification strategies for larger quantum systems.
one_liner: Localized Entanglement Purification purifies multipartite entanglement region-by-region instead of globally to cut resource overhead.
why_it_matters: Scalable entanglement distillation is essential for realistic quantum networks, and regional purification could make large noisy graph states far more practical.
detailed_summary: The work introduces Localized Entanglement Purification, a family of protocols that targets entanglement quality within selected network regions rather than across the full multipartite state. The key idea is to exploit spatially nonuniform noise so purification effort is concentrated where fidelity loss is most severe. This reduces resource consumption relative to global purification strategies and is designed to scale better with network size. The proposal is especially relevant for distributed graph states and heterogeneous repeater-style architectures.
main_result: They propose a purification framework that leverages local noise asymmetries to distill multipartite entanglement with lower overhead than global schemes.
application_angle: Regional purification could reduce memory, gate, and classical-communication costs in quantum repeater networks and distributed quantum computing links.

title: Photonic convolutional neural network with pre-trained in-situ training
abstract: Photonic computing is a computing paradigm which have great potential to overcome the energy bottlenecks of electronic von Neumann architecture. Throughput and power consumption are fundamental limitations of Complementary-metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) chips, therefore convolutional neural network (CNN) is revolutionising machine learning, computer vision and other image based applications. In this work, we propose and validate a fully photonic convolutional neural network (PCNN) that performs MNIST image classification entirely in the optical domain, achieving 94 percent test accuracy. Unlike existing architectures that rely on frequent in-between conversions from optical to electrical and back to optical (O/E/O), our system maintains coherent processing utilizing Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI) meshes, wavelength-division multiplexed (WDM) pooling, and microring resonator-based nonlinearities. The max pooling unit is fully implemented on silicon photonics, which does not require opto-electrical or electrical conversions. To overcome the challenges of training physical phase shifter parameters, we introduce a hybrid training methodology deploying a mathematically exact differentiable digital twin for ex-situ backpropagation, followed by in-situ fine-tuning via Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation (SPSA) algorithm. Our evaluation demonstrates significant robustness to thermal crosstalk (only 0.43 percent accuracy degradation at severe coupling) and achieves 100 to 242 times better energy efficiency than state-of-the-art electronic GPUs for single-image inference.
one_liner: A fully optical CNN on silicon photonics classifies MNIST at 94% accuracy using coherent in-situ trainable photonic hardware.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical path to energy-efficient optical AI accelerators that avoid repeated O/E/O conversion and remain robust to hardware nonidealities.
detailed_summary: The authors implement a fully photonic convolutional neural network using MZI meshes for linear transforms, WDM-based pooling, and microring resonators for nonlinear activation. Training uses a differentiable digital twin for ex-situ backpropagation followed by in-situ SPSA fine-tuning of physical phase shifters. On MNIST, the system reaches 94% test accuracy and shows only 0.43% degradation under severe thermal crosstalk. They also report 100-242x better single-image inference energy efficiency than state-of-the-art electronic GPUs.
platform_or_system: Silicon photonic CNN with MZI meshes and microrings
main_result: They validate an end-to-end optical CNN with fully photonic pooling and in-situ trainability that achieves 94% MNIST accuracy with strong crosstalk robustness.
application_angle: The architecture is relevant to low-power edge inference and high-throughput vision processing where photonic hardware can outperform CMOS energy budgets.

title: Angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy of the DABCO molecule probed with VUV radiation
abstract: We report a study of the diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (DABCO) molecule photoionized using VUV synchrotron radiation in combination with an ion--electron coincidence spectrometer. We determine accurately the adiabatic ionization energy to $7.199\pm0.006$~eV. Two vibrational progressions of DABCO cation ground state are resolved at $847~\text{cm}^{-1}\pm27~\text{cm}^{-1}$ and $1257~\text{cm}^{-1}\pm67~\text{cm}^{-1}$, which we assign to modes of $e'$ symmetry. Analysis of the photoelectron angular distribution shows that the anisotropy parameter depends on the vibrational excitation. This dependence of the $β$ parameter with the vibrational excitation is attributed to the scattering of the outgoing wavefunction mediated by high-lying Rydberg states.
one_liner: VUV angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy of DABCO resolves ionization, vibrational structure, and excitation-dependent emission anisotropy.
why_it_matters: Precise molecular spectroscopic benchmarks are valuable for designing and modeling molecular-state platforms relevant to quantum control and hardware chemistry.
detailed_summary: Using synchrotron VUV radiation and ion-electron coincidence detection, the study measures the DABCO adiabatic ionization energy as 7.199 ± 0.006 eV. Two vibrational progressions of the cation ground state are resolved at 847 ± 27 cm^-1 and 1257 ± 67 cm^-1 and assigned to e' symmetry modes. The photoelectron angular distribution shows that the anisotropy parameter beta depends on vibrational excitation. The authors attribute this behavior to outgoing-electron scattering mediated by high-lying Rydberg states.
main_result: They obtain high-precision ionization and vibrational spectroscopy of DABCO and reveal vibrational-state-dependent photoelectron angular anisotropy.
application_angle: These molecular benchmarks support more accurate modeling of molecular electronic structure and state-selective control strategies relevant to emerging molecular quantum hardware.

title: Engineering Perfect State Transfer Graphs via Givens Transformations
abstract: Perfect quantum state transfer is achievable in different settings, including linear qubit chains, bi-dimensional arrays, ladders, etc. The most studied case contemplates transferring arbitrary one-qubit pure states in systems with homogeneous interactions. These restrictions allow finding numerous examples of systems that show perfect transfer but in geometries that are not implementable or are very difficult to implement in actual experimental settings. Relaxing the homogeneity of the interactions and inspired by the $XX$ qubit chains that show perfect transmission, we present a simple scheme based on the Givens Transformations to analyse and obtain a class of qubit graphs that possess perfect quantum state transmission. We present some simple examples and show how it is possible to generalize them for longer transmission lengths.
one_liner: A Givens-transformation construction generates experimentally friendlier qubit graphs that still support perfect quantum state transfer.
why_it_matters: Perfect state transfer is useful for short-range quantum interconnects, and this method expands the set of implementable coupling graphs beyond idealized chains.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a systematic method based on Givens transformations to analyze and synthesize qubit graphs with perfect quantum state transfer. By relaxing homogeneous-coupling constraints that dominate earlier constructions, the approach generates broader graph families while preserving exact transfer behavior inspired by XX chains. The authors provide explicit examples and show how the method generalizes to longer transmission distances. The construction is useful for turning algebraic PST conditions into network topologies that are closer to experimental feasibility.
platform_or_system: Engineered qubit coupling graphs for state transfer
main_result: They derive a simple graph-engineering procedure that produces inhomogeneous qubit networks capable of perfect state transfer over extended distances.
application_angle: The method can inform practical quantum interconnect and routing designs in photonic, spin, or superconducting architectures where geometry and coupling constraints matter.

title: RFOX (Rotated-Field Oscillatory eXchange) quantum algorithm: Towards Parameter-Free Quantum Optimizers
abstract: We introduce RFOX (Rotated-Field Oscillatory eXchange), a parameter-free quantum algorithm for combinatorial optimization. RFOX combines an almost constant non-stoquastic $XX$ catalyst with a weak harmonic $ZX$ counter-diabatic term. Using the Floquet-Magnus expansion, we derive a closed-form effective Hamiltonian whose first-order term retains the full $XX$ driver, while the leading correction consists of a single qubit $Y$ field at high drive frequency. This structure ensures that the instantaneous spectral gap remains essentially flat, independent of both the interpolation parameter and the disorder strength, modulated only by a $δ$ parameter. This behavior stands in stark contrast to the unpredictable gap reductions, or even collapses, exhibited by the $X$ (stoquastic), $XX$, and $X+sXX$ (non-stoquastic) driver schedules. Extensive noiseless simulations on random-field Ising model (RFIM) instances with 7, 9, and 12 qubits, across three magnetic-field ranges, validate these spectral predictions: RFOX attains near-optimal, and in some cases exact, ground states using up to an order of magnitude fewer Trotter slices. Its performance advantage grows with increasing disorder, as conventional methods slow down near vanishing gaps, whereas RFOX maintains a constant runtime scaling of $T \propto Δ_{\min}^{-2}$. Hardware experiments on IBM Quantum processors (Eagle r3 and Heron r1, with 12, 15, and 20 physical qubits) reproduce similar performance rankings. These results suggest that fixed-gap, non-stoquastic drivers augmented with analytically derived counter-diabatic terms offer a promising, scalable, and tuning-free route toward quantum optimizers for combinatorial optimization problems.
one_liner: RFOX is a parameter-free Floquet-inspired quantum optimization algorithm that keeps the spectral gap nearly flat and improves hardware performance.
why_it_matters: Flattening the optimization gap directly targets a central failure mode of adiabatic and QAOA-like approaches, improving robustness and reducing tuning burden.
detailed_summary: RFOX combines an almost constant non-stoquastic XX catalyst with a weak harmonic ZX counter-diabatic term, and a Floquet-Magnus analysis yields an effective Hamiltonian with the XX driver plus a leading single-qubit Y correction. This structure is designed to keep the instantaneous spectral gap essentially flat across the interpolation path and disorder realizations. Noiseless simulations on 7, 9, and 12 qubit random-field Ising instances show near-optimal and sometimes exact ground-state recovery with up to an order-of-magnitude fewer Trotter slices. Experiments on IBM Eagle r3 and Heron r1 devices using 12, 15, and 20 physical qubits reproduce similar performance ordering.
platform_or_system: IBM superconducting-qubit processors with Floquet-driven Ising optimization
main_result: They demonstrate a tuning-free non-stoquastic optimization protocol that maintains an almost constant gap and outperforms standard driver schedules in simulation and on IBM hardware.
application_angle: The approach is promising for near-term quantum optimizers because it reduces parameter search, tolerates disorder better, and maps onto existing superconducting-gate hardware.

title: From Liouville equation to universal quantum control: A study of generating ultra highly squeezed states
abstract: Within a unified framework, we reveal that the seemingly disparate control approaches for classical and quantum continuous-variable systems are interconnected via differential manifolds of the ancillary representations. For classical systems, the ancillary representation is defined by the time-dependent ancillary canonical variables resulting from a symplectic transformation over the original canonical variables. Under the conditions of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation, the ancillary canonical variables act as dynamical invariants to guide the system nonadiabatically through the entire phase space.
